text:
EDMUND. Greed, mostly. Why else brand his own mother a whore?
He calls me base-born—a cuckoo slipped into the good Lord's nest.
Whether I was struck in the holy mint of marriage, I can't say; that’s a question for my mother's conscience, not mine.
But as for the actual making of me—well! Bless the rogue that put his back into it.
Look at us, my lord. Stand us side by side.
If sickly old Lord Roland really sired us both, and this boy is the very map of his father...
Then on my knees, I thank the stars I am a bastard!
DUKE VINCENT. The heavens have sent us a madman.
DUCHESS CLARA. No, Vincent, look closer. He has the very cut of Count Valerius.
The eye, the insolent jaw.
Do you not see my old friend in the sheer, massive bulk of him?
DUKE VINCENT. The mold is absolute Valerius, true enough.
Boy, speak up. What brings you to dispute your brother’s land?
EDMUND. Dispute? I only point out his half-measure of a body!
He'd take my whole estate with half a chest. Ten thousand crowns a year for a brittle reed!
ARTHUR. My sovereign lord, hear me. When my father lived—and the Count Valerius was ever at his side—
EDMUND. Careful, little brother. That’s no way to win an estate.
Tell the Duke how close he kept our mother.
ARTHUR. My father was dispatched to the Scottish borders. To parley, to hold the line. He was gone for months.
And the Count? The Count took his ease at our manor.
I blush to speak of what happened there, but truth is truth. Miles of harsh country and freezing rivers lay between my father’s bed and my mother.
My father said as much himself, realizing how this... giant came to be.
On his deathbed, he struck him from the will. He swore this child was no son of his! And even if he was, he dropped into the world sixteen weeks before the natural term.
Give me my land, my liege. It was my father's dying wish.
DUKE VINCENT. Now, hold. Your brother was born within the bonds of wedlock.
If your mother played false, the sin is hers—a hazard all husbands risk when they take a wife.
Look at it legally, boy. If the great Count had demanded this youth from your father, could he have him? No.
Your father could have locked up this calf, born in his own barn, from the whole world.
The Count couldn't claim him. And your father, despite his suspicions, couldn't simply cast him out.
The law is the law. Your mother's son is your father's heir, and the heir takes the land.
ARTHUR. So his dying breath means nothing? A sworn testament casting out a stranger, totally void?
EDMUND. Just as void as his attempt to sire me, Arthur.
DUCHESS CLARA. A question for you, giant.
Which is it to be? A Roland, stuffed with land and gold, much like your brother here?
Or the acknowledged bastard of Valerius, with nothing to your name but your own swagger?
EDMUND. Madam... if I had his shape.
If my legs were a pair of riding-crops, and my arms withered vines. If my face were so pinched I'd be afraid to wear a heavy helm, lest a stiff wind snap my neck in two!
And if, attached to that miserable frame, I had the ten thousand crowns...
I’d sell every acre just to buy this face back.
I wouldn't be Lord Twig for all the gold in Spain.
DUCHESS CLARA. I like you. Keep your face, leave the dirt to him, and ride with me.
I march for Spain, and I could use a captain's sword.
EDMUND. Choke on the gold, brother; I’ll take my chances in the field.
Your sickly face just bought you ten thousand crowns a year—though at a market, it wouldn't fetch a copper.
Madam, I am yours to the grave.
DUCHESS CLARA. Then lead the way to the court.
EDMUND. A soldier knows his place. After you.
DUKE VINCENT. What is your name, then?
EDMUND. Edmund, my lord. Just Edmund.
The Lady Roland’s eldest boy.

KING CHARLES. I am the King. I have the right to speak.
MORTIMER. My liege, the wounds that brought us to this field
Cannot be stitched with words.
Therefore, be mute.
PERCY. Draw your blade, executioner.
By God and all His saints, I swear
Mortimer’s only courage is in his throat.
FREDERICK. Well, Charles? Will you yield my right or no?
A thousand men stand fasting on this heath;
They will not break their fast until you fall.
SALISBURY. Deny us, and their blood is on your head.
Frederick fights in the armor of his truth.
PRINCE ARTHUR. If treason is the "truth" that Salisbury wears,
Then hell is heaven. Every sin is holy.
PERCY. Whoever sired you, boy, that is your mother speaking.
I know that shrill, complaining tune too well.
QUEEN ELEANOR. And you? You are a crooked, unnatural thing.
A foul mistake.
God marked you out so honest men would know to step aside,
As they would from a rabid dog or a viper in the grass.
PERCY. Spanish brass, painted with English gold!
Your father claims the dukedom of a mud-patch,
As if a gutter could be called the sea.
Have you no shame? Knowing the dirt you sprang from,
Why do you bare your beggar's heart to us?
FREDERICK. A beggar's rags would cost a kingly ransom
To teach this harpy her true worth.
False Cressida was a truer wife than you,
Though your poor husband plays the willing cuckold.
Troy itself was never wrecked so thoroughly
As this whole kingdom has been ruined by you!
His father marched his armies through the East.
He made the foreign emperors bow their heads.
Had he married a woman of his own rank,
He might have left a peaceful realm behind.
Instead, he took a viper to his bed.
He gave your ruined father a golden seat—and in that hour,
He brewed the very storm that washed his conquests away.
What brought this march of iron to your door? Your pride.
Had you been humble, we would have never stirred.
Out of pure pity for this feeble king,
We would have let our title sleep.
MORTIMER. But when we saw your winter kill our spring...
When your dark shadow choked our ripening fields...
We brought the hatchet to your poisoned root.
And though the falling branches hit our own heads,
We will not sheathe our swords. Not now.
We will hew you down, or drown the dry earth with our blood!
FREDERICK. I will not waste another breath on you,
Since you forbid the wretched King to speak.
Sound the horns. Let the bloody banners fly.
We win the crown today, or else we die.
QUEEN ELEANOR. Hold, Frederick—
FREDERICK. No! No more talking, brawling woman.
Your bitter tongue has cost ten thousand lives.
[Exeunt.]
SCENE III. A field of battle near Ravenspur
Alarums. Excursions. Enter Salisbury.
SALISBURY. I am spent.
Like a hound that’s lost the scent and run itself to near death,
I must lean on my sword. Just for a moment.
The blows I've taken—and the heavy ones I've dealt—
Have sucked the marrow straight from my bones.
I must draw breath or die where I stand.
Enter Frederick, running.
FREDERICK. Weep, heaven, or strike me down, earth!
The day is lost. My star is falling.
SALISBURY. What news, my lord? Is there no hope left?
Enter Percy.
PERCY. Hope is dirt. Our fortune is a mass grave.
The lines are shattered and the vanguard is broken.
What do we do? Where do we run?
FREDERICK. We don't. Their horses run like the wind;
We are too weak to outpace them.
Enter Mortimer.
MORTIMER. Salisbury! Why did you abandon the flank?
The soil has drunk your nephew's blood.
Arthur's heavy spear went right through him.
In the bitter agony of his last breath, he screamed over the din:
"Salisbury, avenge me! Uncle, avenge my fall!"
Then the horses took him.
Crushed beneath the hooves, their iron shoes dyed in his red life...
The boy gave up his soul.
SALISBURY. Then let the meadows choke on our blood!
I will kill my own horse. I will not flee.
Why are we weeping like frightened children?
Mourning our ruin while the butchers laugh at us—
Staring at the mud as if this were just a pageant on a stage!
Here. On this bloodied grass. I swear to God:
I will not take one step back. I will not yield an inch.
Not until the night seals my eyes shut,
Or Heaven gives me the vengeance I am owed.

VALERIUS. What, did you not drop straight down from the clouds?
PEASANT. The clouds? God save me, no. I've never been half that high, and I pray I never am. I’m just taking these capons to the lord justice. Got to settle a bloody mess of a fight between my cousin and the king's tax-gatherer.
CASSIUS. Brother, look no further. Here is the perfect vessel for your suit. Let him carry the birds to the King.
VALERIUS. Tell me, friend. Are you able to present a petition to the King with... a certain grace?
PEASANT. Grace? Well, sir, the only man I've ever petitioned is the tavern-keeper when the ale runs dry.
VALERIUS. Come closer, sirrah. No more dallying.
You shall give your plump birds to his Majesty,
And through my word, find favor at the court.
Here, take this copper for your trouble.
Give me the wax and seal. Now, tell me true:
Can you deliver my plea with a bold face?
PEASANT. Aye, my lord, I can.
VALERIUS. Then take this scroll. When you come before him, drop to your knees on the stones. Kiss his signet ring, hand over the birds, and wait for your great reward. I'll be close by. Do it without fear.
PEASANT. Never fear, sir. I know my business.
VALERIUS. Have you a gauntlet, sirrah? Let me see it.
Cassius, bind it up within the parchment,
For you have penned it like a groveling slave.
[To the Peasant]
When you have placed it in the King's own hands,
Find me at the tavern and tell me what he says.
PEASANT. God keep you, sir; I'm off. [Exit.]
VALERIUS. Come, Cassius. Lucius, attend me. [Exeunt.]
SCENE IV. The Royal Court.
Enter King Leontes, Queen Isabella, her sons Ferdinand and Roderigo, with Attendants. The King angrily grips several torn placards in his fist.
LEONTES. Look at this! Just look at it, my lords.
Was ever a sovereign of this realm
So openly abused? So mocked to his face?
And for what? For the simple execution of the law!
You know it well. The gods themselves know it.
No matter what these rebels whisper in the streets,
We followed strict and proper law
Against the treasonous nephews of Valerius.
Am I to excuse this because his grief has turned his wits?
Must we sit here and endure his daily mockeries,
His sudden frantic fits and bitter taunts?
Now he is sending letters to the ghosts!
Look here—"To Justice." "To the Fates."
This one "To the Underworld," and this to the bloody sun!
Sweet little broadsides to plaster on our walls.
What is this but a libel on the crown?
He is declaring us corrupt to the mob.
A merry jest, isn't it, gentlemen?
As if to say there is no justice left in Rome.
But by my life, his counterfeit madness
Will be no shield for this treason.
He and his rabble will soon learn that my rule
Is absolute. If my justice has been sleeping,
He has woken it. I will strike the traitor down.
ISABELLA. My gracious sovereign, my noble King...
The very sun and center of my heart.
Have patience with old Valerius.
These are just the bitter fruits of sorrow;
The bloody end of his kin has pierced his soul.
It is far better to ignore his manic grief
Than punish an old, broken warrior for his tears.
[Aside.]
So Isabella plays the gentle, cooing dove.
But old Valerius, I have already set the snare.
Once you are dead and Roderigo is safe,
The realm is finally ours. My fears go with you to the grave.
Enter Peasant.
How now, fellow? Do you seek the court?
PEASANT. Aye, truly, if your ladyship happens to be the queenship.
ISABELLA. I am the Queen. Yonder sits the King.
PEASANT. That's him? Right. God and Saint George give you good morrow. I've brought you a parchment and a brace of fat capons here.
[Leontes snatches the scroll and reads.]
LEONTES. Guards! Drag this villain to the block. Sever his head!
PEASANT. Right, and how much is the reward for that?
ISABELLA. Come along, fool. You are to be beheaded.
PEASANT. Beheaded! By my troth, I should have kept the birds and carried my own head in the basket. [Exit guarded.]
LEONTES. Outrageous! Unbearable affronts!
Am I expected to stomach this abuse?
I know exactly who built this foul jest.
He wants the world to think his rebel kin—
Who were lawfully shortened by the axe—
Were slaughtered by my tyranny and hate!

We had a pact, drawn up after endless tears. It went something like this: I was not to squander the southern estate, and I would remain her lawful husband. My trips to the city couldn't last more than a month unless she gave her blessing. I wasn't to parade any permanent mistresses around the province. In return? Elena Ivanovna would turn a blind eye to my little liaisons with the local widows, so long as I kept things utterly discreet. I was never, God forbid, to ask for a formal separation. And finally—should I ever be struck by a genuine, consuming passion (heaven help us)—I had to confess it to her at once.
She felt perfectly safe on that last point. Elena Ivanovna had a coldly logical mind, you see. To her, I was just a jaded cynic. Completely incapable of real devotion. But logic and wounded vanity are a terrible mix, and that is exactly where our misery started.
Look, to judge people fairly, you have to drop the usual moralizing. Forget how society looks at things. I trust your intellect over the crowd's, so I'll be frank. You’ve probably heard a lot of scandalous nonsense about my wife. She had her eccentricities, her foolish moments, yes. But honestly? I genuinely regret the profound misery I caused her. Well. Let that stand as a eulogy for the tolerant wife of a highly intolerable man.
Whenever she raged at me, I usually just stared out the window and smoked. Complete silence. It was a sort of passive, gentlemanly cruelty, and it almost always worked. Eventually, it even made her feel she’d won.
Your cousin, though. She couldn't abide your cousin—not at the end, anyway. How she ever brought such an extraordinary creature into our suffocating house to teach music is beyond me. My best guess? Elena Ivanovna was prone to these sudden, dramatic obsessions. She simply fell in love—platonically—with the girl. And who wouldn't? Look at Natalya Pavlovna!
I saw the danger the moment she arrived. Believe it or not, I actually resolved to play blind to her charms. But it was Natalya Pavlovna who took the first step. Can you imagine? And my wife—this is the best part—my wife was furious with me at first because I wouldn't join in her rapturous, daily praises of the girl. I don't know what she wanted from me!
Naturally, she spilled all my darkest secrets to Natalya. Elena Ivanovna had this wretched compulsion to broadcast our private miseries to anyone who'd listen. Why would she spare her charming new confidante? They must have spent hours dissecting me. Natalya Pavlovna undoubtedly absorbed every grotesque legend floating around about my youth. I'd wager you've caught wind of them, too?"
"I have," Alexei said. "Ivanov claimed you drove a young cadet to suicide. Is it true?"
"Spare me those melodramatic inventions," Grigory Ilyich muttered, his face darkening. "If you really must wallow in that nonsense, I'll enlighten you another time. But for now—"
"They also talked about a serf on your brother's estate. The one you had beaten."
"Drop it," Grigory Ilyich cut in, his patience snapping. "Is this the same serf whose ghost supposedly haunts my study? You brought him up yourself."
Alexei frowned, his expression hardening. Grigory Ilyich watched him closely. For a second, Alexei could have sworn he saw a flicker of malicious amusement in the older man's eyes, but it vanished instantly. Grigory Ilyich's face smoothed into a mask of perfect, chilling politeness.
"Yes, exactly. Your curiosity is clearly inflamed. Don't worry, I promise to gratify it fully when the moment is right. Good heavens! I'm starting to realize I must cut quite the tragic, romantic figure for the locals. Think of how grateful I should be to my wife! Feeding Natalya Pavlovna such lurid, captivating myths about my past. I can't guess exactly how it affected her, but it certainly served my purposes. In spite of her absolute purity—and my own thoroughly repulsive nature—she felt pity. At the very least, she felt profound compassion for a ruined man."

Alarum. Enter Titus.
TITUS. Lost! All of it, lost. I cannot bear to look.
The sapphire galley—our Phoenician pride—
With all her fifty vessels, turns the prow and flees.
My eyes are cursed.
Enter Marcellus.
MARCELLUS. Gods and demons! The whole black host of them!
TITUS. What brings this rage on you?
MARCELLUS. Half the world is tossed away to absolute madness. We've just bartered away empires. Whole territories, gone.
TITUS. How stands the vanguard?
MARCELLUS. Sick to death. It's like a plague on our flank where ruin is guaranteed. That painted sovereign of the East—may rot devour her! Right in the bleeding heat of war, when victory was balanced on a knife's edge... no, when it was leaning to our favor... panic takes her. Like a startled deer, she raises the oars and bolts.
TITUS. I saw it. My eyes shrank from the sight. I couldn't stomach another second of it.
MARCELLUS. But once she turned... the shattered monument of her thrall, Valerius. He spreads his sails and, like a blinded, lovesick gull, abandons the clash at its very peak to chase her! I have never witnessed a retreat so disgraceful. Wisdom, discipline, simple manhood—none of them have ever debased themselves like this.
TITUS. Alas.
Enter Lucan.
LUCAN. Our luck on the waves is drowned. We're sinking, bitterly. If our commander had only been the man he was born to be, we would have won the day. But he set the pattern for cowardice, and did it himself!
TITUS. Is that where your mind is set? Then the day is truly done.
LUCAN. They've escaped toward the northern straits.
MARCELLUS. The wind is fair for it. I'll wait there to see what follows.
LUCAN. Not I. I'll yield my banners and my steel to the usurper. Five captains have already shown me the path.
TITUS. I'll still follow the broken fate of great Valerius, even if my own reason screams against it.
[Exeunt.]
SCENE XI. A Chamber in the Citadel.
Enter Valerius with attendants.
VALERIUS. Listen. The earth itself commands I tread no more upon its dirt. It blushes to hold me. Come here, comrades. I am so shadowed in this fading world that I have lost the sun forever. I have a vault bursting with silver. Take it. Share it out among yourselves, flee, and make your peace with the enemy.
ALL. Flee? Not us.
VALERIUS. I have run myself! I've taught the bravest men to turn and show their backs. Go, my friends. I have decided on a path that requires no escort. Begone. The silver is in the fortress—take it! Oh, I chased a sight that burns my eyes to see. My very blood makes war on itself. My old age mocks my youthful fire, and they rebuke each other for cowardice and lust. Good friends, leave me. I will give you seals to wealthy lords who will guarantee your safety. I pray you, don't weep. And don't speak to me of your devotion. Take the cue my ruin offers you. Leave a man who has forsaken himself. Go to the harbor now; I will give you the ships and the coin. Give me some peace, I beg of you. Pray you, go. Do it. I have lost the right to give orders, and so I beg you. I'll join you shortly.
[Sits down.]
Enter Xanthia led by Livia, Flavia, and Pindarus.
PINDARUS. Nay, gentle lady, go to him! Bring him comfort.
FLAVIA. Do, my sovereign.
LIVIA. Speak to him! What else is left?
XANTHIA. Let me rest here. Oh, Fates!
VALERIUS. No, no, no, no, no.
PINDARUS. Do you see him, my lord?
VALERIUS. Oh, shame. Shame, shame!
LIVIA. Madam.
FLAVIA. Madam, sweet queen!
PINDARUS. Lord, my lord!
VALERIUS. Yes, the conqueror. Yes! He kept his blade at the river like a polished ornament, while I hewed down the seasoned Thracians! It was I who broke the wild rebels. He only traded in his deputies. He had no footing in the bloody dust of war. But now... it matters not.
XANTHIA. Ah, stand aside.
PINDARUS. The Queen, my lord. The Queen!
FLAVIA. Approach him, madam. Give him words. He is undone with sheer disgrace.
XANTHIA. Well then, support me. Oh!
PINDARUS. Mighty general, stand. The Queen draws near. Her face is fallen, and despair will claim her unless your pity grants a reprieve.
VALERIUS. I have murdered my own reputation. A most unmanly betrayal.
PINDARUS. Sir. Your Queen.
VALERIUS. O, where have you dragged me, Sovereign? Look how I must hide my ruin from your gaze. I can only look back at what I left behind, crushed in my disgrace.
XANTHIA. My lord... my lord, forgive my trembling fleet! I never dreamed that you would track my flight.
VALERIUS. Sovereign, you knew too well. My soul was tethered tight to your canvas, and you would drag me with you.

"It's a complete catastrophe, there's no other word for it."
"Then speak to Clara yourself! Tell her you absolutely demand she marry him."
"By all means, send for the girl. She can hear my verdict right now."
Mrs. Sterling violently yanked the bell-pull. A minute later, Clara stepped into the study.
"Come in, Clara," her father said, leaning back in his leather armchair. "We have a matter of grave importance to settle. Word has reached me that Mr. Pendleton asked for your hand this morning. True?"
Clara nodded. "It is."
"Right. And you turned him down?"
"I did, Father."
"Splendid. That brings us to the crux of the issue. Your mother here insists you accept the man. Isn't that right, Mrs. Sterling?"
"Yes! Or I'll never look at her again."
Mr. Sterling sighed heavily. "Well, Clara, you face a grim dilemma. As of today, you are destined to lose a parent. Your mother refuses to ever see you again if you do not marry Mr. Pendleton. And I refuse to see you again if you do."
Clara couldn't help but smile at the trap he'd sprung. Mrs. Sterling, however, had spent the last hour convinced her husband was entirely on her side. She stared at him in sheer outrage.
"What on earth are you talking about, Mr. Sterling? You gave me your word you would force the match!"
"My dear," he said smoothly, "I only ask two small favors of you today. The first is that you allow me to use my own brain. The second is that you allow me the use of my study. I'd very much like the room to myself now."
Being dismissed did nothing to stop Mrs. Sterling. She spent the rest of the afternoon badgering Clara, bouncing wildly between sweet coaxing and bitter threats. She tried to rope her eldest daughter, Mary, into the fight, but Mary politely refused to take the bait. Clara just weathered the storm. Sometimes she answered seriously, sometimes she teased her mother back. Her mood shifted, but her answer never changed.
As for Mr. Pendleton, he spent the afternoon brooding alone. He thought far too highly of his own charm to understand how anyone could reject him. His pride was certainly bruised, but his heart was fine. He'd only ever liked the idea of Clara anyway. Plus, knowing she was about to face the full force of her mother’s wrath made him feel a lot better about the whole thing.
Right in the middle of all this chaos, Eleanor Hayes came to call.
Beatrice caught her in the hallway before she could even take off her bonnet. "Oh, thank goodness you're here, it's a madhouse!" she stage-whispered. "You'll never guess. Pendleton proposed to Clara this morning, and she told him no!"
Before Eleanor could even process the gossip, Sarah bounded out to repeat it. They dragged Eleanor into the sitting room, where Mrs. Sterling was wallowing alone.
The older woman immediately lunged for sympathy. "Eleanor, thank God. Please, talk some sense into her! Nobody in this house cares about me. I'm treated appallingly, and not one of them gives a thought to my blood pressure."
Clara and Mary walked in just then, saving Eleanor from having to think of a polite response.
"Oh, look at her," Mrs. Sterling spat. "Waltzing in without a care in the world. She'd watch us all starve as long as she gets her way. Listen to me, Clara—if you keep turning your nose up at perfectly good men, you're going to die an old maid. And I certainly won't have the money to keep you once your father is dead! So consider this your warning. I wash my hands of you. I told you in the study I'd never speak to you again, and I meant it. I despise talking to ungrateful children. In fact, I despise talking to anyone today. My nerves are completely shattered, and I'm in no mood for conversation."

VALERIUS: Do you smell the scandal?
CASSIUS: Can't say I'd wish it undone. The result is just too handsome.
VALERIUS: The funny thing is, I have a legitimate boy. Older by a year. But I don't love him any more than this one. Sure, the rogue crashed the party before the priest gave his blessing, but his mother was a beauty. We had a magnificent time making him. The bastard has to be acknowledged. Have you met the young man? Marcus!
MARCUS: No, my lord. I haven't had the pleasure.
VALERIUS: Cassius, consider him a good friend from now on.
MARCUS: An honor, sir.
CASSIUS: The pleasure is mine. I hope we get to know each other.
MARCUS: I'll do my best to earn it.
VALERIUS: He’s been away nine years. He’ll be leaving again soon. Shh—the King.
(Trumpets sound. King Leontes enters with the Dukes of Milan and Venice, his daughters Isabella, Beatrice, and Diana, and various attendants.)
LEONTES: Valerius. Go fetch the ambassadors from Aragon and Naples.
VALERIUS: At once, your grace. (Valerius and Marcus exit.)
LEONTES: In the meantime, let's lay out our darker intentions. Bring the map over here. Right. We’ve carved the empire into three. It's our firm plan to strip the heavy lifting off our shoulders and drop it onto younger backs. Let us crawl toward the grave without the baggage. Milan... Venice... today we're making our daughters' dowries public, hoping to kill any future squabbles before they start.
Aragon and Naples have been hovering around the court for months, fighting over our youngest. They want an answer today. But first: daughters. Since I am officially stripping myself of the crown, the land, and the headaches of state—which of you loves me the most? Let's see where natural affection meets actual merit. Isabella. You’re the eldest. Go.
ISABELLA: Father. I love you more than words can actually measure. More than my own eyesight. More than the open air. Beyond any treasure you could name—as much as life itself. I love you with a devotion that makes my breath catch and my words fail. Beyond any limit, I love you.
DIANA: (Aside) What am I supposed to say to that? Just love him, and keep my mouth shut.
LEONTES: Take a look. From this river to the valley—the dark woods, the plains, the pastures. You are sovereign here. To you and Milan’s heirs, forever. Now. What about our second daughter? Beatrice. Speak.
BEATRICE: I’m cut from the exact same cloth as my sister, sire. In my heart, she just spoke my own truth. Except she didn't go far enough. I consider myself a sworn enemy to every other earthly joy. I find my only actual happiness in your love.
DIANA: (Aside) Poor Diana. No, not poor. My love is just heavier than my words.
LEONTES: To you and your bloodline, we leave this wealthy third of the realm. Equal in scope and revenue to Isabella’s. And now. Our final joy. The youngest, but certainly not the least. The prize Aragon and Naples are currently ripping each other apart over. What can you say to claim a slice richer than your sisters'? Go on.
DIANA: Nothing, my lord.
LEONTES: Nothing?
DIANA: Nothing.
LEONTES: Nothing comes from nothing. Try again.
DIANA: I'm sorry, I just can't drag my heart into my mouth. I love you exactly as a daughter should. No more, no less.
LEONTES: Watch yourself, Diana. Fix your tone before you ruin your own life.
DIANA: You fathered me. You raised me, and you loved me. I'm returning that exact debt. I obey you, and I honor you. Why do my sisters even have husbands if they claim they love only you? When I marry, the man who takes my vows is going to take half my love with him. Half my care. I will never marry like my sisters—keeping all my love for my father.
LEONTES: Is this really where your heart is?
DIANA: It is.
LEONTES: So young, and so cold?
DIANA: Young, my lord. And honest.

Picture Avarice giving orders to the virtues. She tells Prudence to stay awake at night, obsessively calculating market shifts just to keep the vault secure. She tells Justice to pay debts and honor contracts—not out of any real sense of fairness, but simply to dodge the magistrate and keep the trade routes open. Then there is Fortitude. Avarice orders Fortitude to suffer through freezing winters and exhausting labor, using the sheer promise of future profits to numb the pain of the present work. And Temperance? She is commanded to eat scraps and wear threadbare coats, terrified that a single lavish meal might shrink the hoard.
In this grotesque scenario, the virtues are stripped of their majesty. They become nothing more than accountants and bodyguards for Wealth. It’s a degrading picture. Scholars rightly call it perverse, something any decent person should find repulsive.
But honestly, it isn't much better if we swap out Wealth for Intellectual Prestige. Sure, academic pride isn't a crass material obsession, but it’s still bloated with arrogance. It is entirely beneath the dignity of the virtues to serve as handmaids to vanity. Why should Prudence study, or Temperance practice restraint, if the only goal is to win an argument or stroke one's own ego? Even the introverted scholars who claim to despise public applause aren't immune. They retreat to their studies to admire their own minds. But flattering yourself is still flattering a human being.
True piety looks entirely different. A person anchored in God looks inward and sees their own poverty. They aren't trying to please themselves; they want to align with the truth. Whatever goodness they find in their character, they credit to God's grace, not their own brilliance. They give thanks for the parts of their soul that have been healed, and they pray over the broken parts that remain.
Worldly prosperity comes only from the true authority that governs the universe.
Because of this, we cannot credit the rise of wealthy empires to anyone but the true God. He saves the actual treasures of heaven for the righteous, but He hands out earthly money and power to both the good and the wicked. He does this exactly as He pleases, and His reasons are always just, even when they baffle us. We might catch glimpses of His grand design here and there. But overall? It is completely beyond our mental capacity to untangle the hidden motives in human hearts and figure out exactly why one nation gets rich while another goes bankrupt.
The one true God never abandons the world to chaos. He gave immense wealth and maritime dominance to the Carthaginians when He felt like it. He did the same for the Greeks and the Egyptians, even though the Egyptians worshipped crocodiles and the Nile. (This leaves aside the Israelites, who actually worshipped the true God while they had their kingdom). God gave the Egyptians bumper crops, even though they never bothered to pray to Ceres. He gave them gold from the earth, even though they didn't worship the spirits of the soil. He handed them absolute economic dominance, regardless of the fact that they completely ignored the false gods other nations insisted were responsible for such blessings.

"So we invited the Woodward girls, but Amelia was sick, which meant Charlotte had to come by herself. And then—oh, you'll never guess what we did! We dressed young Townsend up in a velvet bonnet and a silk pelisse, just to see if we could pass him off as a visiting dowager. It was the funniest thing you can imagine. Nobody knew a thing about it except Captain Lennox, his wife, me, and Florence. We had to borrow my aunt’s clothes, you see, but he looked surprisingly elegant! When Carter and the other lieutenants finally walked in, they were totally fooled. I thought I was going to die laughing. Mrs. Lennox was giggling so hard she gave the whole thing away, and the men finally figured it out."
Chattering about these pranks—with Florence occasionally chiming in to exaggerate a detail—kept the carriage noisy all the way back to Willowbourne. Eleanor tried her best to tune them out. Unfortunately, it was impossible to ignore how often Carter’s name came up.
At least the welcome home was warm. Mrs. Crawford loudly admired Jane’s radiant complexion, while Mr. Crawford quietly caught Eleanor’s eye during dinner and muttered, "Glad you're back, Eleanor."
The dining room was packed. The Winslow family had trooped over to hear all the gossip, creating a chaotic web of overlapping conversations. Across the table, Lady Winslow interrogated Jane about her married sister’s vegetable gardens. At the head of the table, Mrs. Crawford was frantically multitasking: pumping Jane for details on the latest city fashions while simultaneously broadcasting those same details to the younger Winslow girls.
Above it all rose Florence’s piercing voice.
"You really should have come, Beatrice," Florence practically shouted. "We had the best time! We pulled all the carriage blinds down on the way there and pretended the coach was empty. I would've kept them down the whole trip if Charlotte hadn't started feeling car-sick. Oh, and when we stopped at the Red Lion, we were so generous! We bought everyone the most amazing pastries. If you'd been there, we would have bought you one too. By the time we left, I was laughing so hard I could barely climb back into the carriage. We were so loud on the way home, half the county must have heard us!"
Beatrice looked up from her plate with intense solemnity. "I wouldn't dream of criticizing your hobbies, Florence. I am sure they are perfectly suited to the average mind. But they hold absolutely no appeal for me. I would rather read a book."
Florence didn't hear a word of it. She rarely listened to anyone for more than ten seconds, and she completely ignored Beatrice as a rule.
Later that afternoon, Florence begged the girls to walk down to the village to see what the soldiers were doing. Eleanor shut the idea down immediately. She wasn't about to let the neighbors say the Crawford girls couldn't be home for six hours before chasing after the militia. Plus, she was terrified of running into Carter. She planned to avoid him entirely. The thought of his regiment leaving next month was the only thing keeping her sane; once they marched out of town, she hoped she would never have to worry about him again.
Before the evening was out, Eleanor realized her mother’s grandiose plan to vacation at the seaside—which Florence had blabbed about earlier—was currently the main battlefield between her parents. It was obvious her father wasn't going to spend the money, but he was dodging the question so skillfully that Mrs. Crawford still thought she might win.
CHAPTER XX.
By the next morning, Eleanor couldn't keep her secret a second longer. She decided to filter out anything that might hurt Jane’s feelings, warned her sister to brace herself, and finally spilled the entire story of her explosive confrontation with Mr. Sterling.

These stone markers might as well be sitting in the Roman catacombs. It's a strange thing, how we categorize the dead. A common adage says the sea keeps her secrets—and she certainly holds more than any emperor's vault—but why do we dress in pitch-black for a friend who died yesterday, yet wear our brightest clothes if he merely sets sail for the Orient? We weep bitterly for people we claim are walking the streets of heaven. A sudden dark shape in the water is enough to terrify the bravest sailor. Contradictions, all of them, but they carry a certain weight. Belief is a scavenger. It circles the wreckage, pulling its most vital nourishment straight from our deepest despairs.
You can probably guess how I felt. It was the night before I was supposed to leave for the Arctic fisheries, and I was standing in the dim, weeping light of that chapel, reading the doom of the mariners who had gone before me. Yes, Elias, I told myself. That same doom might be waiting for you.
But somehow, my spirit rallied. What marvelous incentives to weigh anchor! Excellent prospects for glory. A crushed hull would make me an angel by default. There is a freezing kind of demise in the ice trade—a breathtakingly sudden plunge into the abyss—but what does it actually matter? I think we’ve completely misunderstood flesh and spirit. The physical form walking this earth is just a shadow. When we try to look at eternity, we're like barnacles staring at the moon through the brine, convinced the saltwater is the clearest sky. My body is just a heavy diving-bell for the mind. Let the sea take it. It isn't me.
Hurrah for the North. Let the crushing ice and the broken timbers come whenever they please. Neptune himself couldn't crush my soul.
CHAPTER 9. The Chancel.
I hadn't been sitting long when the wind blew the heavy chapel door wide open. A man of weathered dignity stepped through. The door slammed shut behind him, and the entire congregation turned to look, their quiet respect making it instantly clear: this was the minister.
It was Reverend Silas. The sealers all knew him, and they held him in the highest regard. He’d spent his youth as a navigator and harpooner before giving his life to the church. He was in the frosty winter of a vigorous old age, the kind of age that seems to hide a second youth burning just beneath the surface. Deep in the crevices of his weathered face, you could see the warm embers of renewed vitality. A summer sun burning right through the December frost.
Even if you didn't know his history, you couldn't look at Silas without being curious. He carried himself with the ingrained habits of a man who had survived a perilous life at sea. He certainly hadn't arrived in a carriage. His canvas sou’wester dripped with freezing rain, and his massive wool coat was so soaked it looked like it was dragging him to the floor.
One by one, he stripped off the hat, the coat, and his heavy boots, hanging them in a small alcove by the vestibule. Underneath, he wore a solemn black suit.
He walked steadily toward the chancel. It was an antique structure, elevated high above the floor. A standard set of steps would have eaten up too much of the cramped seating area, so the builder had apparently taken a suggestion from the Reverend himself. There were no stairs. Instead, a slanted gangway plank led up to the peak, just like the ones used to board a frigate from the docks. A shipwright's widow had even braided a set of hemp handrails for it. The wood was polished to a rich oak hue, and honestly, considering the kind of church this was, the whole setup felt perfectly right.

The apostle offers a clear directive: "Set your minds on things that are above, not on things that are on earth; for you have died, and your life is hidden with Christ in God." Those who actually live by this principle find a strange comfort when political ruin or sudden exile strikes. They realize that the spiritual honors they’ve secured through humility bring them far more joy than any grief caused by the loss of their worldly titles. After all, when a kingdom collapses, the only things that truly perish are the things tied to the dust in the first place.
Consider Christ's warning not to fear those who can only kill the body, but rather to fear the one who can destroy both body and soul. People who take this seriously find out, in times of severe persecution, just how wise it is to trust the ultimate defender of their lives. It's one thing to feel relieved because your physical body is hiding behind fortress walls the enemy hasn't breached yet. It’s a far deeper peace to know you’ve anchored your existence in a sanctuary no human weapon can ever touch.
I think of Martin. He willingly gave up the prestige of a magistrate's seat to live as a humble servant, becoming immensely rich in grace instead. When usurpers finally overran the province and threw him in chains, he didn't panic. He later told me his silent prayer during captivity: "Lord, let me not tremble for my status or my flesh. You alone hold my true dignity." He had deposited his honor exactly where he was taught to put it, trusting the same God who warned us that earthly thrones would eventually fall.
Because of this, those who listened to the Master didn't lose their essential nobility, even when they were degraded in prison cells. As for the others—those who now bitterly regret ignoring His advice—they are finally learning the true value of human praise. If they couldn't learn it through a humility that would have protected their pride, they are learning it through the humiliation that shattered it.
Of course, the objection is raised that some genuinely devout people were thrown into dungeons and tortured to extract political confessions. But let's look closer. They couldn't actually forfeit the inward loyalty that made them good in the first place. If they chose to endure the rack simply to protect their earthly political ambitions, then they weren't truly devout. Someone should have pointed out the irony: if they were willing to suffer that much for a temporary crown, they should have been willing to endure it for the King of Heaven. Suffering for the favor of a mortal monarch is a tragedy, whether you keep that favor by lying or lose it by telling the truth.
Under the whip, no one loses their salvation by confessing it, and no one keeps their earthly power without compromising their soul. In the end, the torture that forces someone to value an eternal kingdom over a temporary office is far more useful than the office itself, which only ever brought its owner anxiety.
But what about those who were tortured because they were suspected of having political influence, even when they actually had none? Their captors simply refused to believe them. Even here, it’s entirely possible they harbored a secret, unsanctified lust for power. If so, the pain served to show them that merely craving worldly authority is just as toxic as possessing it. And what if they were entirely free of that ambition, living only for the life to come? I can't say for sure if anyone completely innocent of political ambition was tortured on the mere suspicion of it. But if they were, then by confessing their holy obscurity under the torturer's hands, they were ultimately just confessing their allegiance to Christ.

"Have you tracked the Shadow Leviathan?" Elias called out as the skiff pitched closer to the hull.
"Reflect on your shattered timbers! Think of your drowned men! Beware the sweeping fin!"
"I declare to you again, Malachi, that—"
Captain Thorne's words were cut off. The small boat jerked forward, shoved by some unseen force beneath the water. Nobody spoke for a long minute. A chaotic run of rogue waves surged past them, churning rather than swelling, driven by a strange oceanic humor. Tied alongside the ship, the carcass of a pilot whale pitched violently in the froth. Malachi stared down at it. For a self-proclaimed prophet, he looked terrified.
Once the sea settled, Thorne finally told the story of the Shadow Leviathan. He couldn't get through it without Malachi interrupting every time the beast's name was spoken.
It turned out the Providence had barely rounded the Cape when they hailed a passing merchantman and first heard about the Leviathan's path of ruin. Malachi had soaked up every dark detail. He began threatening the captain, warning him never to pursue it. In his fevered mind, the Leviathan was the Wrath of the Almighty made flesh—a divine instrument of judgment.
But months later, the lookout spotted the beast from the rigging. Silas, the first mate, was practically burning with the need to kill it. Despite the prophet’s screaming omens, Thorne gave Silas the go-ahead. The mate grabbed six men and lowered his boat.
They rowed for hours. After several near-misses, Silas finally managed to sink a harpoon into the creature's flank.
High above them on the ship, Malachi had climbed to the cross-trees. He was thrashing his arms, screaming that doom was coming for the wicked men attacking his false idol.
Down in the boat, Silas stood tall in the prow. He was shouting, full of reckless adrenaline, maneuvering to strike again with his raised lance.
Then a massive dark shape breached. The sheer force of the displacement sucked the air right out of the rowers' lungs. A heartbeat later, the mate was launched bodily into the sky. He traced a high, long arc and plunged into the waves forty yards away. The boat wasn't scratched. None of the rowers lost a single drop of blood.
But Silas never came back up.
Oddly enough, this kind of accident is shockingly common in the deep-sea fisheries. Sometimes a man is just extinguished, leaving the boat completely intact. Other times the stem is shattered, or the bracing-plank is ripped out and falls into the sea with the harpooneer. The strangest part is when the body is actually recovered. Often, there isn't a single bruise or broken bone to be found. The man is just dead.
The crew watched the entire disaster from the deck of the Providence.
"The Seventh Seal is broken!" Malachi shrieked, commanding the terrified crew to abandon the hunt.
The tragedy gave the madman an iron grip over the crew. They actually believed he had predicted Silas's death with divine accuracy, rather than just shouting a broad curse that happened to come true in a highly dangerous profession. From that day on, he became a walking terror aboard the ship.
When Thorne finished the story, Elias asked a few pointed questions. Finally, the visiting captain had to ask if Elias actually intended to chase the Leviathan if he got the chance.
"Aye," Elias said.
Malachi sprang to his feet. He glared at the scarred captain and pointed a trembling finger at the water. "Think of the dead mate! Rotting in the dark! Beware the sinner's grave!"
Elias barely even blinked. He turned away and looked down at Thorne. "Captain, I just remembered my dispatch box. I believe I have a parcel for one of your men. Mr. Vance, fetch the mail."
It was a common enough practice. Voyaging vessels always carried letters for other ships, trusting the sheer coincidence of crossing paths in the trackless oceans to get the mail where it belonged.

"What possible good does it do to alienate Mr. Hawthorne?" Eleanor whispered frantically. "You certainly aren't going to win over his best friend by acting like this."
It was no use. Her mother ignored the warning entirely, continuing to broadcast her grand social schemes at the exact same piercing volume. Eleanor felt her cheeks burning. Wave after wave of humiliation washed over her. She couldn't stop herself from casting nervous glances across the room at Mr. Hawthorne, even though every look only confirmed her worst fears. He wasn't staring directly at her mother the whole time, but Eleanor knew he was listening to every single word. She watched the expression on his face harden, slowly shifting from shocked disdain to a mask of freezing, impenetrable stoicism.
Eventually, though, Mrs. Stanton ran out of breath. Lady Bertram—who had spent the better part of an hour stifling yawns while listening to social triumphs she would never share—was finally left in peace with a plate of cold roast pheasant.
Eleanor exhaled.
The relief didn't last. The moment the supper plates were cleared, someone proposed musical entertainment. To Eleanor's absolute horror, Beatrice needed barely a whisper of encouragement before marching over to the pianoforte. Eleanor tried everything—desperate glares, silent pleading motions—to stop her sister's eager exhibition. Beatrice was blind to it all. The chance to show off was simply too intoxicating, and she launched into a ballad.
Eleanor sat in agony. She tracked the painfully slow progression of the verses, praying for the song to end. But her patience was bitterly rewarded. Beatrice took the polite, tepid applause as a desperate demand for an encore. She paused just long enough to draw a breath, then plunged straight into a second melody.
She simply didn't have the talent for this kind of display. Her pitch wobbled terribly, and her delivery was hopelessly, embarrassingly melodramatic. Eleanor looked around the room in a panic. She checked Charlotte to see how she was bearing the torture, but Charlotte was perfectly happy, completely absorbed in her conversation with Mr. Alden. Hawthorne’s haughty sisters, however, were openly exchanging smirks. Hawthorne himself sat like a granite statue.
Desperate, Eleanor shot a pleading look at her father. Do something, she prayed, terrified Beatrice was going to screech until dawn.
Mr. Stanton took the hint. The very second Beatrice finished her second song, he spoke up from his armchair. "That will do perfectly well, my dear. You've entertained us quite enough for one evening. Let the other young ladies have a turn."
Beatrice pretended not to hear the slight, but she flushed bright pink, clearly rattled. Eleanor’s stomach dropped. She felt awful for her sister and hated her father's tactless delivery, instantly regretting that she had begged him to intervene at all.
By then, the rest of the room was moving on.
"If the Almighty had blessed me with a singing voice," Mr. Whitfield suddenly announced to the room at large, "I assure you, I would be exceedingly glad to favor you all with a hymn. I regard musical exertion as a perfectly wholesome pastime—highly suitable for a clergyman! I don't mean to suggest one should dedicate excessive hours to it, naturally. A vicar has much heavier burdens."
He paused, ensuring he had an audience. "Primarily, he must negotiate his tithes so they are profitable to him, yet entirely agreeable to his noble patron. He must compose sermons! The hours leftover are barely enough for pastoral visits and the upkeep of the parsonage. And, of course, I consider it a matter of supreme importance that a clergyman exhibit a flattering, subservient demeanor to everyone—especially the aristocratic hands that feed him. I could never respect a man who failed to seize every public occasion to broadcast his deep reverence for anyone connected to his patron's noble line."
To punctuate this bizarre, unprompted speech, Whitfield executed a deep, sweeping bow directly at Mr. Hawthorne. He had spoken so loudly that every single person in the parlor heard him.
People stared. A few hid their smiles behind their hands. Mr. Stanton looked absolutely delighted by the sheer absurdity of it all, while his wife earnestly whispered to Lady Bertram about what a staggeringly brilliant young man Mr. Whitfield was.
Eleanor closed her eyes. If her relatives had signed a formal, blood-sworn pact to thoroughly disgrace themselves before midnight, they couldn't have executed it with more devastating perfection. Her only comfort was knowing that Mr. Alden was so wrapped up in Charlotte that he had missed most of the circus, and that his nature was far too sweet to be repelled by the profound idiocy he was forced to witness.

The clock chimed four. Eleanor knew it was time to leave, though she hated the thought of going. Miss Harrington politely offered the carriage. Eleanor was just about to accept—needing only the slightest bit of prodding—when Margaret became so distressed at the idea of being left behind that Miss Harrington had no choice. She was forced to turn her offer of a ride into an invitation to stay.
Eleanor accepted with massive relief. A footman was quickly sent off to Willowbourne to let her parents know and to pack a trunk of clothes.
CHAPTER IX.
By half-past five, the ladies went upstairs to change, and a little after seven, Eleanor was called down to dinner.
A chorus of polite inquiries greeted her, though only Charles Harrington’s concern felt remotely genuine. Unfortunately, Eleanor had no good news to share; Margaret wasn't doing any better. Hearing this, the two Harrington sisters immediately launched into a theatrical display of sympathy. They repeated over and over how devastating fevers were, how dreadful the whole situation was, and how much they absolutely hated being sick themselves.
And then, having checked that social box, they dropped the subject entirely.
Watching them completely forget Margaret's existence the moment she was out of sight brought all of Eleanor’s original dislike rushing right back. Charles really was the only person in the room she could stand. He was obviously worried about Margaret, and he went out of his way to make Eleanor feel comfortable, which made her feel a little less like an unwanted intruder.
Certainly, no one else was paying her any attention. Miss Harrington was completely occupied with fawning over Mr. Sterling, and Augusta was doing exactly the same. As for Augusta’s husband, Mr. Vance? He was a sluggish man whose only real interests were eating heavy meats and playing billiards. He happened to be sitting next to Eleanor, but the moment he realized she preferred a simple broth to the heavy, rich sauces, he wrote her off as entirely unworthy of his time.
The second the meal ended, Eleanor escaped back to Margaret's room.
The door had barely clicked shut before Miss Harrington started tearing her apart. Her manners were supposedly atrocious—a terrible mix of arrogance and awkwardness. She had no wit, no elegance, no charm whatsoever.
Augusta eagerly agreed. "She has absolutely nothing going for her, except maybe the fact that she's a very vigorous hiker. I don't think I'll ever recover from seeing her walk in this afternoon. She looked practically feral."
"She really did, Augusta. I actually had to look away! I mean, what utter nonsense to march all the way over here. Why does she have to go tramping across the fields just because her sister caught a chill? Her hair was a total disaster."
"And her coat! Tell me you saw the hem of her coat. She was splashed with mud up to her calves, I'm sure of it. That cloak she wore to cover it up didn't hide a thing."
"You might be right, Augusta," Charles cut in, "but I confess I didn't notice any of that. I thought Miss Eleanor looked exceptionally vibrant when she walked in today. I certainly didn't see any mud."
Miss Harrington turned to the dark-haired guest. "You must have noticed it, Mr. Sterling. I’m quite sure you would never want to see your sister make such a public spectacle of herself."
"Undoubtedly not."
"To march two or three miles—or whatever it is—up to her shins in muck! And completely alone! What on earth was she thinking? To me, it just shows this dreadful, arrogant rebelliousness. A totally provincial disregard for basic propriety."
"It shows she's deeply devoted to her sister," Charles argued. "I think it's highly commendable."
Miss Harrington leaned in, lowering her voice to a theatrical whisper. "I do fear, Mr. Sterling, that this wild little escapade might have lessened your appreciation for her brilliant eyes."
"Not in the slightest," Sterling replied mildly. "The brisk air only made them brighter."
A brief, uncomfortable silence settled over the room. Augusta quickly pivoted to a new angle of attack.
"I really am terribly fond of Margaret. She's a sweet girl, and I'd love to see her make a respectable match. But with parents like hers? And those appallingly low relations? There is zero probability of it."
"Didn't you say their uncle is a clerk down in the village?" Miss Harrington asked.
"Yes! And they have another one who trades in textiles somewhere near Cheapside."
"How marvelous," Miss Harrington said drily.
The two sisters looked at each other and burst into loud, ringing laughter.

LEONORA: Tell me. Now. I wish you had a stutter so you’d just choke the secret out, like water glugging out of a bottleneck—either pouring out in a flood or stopping completely. Just pull the cork out and let me drink the news!
JULIA: Careful, you'll drown on it.
LEONORA: Is he handsome? What kind of man are we talking about? Does he have a noble head? A decent jawline?
JULIA: He has the beginnings of a beard, at least.
LEONORA: Well, time will fix that if he’s patient. I don't care about the beard, just tell me whose face it’s on!
JULIA: It’s Sebastian. The one who unhorsed the champion yesterday and stole your heart in the exact same afternoon.
LEONORA: Stop. Don't mess with me. Look me in the eye and tell me the truth.
JULIA: I swear on my life, cousin. It’s him.
LEONORA: Sebastian?
JULIA: Sebastian.
LEONORA: Oh, God. What am I supposed to do in these ridiculous stable-boy clothes? What was he doing when you saw him? What did he say? How did he look? What was he wearing? Why is he even here? Did he ask about me? Where is he staying? How did you leave things with him, and when are you going to see him again? Answer me in one word.
JULIA: I'd need the mouth of a sea monster. You're asking for the impossible. Just saying "yes" or "no" to half of that would take me an hour.
LEONORA: But does he know I'm hiding in this province? Does he know I'm dressed like a boy? Does he look as incredible as he did at the joust?
JULIA: It would be easier to count the atoms in the air than to answer a woman in love. But listen. Here's how I found him. He was lying under a massive willow tree, curled up like a dropped branch.
LEONORA: A sacred tree, to drop something so gorgeous.
JULIA: Will you let me speak?
LEONORA: Go on.
JULIA: He was stretched out in the grass, looking like an exhausted soldier.
LEONORA: It hurts to think of him suffering, but I bet the grass was thrilled.
JULIA: Rein it in, Leonora, seriously. Your tongue is running away without you. Anyway, he was dressed like a traveling merchant.
LEONORA: Oh, God. He's here to buy my soul.
JULIA: I'd really love to finish this story without the background commentary. You're ruining my flow.
LEONORA: I can't help it! When I have a thought, it has to come out. Go on, sweetie, keep going.
(Enter Sebastian and Cassius)
JULIA: Well, you distracted me. Look—isn't that him?
LEONORA: It is! Hide, hide. Let's listen.
(Leonora and Julia duck behind a hedge.)
CASSIUS: Thanks for the walk. Honestly, though? I'd rather be alone.
SEBASTIAN: The feeling is mutual. But I'm trying to be polite, so thanks for the company.
CASSIUS: Take care of yourself. Let's try to never do this again.
SEBASTIAN: I'd love it if we could be perfect strangers.
CASSIUS: Do me a favor and stop vandalizing the tavern walls by carving your terrible love poems into the wood.
SEBASTIAN: Do me a favor and stop ruining my poems by reading them out loud in that grating voice.
CASSIUS: Your girl's name is Leonora, right?
SEBASTIAN: It is.
CASSIUS: Hate the name.
SEBASTIAN: I don't think anyone was consulting you at her christening.
CASSIUS: How tall is she?
SEBASTIAN: Tall enough to reach my heart.
CASSIUS: Very slick. Did you memorize that off a cheap greeting card?
SEBASTIAN: No, but I'm matching the cheap, painted-on sarcasm you're using to ask these questions.
CASSIUS: You're quick, I'll give you that. Want to sit by the river with me? We can complain about how awful the world is and how miserable we are.
SEBASTIAN: I don't criticize anyone in this world but myself. I'm the only one whose flaws I actually know.
CASSIUS: Your worst flaw is being in love.
SEBASTIAN: A flaw I wouldn't trade for your best virtue. I'm so bored of you.
CASSIUS: I swear, I was wandering around looking for an idiot when I ran into you.
SEBASTIAN: The idiot drowned in the river. Go look in the water, you'll see him.
CASSIUS: If I look in the water, I'm just going to see my own reflection.
SEBASTIAN: Which is either an idiot or a total zero. Take your pick.

FALLOW: That’s the meat and potatoes of it for me. I’ve watched the big mastiff get turned loose a dozen times, and I’m the one who holds him by the leather collar! Course, the ladies always shrieked and cried until it was deafening. Women just can't stomach it. Too rough and ugly for 'em.
(Sterling enters.)
STERLING: Come along, Fallow. We’re all waiting on you.
FALLOW: Oh, I couldn't eat a thing. Thank you, though.
STERLING: Nonsense, I won't take no for an answer! Come on.
FALLOW: No, please, you go ahead.
STERLING: Come on, man.
FALLOW: Mistress Clara? You first.
CLARA: Oh, no. Please, lead the way.
FALLOW: I wouldn't dream of it. Truly! That would be a terrible insult.
CLARA: I insist.
FALLOW: I’d rather be rude than annoying. You do yourself a disservice, truly!
(They exit.)
SCENE II
(Father Clement and Tobias.)
CLEMENT: Go on, then. Stop by the apothecary’s and ask for directions. You're looking for a Dame Prattle. She’s his housekeeper. Or maybe his nurse, or baker, or laundress—basically, she washes the sheets and bakes the bread.
TOBIAS: Got it.
CLEMENT: Wait, there's more. Give her this letter. She's got a lot of influence with Clara Sterling. The letter tells her—well, begs her, really—to push your master’s suit with the girl. Hurry up, now. I need to finish my supper. I think there are roasted apples coming.
(They exit.)
SCENE III. The Boar's Head Tavern.
(Captain Stone, the Innkeeper, Tankard, Slicer, and Flint.)
STONE: Host! Mine excellent host of the Boar's Head!
INNKEEPER: What's the word, my stout general? Lay some wisdom on me.
STONE: Honestly? I need to lay off some of my men.
INNKEEPER: Cut 'em loose, Samson! Send 'em packing. Let them hit the road.
STONE: I'm paying five pounds a week just for my rooms.
INNKEEPER: You're a king, a sultan, an absolute czar! Tell you what. I’ll hire Tankard. He can draw the taps and pour the ale. How’s that sound, Hector?
STONE: You're a lifesaver.
INNKEEPER: Done. Let's see you pour a pint, Tankard. Come on.
(The Innkeeper exits.)
STONE: Follow him, Tankard. Barkeep is a noble job. An old coat makes a great bar rag, and a washed-up thief makes a decent pourer. Farewell.
TANKARD: It’s everything I ever wanted. I'm going to be rich.
FLINT: You miserable wretch. Are you really going to stoop to pouring cheap ale?
(Tankard exits.)
SLICER: He was probably conceived in a wine barrel anyway. Funny, right?
STONE: Good riddance. The man was a completely incompetent thief. His pickpocketing was painfully obvious—like a dancer who can't find the beat.
SLICER: The real trick is swiping the purse on the downbeat.
FLINT: "Acquire," gentlemen. The learned call it "acquiring." "Steal" is such an ugly word!
STONE: Regardless, my purse is totally empty.
FLINT: Guess we starve, then.
STONE: No. I'll have to cheat. I need a scheme.
FLINT: Wolves gotta eat.
STONE: Do any of you know Mercer? The local fellow?
FLINT: I know him. Guy's loaded.
STONE: Well, boys, let me tell you what I'm planning.
FLINT: A massive dinner?
STONE: Quiet, Flint. Yes, I have a massive gut, but I don't have time for belly-laughs. I need cash. Long story short, I’m going to seduce Mercer’s wife. I see an opening. She chats, she smiles, she lingers just a little too long. I can read between the lines of her bold behavior, and the absolute loudest thing she's broadcasting is, "I belong to Captain Stone."
FLINT: He's decoded her virtue and translated it into plain English.
SLICER: The plot thickens. Think it'll work?
STONE: Word on the street is she controls every single penny of her husband’s money. And he has a mountain of gold.
FLINT: Go get 'em, soldier.
SLICER: I like where this is going. Go get us that gold.
STONE: I’ve already written her a letter. And I wrote another one to Sterling’s wife, who actually gave me the eye today, too. She gave my physique a very thorough look. One second she's admiring my boots, and the next, she's eyeing my majestic stomach.
FLINT: Like the golden sun beaming down on a pile of manure.
SLICER:

VALENTINE: So you're telling me a fool and his money are never truly parted?
TRANIO: Exactly, sir. The gold merely leaves his pocket to buy a looking-glass—where he immediately discovers an even bigger fool.
VALENTINE: That's a terribly flimsy excuse for going broke.
TRANIO: Let me fix it, then. The glass breaks, the money is gone, and the fool is left with absolutely nothing but his own reflections. Which, frankly, will be worthless until the end of time.
VALENTINE: A shattered conclusion if I ever heard one. Wait—hold on. Who is this coming toward us? She looks furious.
(Enter Isabella and Beatrice)
ISABELLA: Yes, go ahead, Valentine. Look away. Act like you don't know me. I suppose some new mistress has caught your eye today? I am not Isabella anymore. I'm certainly not your wife.
There was a time when you used to swear—without my having to ask!—that no music sounded as sweet as my voice. That no sight was as lovely as my face, and no touch as warm as mine. Unless I spoke, or smiled, or sat beside you, the world was nothing to you.
How is it now, my husband? How can it be that you are so completely alienated from yourself? And I do call it yourself, even though you treat me like a stranger. Because we are indivisible. We are one. I am the better part of you.
Don't tear yourself away from me. Listen to me: you might as well drop a cup of wine into the ocean and try to scoop that exact vintage back out—without a single drop of saltwater in it—as try to separate your life from mine.
Think about it. How deeply would it cut you if you heard I was being unfaithful? If this body, which belongs entirely to you, had been contaminated by some stranger's lust? Wouldn't you spit on me? Wouldn't you throw the title of 'husband' back in my face, strip the clothes off my back, and smash our wedding ring against the stones?
I know you would. So do it.
Because I am tainted. I'm stained by your sins. If we are one, and you play false, then I am the one digesting the poison of your affairs. Your contagion makes me a whore. Keep your vows. Come back to your true bed. Otherwise, I live stained, and you live dishonored.
VALENTINE: Are you... talking to me, lady? I don't know you. I've been in Mantua for exactly two hours. I'm as unfamiliar with your city as I am with your conversation. I'm listening to every word you say, and I don't understand a single one of them.
BEATRICE: Shame on you, brother. How can you change so quickly? When did you ever treat my sister like this? She sent Tranio to fetch you home for the feast.
VALENTINE: Tranio?
TRANIO: Me?
ISABELLA: Yes, you. And you came back with a miserable report. You claimed he beat you, and between the blows, he swore this wasn't his house and I wasn't his wife.
VALENTINE: Did you speak with this woman, sir? What kind of scam are you running here?
TRANIO: Me? Sir, I have never seen her in my life!
VALENTINE: You liar. You shouted those exact words at me in the market square not ten minutes ago!
TRANIO: I've never spoken to her!
VALENTINE: Then how does she know our names? Unless it's witchcraft.
ISABELLA: It is so beneath you to play these games with your servant. Encouraging him to lie just to get under my skin. Even if you're wronging me, I can't leave you—so don't add insult to injury.
Come on. I'm holding onto your sleeve and I'm not letting go. You are the oak, my husband, and I am the vine. I draw my strength and life from your roots. If anything distracts you from me, it's just rot. It's choking ivy that needs to be pruned before it infects your sap and destroys you.
VALENTINE: (Aside) She's looking right at me. I'm the center of this entire story. Did I marry this woman in my sleep? Am I asleep right now? What is wrong with my eyes and ears? ...You know what? Until I figure out what's going on, I'll just play along with the illusion.
BEATRICE: Tranio, run ahead and tell them to pour the wine.
TRANIO: Someone get a priest! I'm crossing myself, this place is full of witches!

"As much as I esteem your family, my thoughts were entirely consumed by you."
Beatrice was too overwhelmed to speak.
He paused, waiting, before finally adding, "You're too kind to toy with me. If your feelings haven't changed since November, just say so. My own affections are exactly the same. But one word from you, and I will never speak of this again."
The sheer vulnerability in his voice finally pushed Beatrice to answer. Though her words came out a bit tangled, she managed to make him understand that her sentiments had completely shifted. The man she had once rejected was now the only one she wanted; she accepted his offer with absolute gratitude.
The happiness that flooded his face was something he had likely never felt before. He spoke with all the warmth and desperate relief of a man violently in love. Beatrice couldn't quite bring herself to meet his eyes, but she listened. He poured out his heart, making her realize just how thoroughly she had consumed his life, and his affection felt more precious with every step they took.
They kept walking, completely blind to where they were going. There was simply too much to say, too much to feel, to pay attention to the path.
Before long, Beatrice learned the hilarious truth: they owed this entire reunion to his overbearing aunt. Lady Westbourne had marched straight to him upon returning to London, bitterly complaining about Beatrice’s insolence. She recounted their entire argument, emphasizing Beatrice’s stubborn refusal to promise she would never marry him. The old woman had fully expected this to cure him of his infatuation.
Instead, it had done the exact opposite.
"It gave me hope," he admitted, his voice softening. "I barely dared to believe it at first. But I knew your character, Beatrice. I knew that if you were still completely and irrevocably disgusted by me, you would have had no problem telling my aunt exactly that."
Beatrice blushed, though a laugh escaped her. "Well, you certainly know my frankness well enough. After insulting you so horribly to your face, why would I hold back with your aunt?"
"Because you didn't say anything I didn't deserve. Your assumptions might have been wrong, but my behavior that night was inexcusable. I still shudder when I think of it."
"Let's not fight over who was more awful that evening," Beatrice replied gently. "Neither of us behaved perfectly. But I like to think we've both improved since then."
"I can't forgive myself that easily. The way I spoke to you, my arrogance... it's tormented me for months. You told me I hadn't behaved 'as a true gentleman.' I'll never forget that. You can't imagine how much those words stung, even if it took my pride a while to admit you were right."
"I never expected them to leave such a mark. I didn't think you'd care."
"Of course you didn't. You thought I was completely devoid of human feeling. The look on your face when you told me that nothing could ever persuade you to marry me..."
"Oh, please, stop," Beatrice groaned, burying her face in her hands. "Don't repeat it. I've been ashamed of that speech for months."
Carlisle smiled, then quietly brought up the letter he had written her the next day. "Did it help?" he asked. "Did you actually believe anything I wrote when you first read it?"
She finally looked up at him, explaining exactly how his words had struck her, and how, bit by bit, all her angry prejudices had quietly melted away.

"But the whole thing was so careless. So brazen."
Viktor paced the narrow room, his hands gesturing wildly. "If they actually had proof—real, undeniable proof—or even a decent reason to suspect you, they’d have played it close to the chest. They would have searched this rat hole days ago. But they’ve got nothing. Not a single thread. It’s all smoke and mirrors. A floating theory. So they’re trying to rattle you with pure insolence. Maybe Lebedev is just frustrated because he has no evidence, and he let it slip out of anger. Or maybe he’s playing a longer game... he’s a smart bastard, I’ll give him that. Maybe he just wanted to scare you into thinking he knows everything. Cops have their own twisted psychology, my friend. But it’s sickening just thinking about it. Enough!"
He stopped and threw his hands up. "It’s degrading! I get it. But... look, since we’re finally laying our cards on the table (and thank God we are, it’s a relief), I’ll admit I noticed this undercurrent with them days ago. Just a whisper, of course. An implication. But how do they even have the nerve? What are they basing it on? God, you have no idea how furious I’ve been."
Viktor leaned in, his voice rising. "Just think about it! A starving clerk, half-mad from nervous exhaustion, practically delirious with a fever. A guy who is sensitive, proud, totally isolated—who hasn't had a real conversation in three months. He walks into a precinct wearing shoes with no soles and a coat falling apart at the seams. He has to deal with the sneers of petty officials. Then, boom—a demand for back rent shoved in his face. The landlord screaming. The stink of fresh varnish, the suffocating heat of the station, a crowd pressing in, and suddenly they’re gossiping about the violent robbery of a merchant he’d just visited that morning. All on an empty stomach! Of course he fainted! And that—that one moment of physical weakness—is their entire case! Damn them. If I were you, Leonid, I’d laugh in their faces. I’d spit at them. I’d strike back so fast it would put an end to this whole circus. It's an outrage."
He actually makes a decent argument, Leonid thought.
"Damn them?" Leonid muttered, his voice dripping with bitterness. "What about the summons for tomorrow? Do I really have to go back and justify myself to them again? I’m already sick to my stomach that I lowered myself to drink with that rat Sokolov at the tavern yesterday."
"To hell with that! I’ll go to Lebedev myself. I know the man. I’ll wring the truth right out of him and make him lay his cards on the table! And as for Sokolov..."
Finally, he sees him for what he is, Leonid thought.
"Wait!" Viktor grabbed Leonid’s arm, his eyes widening with sudden realization. "Hold on. You miscalculated. I just figured it out—you're completely wrong! How could that be a trap? You said the question about the shattered mirror was a trap. But if you had done it, would you have ever admitted to seeing the broken glass and the blood? Of course not. You would have played dumb, even if you’d stared right at it. Nobody willingly implicates themselves."
Leonid grimaced. Visible disgust washed over his face. "If I had done it, I absolutely would have said I saw the glass and the room."
"Why? Why testify against yourself?"
"Because only idiots and frightened amateurs deny everything. If a man has even half a brain, he admits to the physical facts that can’t be disproven. But he invents a different excuse for them. He spins the context, introduces some unexpected detail that changes the whole meaning and makes him look innocent. Lebedev would count on me doing exactly that. He’d expect me to claim I saw the mirror just to look honest, and then weave a lie around it."
"But he’d just snap back that the mirror wasn't broken until Tuesday night! That would prove you were there at the exact time of the robbery. He’d catch you on a technicality."
"Exactly," Leonid said softly. "That’s what he was banking on. That I’d be too rushed to think it through. That I'd panic, give the most plausible-sounding lie, and completely forget that the mirror had been whole until Tuesday."
"But how could a man forget something like that?"
"Nothing is easier."

I had my moments of happiness, I suppose—maybe once a week. At first, I was sure I’d have a terrible time convincing you to let me keep my promise to Linton. I’d told him I’d come back the day after we left him, but you stayed in your room the next morning, so I slipped out without any trouble. Silas was busy fixing the lock on the park gate that afternoon, so I snatched the key. I told him how my cousin was sick and couldn't make it to the Grange, and how Papa would never let me go visit him, so I needed his help with the pony. Silas is a big reader and wants to head off and get married soon, so he made me an offer: he’d do whatever I wanted if I brought him books from the library. I gave him some of my own instead, which made him even happier.
"When I got there the second time, Linton was in such high spirits. Zillah, the housekeeper, fixed us a clean room with a roaring fire. She told us that since Joseph was off at a prayer meeting and Hareton was out with his dogs—poaching our pheasants, as I found out later—we could do whatever we liked. She brought us warm wine and gingerbread and seemed incredibly sweet. Linton sat in the big armchair, and I sat in the little rocking chair on the hearth. We laughed and talked so merrily, finding so much to say to each other. We planned out exactly where we’d go and what we’d do once summer arrived. I don’t need to tell you what we said, because you’d just call it silly.
"We almost had a fight, though. He said his idea of heaven was lying on a bank of heather in the middle of the moors all through July, just listening to the bees hum and the larks sing under a steady, cloudless sky. My heaven was rocking in a rustling green tree, with the west wind blowing and bright white clouds racing overhead. I wanted the whole world to be wild and awake—thrushes and blackbirds pouring out music, woods, rushing water, and tall grass undulating like waves. He wanted a quiet ecstasy; I wanted everything to sparkle and dance. I told him his heaven would be half-dead; he told me mine would be exhausting. I said I’d fall asleep in his; he said he couldn't breathe in mine and started getting really snappish. Eventually, we agreed to try them both when the weather turned. We kissed, and we were friends again.
"After we sat still for an hour, I looked at that huge, uncarpeted room and thought how fun it would be to play in if we just pushed the table aside. I asked him to call Zillah in to help us so we could play blindman's-buff—you remember how we used to play, Ellen? He wouldn't do it. He said there was no pleasure in that kind of game, but he did agree to play ball. We found two in a cupboard full of old toys, tops, and shuttlecocks. One was marked 'C' and the other 'H'. I wanted the 'C' because it stood for Catherine, and I thought the 'H' might be for Heathcliff, but the stuffing was coming out of it and he didn't like that one bit. I beat him at the game over and over, and he got cross again, started coughing, and went back to his chair.
"He cheered up by nightfall, though. He was totally charmed by a couple of pretty songs—your songs, Ellen. When I finally had to leave, he begged me to come back the next evening, and I promised I would. Minny and I went flying home, feeling light as air. I dreamt of Wuthering Heights and my sweet, darling cousin until the sun came up.
"I felt a bit sad the next day, partly because you were poorly, and partly because I really wanted Papa to know about my trips and approve of them. But it was a beautiful, moonlit night after tea, and as I rode on, the gloom just melted away."

EDMUND. Our father's blood runs in me no less than in you, though I admit the privilege of birth has placed you first.
ALARIC. Bold words.
EDMUND. Necessary ones.
ALARIC. Do you mean to threaten me?
EDMUND. No. I mean to speak plainly. I am Sir Geoffrey's son as surely as you are, and I will not stand silent while I am treated as though I were less.
MARTIN. [Moving between them.] Gentlemen, for your father's sake, let this quarrel end.
ALARIC. Stand aside.
EDMUND. Not yet. You must hear me first. Our father wished me educated as a gentleman. Instead, I have been kept idle and ignorant while every opportunity was reserved for you. I have tolerated it long enough. Either provide what was promised me, or release the inheritance my father left. I will take my chances elsewhere.
ALARIC. And squander it within a year?
EDMUND. That risk belongs to me.
ALARIC. Very well. I tire of arguing. Leave me.
EDMUND. I seek only fairness.
ALARIC. Go, then—and take your aged companion with you.
MARTIN. A poor reward for faithful service. Your father had kinder words for those who served him.
They exit.
ALARIC. The boy has grown dangerous. Ambition is easier crushed early than late. Stephen!
Enter Stephen.
STEPHEN. My lord?
