ALARIC. Has Victor arrived?
STEPHEN. He waits outside.
ALARIC. Bring him in.
Stephen exits.
Interesting. Tomorrow's tournament may spare me future trouble.
Victor enters.
VICTOR. My lord.
ALARIC. Victor. What news from court?
VICTOR. Nothing new, only familiar stories. The elder duke remains in exile. Several loyal nobles followed him, leaving their lands behind. Those estates now belong to the younger duke.
ALARIC. And the duke's daughter?
VICTOR. Still at court. She and her cousin are inseparable. One would not leave the other.
ALARIC. Where has the exiled duke settled?
VICTOR. Deep in the Greenwood Forest. More young nobles join him each week. By all accounts they live freely there.
ALARIC. You wrestle before the duke tomorrow?
VICTOR. Yes, and that brings me here. Word has reached me that your brother plans to challenge me.
ALARIC. Orlando—always eager to prove himself.
VICTOR. I bear him no ill will. Yet once the match begins, I cannot show mercy without losing reputation. If he enters the contest, he may leave it injured.
ALARIC. I appreciate your warning. I have tried to change his mind, but he listens to no one.
VICTOR. Then the responsibility is his.
ALARIC. Entirely his.
Victor nods. Alaric watches him go, already turning the matter to his own advantage.

SCENE. A market square before the city gates.
Noise within. Bells ringing. Enter ROWE with a band of townsmen.
ROWE. Why linger here? The city's waking at last. Off with you—some to the guildhall, some to the custom house. If you find a ledger, throw it in the fire. Those books have burdened more shoulders than any packhorse.
MARTIN. Captain, I would ask a favor.
ROWE. A favor? Why, man, you make me sound a prince. Speak it.
MARTIN. Only this: let no man be taxed unless he consents to it.
POTTER. [Aside.] Then the treasury shall soon be begging its supper.
COOPER. [Aside.] And the clerks may learn a trade at last.
ROWE. Good. Let it be so. Enough of sealed orders and hidden accounts. If a man has a grievance, let him bring it before his neighbors and speak it plainly.
POTTER. There'll be no end to speaking, then.
ROWE. Better too much speech than too much silence.
A MESSENGER rushes in.
MESSENGER. Captain! We have him. Lord Varney is taken.
Enter HUGH with LORD VARNEY under guard.
ROWE. Bring him forward.
So, my lord. You have ridden through these streets often enough, though never with so many eyes upon you. What answer have you for the farmers whose grain paid your levies? For the families who watched their sons march away to wars that yielded them nothing?
VARNEY. If I am to answer, I ask only to be heard.
ROWE. Heard? The people have listened for years. Now it is your turn.
HUGH. Aye, let him account for the tolls.
MARTIN. And for every new charge that came with them.
ROWE. We were told the kingdom lacked money. Yet the palace grew finer. New monuments rose. New offices appeared. Somehow there was always enough silver for that.
You filled the city with officials, inspectors, secretaries. Men who knew every figure in a ledger and not a single laborer whose life supplied it.
HUGH. True enough.
ROWE. Summonses were sent to poor men who scarcely knew their letters. When they failed to answer in the proper language, they paid fines. When they could not pay, they lost what little they had.
VARNEY. The law cannot govern itself.
COOPER. It governs us well enough.
Laughter among the crowd.
ROWE. Tell me, my lord. When was the last time you worked with your hands?
VARNEY. My office required other duties.
ROWE. Other duties. Your horse wears ornaments worth more than a laborer's yearly earnings.
MARTIN. Some men would be glad merely to own the horse.
VARNEY. You see the trappings and think you know the man beneath them.
ROWE. Then enlighten us.
VARNEY. Gladly.
You speak of tolls. Those tolls repaired bridges after the floods. You speak of levies. The borders did not defend themselves. During the famine in the northern counties, grain was released from my stores. When merchants threatened to abandon the harbor, I persuaded them to remain. Trade continued. The city prospered because of it.
A few in the crowd exchange uncertain looks.
HUGH. He has an answer for everything.
MARTIN. Men like him always do.
VARNEY. Not an answer—facts.
You may condemn me if you wish. But consider what follows if every record is burned and every magistrate driven away. Who settles disputes over land? Who decides inheritance? Who keeps neighbors from settling every quarrel with a club and a knife?
ROWE. You speak as though order and justice were the same thing.
VARNEY. They are not. Yet one seldom survives long without the other.
The crowd grows quieter.
VARNEY. I do not claim perfection. No man in office can. Mistakes were made; some by me. Yet a government is not built in a day, nor repaired by rage alone.
POTTER. There is sense in that.
COOPER. Some sense.
ROWE. Enough.
The crowd falls silent.
You plead your cause skillfully, my lord. Whether skill and innocence are companions is another matter.
VARNEY. Then let the people judge. I ask no privilege beyond a fair hearing.
ROWE. You shall have it. We will hear every charge before sentence is spoken.
HUGH. Come along.
VARNEY. I come willingly. Rumor has had its turn. Let judgment now have its own.
They lead him away. The crowd follows after, arguing among themselves.
Exeunt.

SCENE I. A Physician's Shop.
Enter ADRIAN.
ADRIAN.
No rest tonight. My thoughts drive sleep away.
As I came through the poorer streets, I passed a narrow house whose walls seemed ready to fall about their owner. I remember thinking then that misery makes bargains prosperity would never entertain. If a desperate man required some forbidden thing, there lived one who might provide it.
And now I find myself at his door.
This should be the place.
Ho! Is anyone within?
Enter PHYSICIAN.
PHYSICIAN.
Who calls?
ADRIAN.
A friend, if money can purchase friendship. Come nearer.
I see enough in your face to know your circumstances. Here—take this purse. In return, I ask for a certain remedy.
PHYSICIAN.
What remedy?
ADRIAN.
One that leaves no time for suffering.
PHYSICIAN.
Sir, speak lower.
Such medicines exist, but the law forbids them. Were I discovered selling one, it would cost me my life.
ADRIAN.
And what sort of life is left to you now?
Your coat is worn thin. Hunger has hollowed your cheeks. The law threatens you with death, yet it has done little to preserve your living.
PHYSICIAN.
You press a hard argument.
ADRIAN.
Need presses harder.
Take the gold.
PHYSICIAN.
I do not like this business.
ADRIAN.
Nor I. Yet here we stand.
PHYSICIAN.
Then listen carefully. I have a powder. Mixed into wine or water, it works swiftly. A healthy man would not survive the hour.
ADRIAN.
That is enough.
PHYSICIAN.
May Heaven forgive me.
ADRIAN.
And me as well.
There is your payment. Strange thing, gold. Men condemn poisons and outlaw them, yet this metal has ruined more souls than any apothecary's vial. It has bought betrayals, murders, wars, and false friendships.
Keep it. Buy food. Mend your household.
Farewell.
Exit ADRIAN.
PHYSICIAN.
Poor comfort. Yet poor comfort is sometimes all that poverty receives.
Exit.
SCENE II. A Scholar's Chamber.
Enter BROTHER MATTHIAS carrying a sealed letter.
BROTHER MATTHIAS.
Brother Sebastian!
Enter BROTHER SEBASTIAN.
BROTHER SEBASTIAN.
You have returned at last. Was the message delivered?
BROTHER MATTHIAS.
No.
I had scarcely begun my journey when I was detained outside the western quarter. A sickness had broken out there. The authorities closed the roads and posted guards at every entrance.
BROTHER SEBASTIAN.
For how long?
BROTHER MATTHIAS.
Three days.
No one was permitted to leave. No messenger would accept a letter from our hands. Fear traveled faster than the disease itself.
He gives him the letter.
Here. It remains unopened.
BROTHER SEBASTIAN.
This is grave news.
The contents were urgent. Had they been merely courteous words, the delay would matter little. Instead, every hour lost increases the danger.
BROTHER MATTHIAS.
What is to be done?
BROTHER SEBASTIAN.
We must act without waiting.
Fetch me a lantern. Bring also the iron tools from the storeroom. Quickly.
BROTHER MATTHIAS.
I shall return directly.
Exit.
BROTHER SEBASTIAN.
What cruel fortune chooses its moments so precisely.
Before dawn, the young woman concealed within the chapel will wake. Adrian should already know the truth. Instead he remains ignorant, and ignorance is often more dangerous than malice.
There is no time left for wishing.
I must go myself.
Exit.
SCENE III. A Burial Ground.
Enter LORD RENALD and a PAGE carrying flowers and a lantern.
RENALD.
Give me the lantern.
Now go and wait beneath that elm tree. Stay where you can watch the path. If anyone approaches the tomb, whistle to me at once.
PAGE.
My lord, I would rather be anywhere else than in a churchyard at night.
RENALD.
You are not the first to feel so.
Take courage and do as I ask.
PAGE.
I will.
He retires.
RENALD.
Isabel.
I bring flowers again, though they are poor substitutes for the life that should have been yours. This stone receives them gladly enough, but it returns nothing.
Every night I come here. Every night I tell myself it eases the grief.
It does not.
Still I return.
He lays down the flowers.
The earth has claimed too fair a treasure. Had fate shown a little mercy, these blossoms would have adorned a wedding feast rather than a grave.
The PAGE whistles.
RENALD.
Someone comes.
Either a mourner or a wanderer. I would know which.
He withdraws into the shadows.
Exeunt.

SCENE V. A blasted moor. Thunder. Enter three ENCHANTRESSES meeting MORWENA.
FIRST ENCHANTRESS.
Mistress, you are angered.
MORWENA.
Would you have me otherwise?
You have been whispering in Lord Arcturus' ear again, filling his mind with riddles and promises, and never once thought to seek my counsel. Crowns, bloodshed, succession—these are not trifles to be played with.
SECOND ENCHANTRESS.
We meant only to guide him.
MORWENA.
Guide him? You flatter yourselves.
The man cares nothing for you. He listens because your words feed his ambition. Give him a throne and he would forget every hand that helped him climb to it.
THIRD ENCHANTRESS.
What would you have done?
MORWENA.
What should have been done.
Tomorrow, before sunrise, meet me at the old well beneath the blackthorn trees. He will come there. His appetite for prophecy grows by the hour. One answer breeds two questions, and every question drives him further on.
Bring what is needed. Roots, oils, charms—whatever remains from the last working.
As for me, I have another task.
There drifts tonight above the western hills a wandering spirit born of fear and longing. Before dawn I shall bind it and give it voice. It will tell Arcturus exactly what he wishes to hear.
That is the surest road to ruin.
Let a man believe himself untouchable and he begins to mistake recklessness for courage. He stops listening. He ceases to doubt. Soon enough he digs the pit into which he falls.
Music within.
There. The summons comes.
My little servant waits among the marsh-fog. I must not keep it long.
Exit.
FIRST ENCHANTRESS.
Come quickly. We have gathering to do.
Exeunt.
SCENE VI. A chamber in the royal fortress.
Enter VARREN and a NOBLE.
VARREN.
The kingdom feels different now.
NOBLE.
It does.
VARREN.
Not long ago men spoke openly. Now they lower their voices before they finish a sentence.
NOBLE.
Fear teaches caution.
VARREN.
A costly lesson.
The king's death still troubles me. Lord Arcturus mourned him publicly and inherited much from the mourning. The commander died soon after. Then the commander's son vanished. Every event seems to point in one direction, though no man dares say where.
NOBLE.
Some things are dangerous even to suspect.
VARREN.
True.
Yet silence has become dangerous too.
NOBLE.
Have you heard from Lord Cedric?
VARREN.
Nothing certain.
NOBLE.
Then I shall tell you. He has crossed the sea and joined the prince.
VARREN.
The prince still lives?
NOBLE.
So all reports agree. He resides under the protection of a neighboring king, who has received him with honor.
VARREN.
That is welcome news.
NOBLE.
Better still, Cedric seeks support for a return. There are noblemen abroad who have not forgotten their loyalties.
VARREN.
Nor, I think, those at home.
NOBLE.
No. Though many keep their thoughts hidden.
A pause.
I think people desire simple things now. Peaceful meals. Undisturbed sleep. The ability to speak honestly without first looking over a shoulder.
VARREN.
Things once taken for granted.
NOBLE.
Exactly.
VARREN.
And Arcturus?
NOBLE.
He prepares. Soldiers are being called in from distant regions. Couriers ride day and night.
VARREN.
Then he knows.
NOBLE.
Or fears.
VARREN.
Was Cedric ordered to return?
NOBLE.
Yes.
VARREN.
And refused?
NOBLE.
Without hesitation. The messenger came back displeased and not a little frightened. Whatever words passed between them, they were enough to darken the man's countenance.
VARREN.
Then events move quickly.
NOBLE.
They do.
VARREN.
May Heaven protect the prince. The land has suffered under too dark a cloud already.
NOBLE.
I shall pray for the same.
Exeunt.
ACT IV
SCENE I. A cavern beneath a ruined tower. A brazier burns in the center. Thunder. Enter the three ENCHANTRESSES.
FIRST ENCHANTRESS.
Twice hath the raven sounded from the crag.
SECOND ENCHANTRESS.
And twice the marsh-hound answered through the mist.
THIRD ENCHANTRESS.
Hush now. The hour approaches.

SCENE III. Within the fortress.

Enter LORD VAREK, a PHYSICIAN, attendants.

VAREK.
Enough. Take your medicines elsewhere. I am weary of hearing what cannot be cured.

My sword. Where is it? And the armor.

To an attendant.

See that the watch remains alert. No gate is to be left unwatched tonight.

PHYSICIAN.
My lord, if I might—

VAREK.
You might do many things, if skill were equal to hope.

Men praise physicians for restoring sick bodies. Yet look beyond these walls. There is a nation wasting away. Distrust in every province. Fear in every household.

If you could diagnose that illness and draw it out by the root, I would honor you more than any general.

PHYSICIAN.
The reports from the countryside grow worse.

VAREK.
Reports always grow worse.

Yesterday it was famine. Last week rebellion. Before that, plague. The kingdom survives them all.

Still, bring my armor after me.

Exit VAREK with attendants.

PHYSICIAN.
The kingdom may survive. Whether its ruler does is another matter.

Exit.

SCENE IV. Open country before the fortress.

Enter PRINCE ALDRIC, GENERAL ROWAN, LORD EDRIC, LORD MARSH, and Soldiers.

ALDRIC.
We are close now. Closer than we have ever been.

ROWAN.
The men can feel it.

MARSH.
What woodland lies ahead?

EDRIC.
Ashen Hill Wood.

ALDRIC.
Good.

Let every soldier cut a branch and carry it before him. From the walls they will see a forest where an army stands. Let uncertainty do part of our work for us.

SOLDIERS.
We shall.

ROWAN.
The latest scouts agree on one point: Varek remains inside the fortress.

ALDRIC.
Where else should he be?

He has lost too many allies to trust the open field. Men continue to serve him, but not with their hearts. Some obey from fear, others because they see no safe path away.

MARSH.
A ruler may command hands long after he has lost loyalty.

EDRIC.
Before long the matter leaves the realm of opinion. Battle settles arguments that words only prolong.

ROWAN.
Then let us advance and learn the truth of it.

Exeunt marching.

SCENE V. Within the fortress.

Enter VAREK, CAPTAIN, Soldiers.

VAREK.
Hang every banner.

If they mean to besiege us, let them look upon our colors and know we remain unbroken.

The cry never changes. "They come." Always they come.

Let them.

Stone walls do not frighten easily. Hunger and weather are better allies than panic.

Had certain men remembered their oaths, we would have met this army in the field and driven it back before it reached our borders.

A cry is heard within.

What was that?

CAPTAIN.
The women, my lord.

Exit CAPTAIN.

VAREK.
The women.

There was a time such a sound would have chilled me.

A scream in the dark. A tale of murder. The rumor of some disaster waiting beyond a door. Once my imagination did half the work for fear.

Now it grows tired.

I have watched too many calamities arrive. After a while they cease to surprise.

Re-enter CAPTAIN.

Why do they cry?

CAPTAIN.
Your sister has died, my lord.

A pause.

VAREK.
Has she?

Another pause.

Poor soul.

Yet death has been circling this house so long that I scarcely know how to greet the news. There should be time to mourn. Time to sit with sorrow and give it its due.

But the world rarely waits for proper moments.

A life ends. Another day begins. The wheel turns.

We spend our years pursuing victories that fade almost as soon as they are won. We build, destroy, love, hate, remember, forget.

Then someone else inherits the unfinished business.

Enter a SCOUT in haste.

SCOUT.
My lord.

VAREK.
What now?

SCOUT.
I bring strange news.

VAREK.
Those are seldom welcome words.

SCOUT.
I stood watch on the western tower. Looking toward Ashen Hill, I thought at first that the wind had stirred the forest.

Then I looked again.

VAREK.
And?

SCOUT.
The forest was moving.

VAREK.
Take care.

SCOUT.
I speak what I saw. The trees advance across the plain.

Or rather, men concealed beneath them.

Within the hour they may be visible from the walls.

VAREK.
If this is some frightened mistake—

SCOUT.
It is not.

VAREK.
No.

A silence.

No, perhaps it is not.

There are promises that seem solid until the day they are tested. Warnings too. We hear what pleases us and call it certainty.

He turns away.

Sound the alarm.

Let every able man take up arms. Open the armories. Prepare the gates.

Whether fortune favors us or abandons us, the matter approaches quickly now.

CAPTAIN.
At once, my lord.

VAREK.
Come, then. If the end has chosen this day, I would rather meet it awake than hiding from it.

Exeunt.

LEON.
You have twisted my meaning completely.

FELIX.
Then perhaps it was bent to begin with.

LEON.
You never miss a chance.

FELIX.
To improve another man's argument? Rarely.

LEON.
Improve it?

FELIX.
Certainly. I take ordinary nonsense and give it polish.

LEON.
I thought the nonsense was yours.

FELIX.
Only in part. I borrow freely.

MARCUS.
That much is evident.

FELIX.
There speaks a man who contributes little and judges much.

MARCUS.
A comfortable arrangement.

LEON.
Your wit is restless today.

FELIX.
It has had no proper exercise. If I do not let it run about, it becomes destructive.

LEON.
More destructive than usual?

FELIX.
I hope not.

Enter HOUSEKEEPER and SERVANT.

LEON.
Here comes company.

FELIX.
Aye, and with purpose. Casual visitors do not march so directly.

HOUSEKEEPER.
Thomas!

SERVANT.
Madam?

HOUSEKEEPER.
My basket. Have you lost it again?

SERVANT.
No, madam.

FELIX.
A remarkable achievement. Let it be recorded.

HOUSEKEEPER.
Good day, gentlemen.

FELIX.
A very good day, if appearances may be trusted.

HOUSEKEEPER.
And if they may not?

FELIX.
Then we are all deceived together.

HOUSEKEEPER.
You have an answer for everything.

FELIX.
Not at all. Only for things I do not understand.

LEON.
Which explains the quantity.

HOUSEKEEPER.
Can someone tell me where I might find Master Leon?

LEON.
You have found him already, though perhaps not improved by the discovery.

HOUSEKEEPER.
That remains to be seen.

FELIX.
A cautious judgment. I approve.

HOUSEKEEPER.
Sir, I would have a private word.

MARCUS.
Now there is a phrase that always awakens curiosity.

FELIX.
Or anxiety.

LEON.
Usually in those not invited to hear it.

FELIX.
A cruel custom.

HOUSEKEEPER.
Will you leave us, then?

FELIX.
Since I am plainly unwanted, I shall preserve what remains of my dignity and go willingly.

MARCUS.
You cannot preserve what was never there.

FELIX.
A painful farewell gift.

Come, Marcus. Let us find food before philosophy becomes necessary.

MARCUS.
Lead on.

FELIX.
Madam, farewell. Thomas, keep watch over the basket. It appears to depend upon you.

Exeunt FELIX and MARCUS.

HOUSEKEEPER.
Who is that fellow?

LEON.
A good friend, though not always at convenient times.

HOUSEKEEPER.
He speaks enough for three men.

LEON.
And listens for half of one.

HOUSEKEEPER.
Had he continued much longer, I should have put him in his place.

SERVANT.
I would have assisted.

HOUSEKEEPER.
You?

SERVANT.
Certainly.

HOUSEKEEPER.
You never assist until the matter is over.

SERVANT.
That is unfortunate timing, not lack of courage.

HOUSEKEEPER.
Keep telling yourself so.

SERVANT.
I do, madam. It is a great comfort.

A herald is heard offstage.
HERALD. Room there! The challengers come to the field.
A Knight enters with his Squire.
KNIGHT. Friend, let the king know I am arrived.
HERALD. State your name and country.
KNIGHT. A nobleman of Macedon. I have crossed many seas to seek honour in this court and to offer my service before the princess.
HERALD. Wait here and be presented.
Enter King Leontes, Princess Helena, with Lords and attendants.
LEONTES. Who comes before us?
KNIGHT. My liege, fortune has dealt unevenly with me, yet she has not robbed me of courage. News of your daughter's grace drew me here, and I ask leave to compete among the other knights.
HELENA. You speak with good manners, sir. Tell us, what sign is carried on your shield?
SQUIRE. A silver falcon rising through the clouds toward a single star. The motto reads, Spes super astra.
LEONTES. An ambitious image. It suggests a man who aims beyond the limits of his station.
KNIGHT. Perhaps so, my lord. Wealth is uncertain, but honour belongs to any man willing to earn it. Should I succeed, let my deeds speak; should I fail, I shall regret nothing.
LEONTES. A measured answer. Confidence sits better when it is not dressed as pride.
FIRST LORD. Sire, the remaining competitors are ready.
LEONTES. Then there is no cause to delay. Let the trial begin.
Trumpets sound.
HELENA. I often think these contests reveal more than strength. A person's nature is seen most clearly when something important is at stake.
LEONTES. There is wisdom in that. Gold is tested by fire, and character by challenge.
HERALD. Open the lists!
The knights move toward the field.
KNIGHT. Whatever happens today, I count myself fortunate to have stood among such company. Even the attempt carries its own reward.
LEONTES. Go forward, then. May honour find its rightful owner.
They exit toward the tournament grounds.

Enter Lord Aurelian and Sir Edmund.
AURELIAN. Has the king stirred yet?
EDMUND. Not that I know of, my lord. He asked me to attend him early, and I fear I am close to missing the appointed hour.
AURELIAN. Come then. I’ll take you to his chamber.
EDMUND. A pleasant duty, though duties seldom come without trouble.
AURELIAN. Trouble weighs little when the heart is willing. The door is here.
EDMUND. I’ll announce myself.
Exit Edmund.
RICHARD. Does His Majesty leave today?
AURELIAN. That was his intention.
RICHARD. The night gave ill signs of it. The wind shook the walls, and more than one man claimed to hear cries carried through the darkness. Even the dogs would not rest. Before sunrise the ravens circled the courtyard in great numbers.
AURELIAN. A bitter night, certainly.
RICHARD. Stranger than any I remember.
Edmund returns suddenly.
EDMUND. Horror! O God, what horror!
AURELIAN. What has happened?
RICHARD. Speak!
EDMUND. Wickedness has done its worst. The king is struck down. The crown itself has been assaulted.
AURELIAN. What are you saying?
EDMUND. Look for yourselves. I cannot tell it properly.
Aurelian and Richard hurry away.
Awake! Awake! Ring the bell! Treason and murder! Princes, rise! Throw off your sleep and see what dreadful thing has been done!
A bell sounds throughout the castle.
Enter Queen Eleanor.
ELEANOR. What alarm is this? Why is the house in such confusion?
EDMUND. Madam, the tale is too dreadful to repeat.
Enter Lord Stanley.
STANLEY. What misfortune stirs everyone so?
EDMUND. Our king has been murdered.
ELEANOR. Murdered?
STANLEY. No. Let that not be true.
Aurelian and Richard return with several lords.
AURELIAN. Had my life ended an hour ago, I should have died happier than I stand now. Everything seems diminished after this.
Enter Prince Henry and Prince Edward.
HENRY. What has occurred?
AURELIAN. The man who gave you life is gone.
EDMUND. Your father has been slain.
HENRY. By whom?
RICHARD. Suspicion falls upon those who guarded his chamber. They were covered in blood, and bloody daggers lay beside them.
AURELIAN. I killed them in my fury.
EDMUND. Before they were questioned?
AURELIAN. Yes. I saw the king's wounds and lost command of myself. Grief moved faster than judgment.
ELEANOR. I cannot bear this.
STANLEY. Help the queen.
HENRY. We speak too little when the matter concerns us most.
EDWARD. And perhaps too openly. If this plot reaches the throne, who can say where it ends?
STANLEY. Let us gather ourselves before we act. Fear makes poor counsel.
HENRY. Yet the truth must be found.
STANLEY. It shall be. Until then, I trust in Heaven and keep watch against whatever treason still hides in shadow.
Exeunt.

The second Knight passes, and his Squire presents a shield before Helena.
LEONTES. Who offers himself next?
HELENA. A duke from Sicily, my lord.
His shield bears an eagle climbing through dark clouds toward the sun.
The motto reads, Virtus superat fata.
LEONTES. A bold sentiment. Some men would rather challenge fate than submit to it.
A third Knight approaches.
LEONTES. And this one?
HELENA. A Venetian nobleman.
Upon his shield grows a laurel from bare stone.
His word is Per dura ad gloriam.
LEONTES. Glory won through hardship. An old lesson, but a worthy one.
The fourth Knight presents his device.
HELENA. A shattered sword bound together with golden cords.
The motto, Fides reparat.
LEONTES. Faith and loyalty often repair what strength cannot.
The fifth Knight advances.
HELENA. He bears a vessel crossing rough seas.
Below it stands the phrase Spem sequor.
LEONTES. Hope has carried many safely through greater storms.
The last Knight enters without attendant or display. His armour is worn and dull with age.
LEONTES. And what of this final challenger?
HELENA. His shield shows a flower breaking through snow.
The words are Dum vivo spero.
LEONTES. Plain enough, yet full of meaning. A man brought low still believes better days may come.
FIRST LORD. His appearance gives little cause for confidence.
SECOND LORD. Appearances have deceived wiser men than us.
THIRD LORD. Perhaps the rust is intentional. Victory might shine brighter beside it.
LEONTES. We are all too eager to read character from clothing. Let us wait for proof instead.
Shouts erupt offstage.
VOICES. The Stranger Knight! The Stranger Knight!
LEONTES. It seems the field has already given its answer.
Exeunt.

SCENE III. A Hall Prepared for Feasting
Enter King Leontes, Helena, Lords, Attendants, and Knights.
LEONTES. Noble knights, welcome.
Yet your actions have welcomed you better than any words I could offer.
The day calls for celebration, not ceremony.
Take your places and enjoy what has been prepared.
HELENA. And you, sir,
Accept this wreath.
The honour belongs to you.
STRANGER KNIGHT. Lady, I fear fortune deserves more credit than I do.
LEONTES. Fortune may help, but she rarely wins tournaments alone.
Today is yours.
HELENA places the wreath upon him.
LEONTES. Daughter, sit beside me and guide the rest to their seats.
KNIGHTS. Your kindness honours us.
LEONTES. The honour is shared.
MARSHAL. Sir, this place has been reserved for you.
STRANGER KNIGHT. There are worthier men present.
FIRST KNIGHT. We disagree.
No gentleman should envy another's success, especially when it has been fairly earned.
STRANGER KNIGHT. Then I thank you for your courtesy.
He sits.
LEONTES. Aside.
My daughter hardly touches her meal.
The victor occupies more of her attention than the feast.
HELENA. Aside.
Nothing on the table pleases me.
My thoughts remain fixed elsewhere.
There is something rare about this man.
LEONTES. Aside.
A capable knight, certainly, but no miracle.
Young hearts are quick to exaggerate.
HELENA. Aside.
The difference seems greater to me.
The others shine, yet he draws the eye without trying.
STRANGER KNIGHT. Aside.
The king reminds me of my father as he once stood:
powerful, admired, surrounded by loyal nobles.
Time has altered everything.
Where he blazed like the sun, I wander as a small light in darkness.
So it goes with men. Time raises them high, then lowers them again, caring little for what they desire.
The feast continues.

BALTHAZAR. He neither hears nor stirs. Love has carried him so far from ordinary company that I doubt he remembers we exist.
LUCIUS. Then leave him to his dreaming.
BALTHAZAR. One call more. By Celia's bright eyes, by the sweetness of her smile, by every charm she possesses, I summon him forth.
LUCIUS. You'll earn his displeasure if he catches you.
BALTHAZAR. Hardly. Lovers forgive much when their lady's name is involved.
LUCIUS. There, among the vines. He's hidden himself away again.
BALTHAZAR. The night is a faithful friend to lovers.
LUCIUS. And an unfaithful one to common sense.
BALTHAZAR. Come. We shall find warmer beds than this garden path.
They leave.
Exeunt.
SCENE II. The Garden
Enter ADRIAN alone.
ADRIAN. Easy enough to mock a wound you've never felt.
A light appears at an upper window. CELIA steps forward.
What light is that?
The whole garden seems altered by it.
The moon, which a moment ago ruled the sky, now looks pale and unnecessary.
There she stands.
If those stars above exchanged places with her eyes, I think the heavens themselves would shine no brighter.
Even the birds might mistake this night for morning.
She leans upon her hand.
To be that hand's companion for a moment—what fortune that would be.
CELIA. Ah me.
ADRIAN. At last, her voice.
CELIA. Why should the person dearest to me carry a name that stands against my own house?
If he were called something else, would anything important change?
Would his heart be different?
His nature?
Names seem powerful, yet they are only sounds.
A rose would remain a rose regardless of what people chose to call it.
Its fragrance would not fade.
Neither would his worth.
ADRIAN. Aside.
Do I wait, or answer her?
CELIA. If he would abandon that single obstacle, I could give him everything.
ADRIAN. Then take me at my word.
Give me another name, and I shall wear it gladly.
CELIA. Who is there?
ADRIAN. A man who has grown tired of hearing his own name spoken as your enemy.
If I could erase it with a stroke, I would.
CELIA. I have not heard many words, yet I know that voice.
It is Adrian.
ADRIAN. Then I am discovered—and fortunate to be so.
They gaze at one another as the night grows quiet.

PISTOL.
Rot thee and all thy counsel! Keep thy friendship for fools.
FLUELLEN.
So be it. I have heard thee.
PISTOL.
A fig of Spain for thee and all thy discipline!
[Exit.]
FLUELLEN.
He is gone off in heat.
GOWER.
He is no soldier. I have seen such men before—vagabonds that creep into wars to clothe themselves in borrowed honor. I know him now well enough: a thief of reputation more than of coin.
FLUELLEN.
He spoke bravely enough upon the bridge, I will say that. Yet a brave tongue is not always a brave heart.
GOWER.
No. These fellows learn the names of captains as one learns a song, then sing it in alehouses afterward, as though they had stood where steel was crossing steel.
FLUELLEN.
If he returns my way, I shall look more closely at his courage. Words are easy things to wear.
[Drums within.]
GOWER.
The King comes on.
FLUELLEN.
From the bridge, is it?
GOWER.
Aye.
Enter KING HENRY, GLOUCESTER, and SOLDIERS.
FLUELLEN.
God save your Majesty. The bridge holds well.
KING HENRY.
Tell me then, Fluellen, how goes the field?
FLUELLEN.
In truth, sir, the enemy has drawn back somewhat disorderly. Our men stood firm. Exeter bore himself like a man born to command.
KING HENRY.
Losses?
FLUELLEN.
Not many. A few wounded, nothing that touches the chief strength of your army.
There is one matter I must report, though.
KING HENRY.
Speak it.
FLUELLEN.
A soldier named Bardolph was taken in theft against a church. He has been executed according to the law of war.
KING HENRY.
So.
FLUELLEN.
A man with a face all inflamed and broken-looking, as though fire had once lived in him and burned itself out.
KING HENRY.
Let it be proclaimed through the camp: no plunder, no violence against the townsfolk. All must be paid for. Discipline first, always.
FLUELLEN.
It shall be done.
[A tucket sounds.]
Enter MONTJOY.
MONTJOY.
I bring a message from the French King.
KING HENRY.
We know thee. Speak.
MONTJOY.
He says this: though fortune has gone against him in the field, the war is not yet settled. He bids you weigh your losses and consider whether further resistance is worth the cost.
He offers terms for your withdrawal and the return of your men.
KING HENRY.
And that is all?
MONTJOY.
He warns that your strength will diminish if you continue, and urges you to end matters before greater harm follows.
KING HENRY.
What does he think remains of us?
MONTJOY.
He believes you weakened, though still dangerous.
KING HENRY.
Then return and tell him he miscounts us. We are fewer perhaps, but not less resolved.
England has not yet spent itself, and what remains is not for bargaining under pressure.
Say to him also: we will answer his proposals in our own time.
MONTJOY.
I shall carry it.
KING HENRY.
Go.
[Exit MONTJOY.]
GLOUCESTER.
Your Majesty holds firm.
KING HENRY.
There is little choice in it. One either stands, or yields.
Come. The day is not finished yet, and neither is the work before us.
[Exeunt.]

SERVANT TO AURELIUS.
You speak more wisely than you seem.
FOOL.
And you seem wiser than you are. I call that an uneven trade.
SERVANT.
For a fool, you move quickly in reply.
FOOL.
Slowness is a luxury for those who can afford better arguments.
SERVANT.
Here comes Lord Aurelius.
Enter AURELIUS and MARCUS.
MARCUS.
Stay near him a while. I would have speech with you when I can.
FOOL.
I prefer standing near nothing at all. Less chance of error.
SERVANT.
And I prefer stepping aside before I am asked to stand in judgment.
[Exeunt Servants.]
AURELIUS.
I cannot put my mind to rest on this matter.
Why was my estate never set plainly before me? I might have governed my spending differently had I known the truth in time.
Instead I was led to believe I had more beneath me than I truly did.
MARCUS.
My lord, I spoke of it more than once. I set the accounts before you when there was still room to act upon them.
AURELIUS.
You did speak, yes. But not in the moment it might have mattered most. Or so it seemed then.
There were times I would not hear it, other times I put it aside, thinking it could wait.
MARCUS.
I brought you the reckoning itself. Not once, but many times. You dismissed it as caution or alarm, not fact.
When I urged restraint, you thought it resistance to your will.
AURELIUS.
And now restraint arrives too late to be of use.
MARCUS.
I am afraid so, my lord.
AURELIUS.
So the foundation was thinner than I was told.
MARCUS.
Thinner, or more strained than it appeared. Some lands are gone, others pledged. The rest can barely meet what is already owed.
AURELIUS.
Then there is no remainder worth speaking of.
MARCUS.
There is little that can be turned into coin.
AURELIUS.
Strange. I was thought to be rich.
MARCUS.
Riches that are spent freely often remain only in memory.
AURELIUS.
So I arrive at poverty without noticing the road.
MARCUS.
It rarely announces itself.
AURELIUS.
What would you have me do now?
MARCUS.
Face it as it stands. And consider those who depend upon you still.
AURELIUS.
Even now you ask me to pause.
MARCUS.
Because there is nothing gained by rushing further.
AURELIUS.
Pause has become a word that always arrives after damage is done.
MARCUS.
It is still the only word left that can change direction.
Enter MESSENGER.
MESSENGER.
My lord, the men to whom you owe money demand immediate payment.
AURELIUS.
Tell them I am in debt even to delay.
MESSENGER.
They speak of legal action.
AURELIUS.
Let them speak. Speech is cheaper than settlement.
MESSENGER.
They expect what is owed.
AURELIUS.
And I expect what I no longer have.
[Exit MESSENGER.]
AURELIUS.
So it narrows to this.
I did not think myself careless, only fortunate. Men around me spoke as if fortune were stable, as if wealth stood still once gained.
It did not.
MARCUS.
Nothing does.
AURELIUS.
No, I see that now. Too clearly.
A silence.
If I was misled, it was not by silence but by comfort. Everyone spoke well while there was something to gain by it.
MARCUS.
There is still time to repair part of it.
AURELIUS.
Repair with what? The structure is already half gone.
MARCUS.
With what remains. Small things can still hold weight if placed carefully.
AURELIUS.
Then we begin with what is left, however small it is.
MARCUS.
That is all I ask.
AURELIUS.
Begin, then. And be careful how you set each stone.
[Exeunt.]

SCENE I. A hall of judgement.
HERALD.
The matter is resolved by royal decree. No further appeal shall be heard.
SOMERSET.
I accept the King’s sentence.
HORNER.
And I accept the combat laid upon me.
PETER.
My lord, I cannot fight. I am no man for such work. Have mercy, I beg you. My strength fails me before I even begin. I am undone by fear alone.
GLOUCESTER.
Fight, or answer with your life.
KING HENRY.
To prison with them. The day of combat shall fall upon the last day of the coming month.
Somerset, you shall be removed from hence.
[Flourish. Exeunt.]

SCENE II. A garden within Gloucester’s house. Night.
Enter MARGERY, HUME, SOUTHWELL, and BOLINGBROKE.
HUME.
The duchess waits. There is no turning back from what was promised.
BOLINGBROKE.
Everything is prepared. The work may proceed at once.
HUME.
She will be present to witness it?
BOLINGBROKE.
She will. Though I see no cause for doubt in her resolve.
HUME.
Then I will not remain. I prefer to be elsewhere when these matters begin.
[Exit Hume.]
BOLINGBROKE.
Mother Jourdain, take your place. Southwell, read when the circle is set.
JOURDAIN.
Do not delay on my account. I am ready.
SOUTHWELL.
Night itself seems made for such proceedings.
BOLINGBROKE.
Set the circle close. Nothing must pass its bounds.
[The Duchess appears above; Hume follows unseen.]
DUCHESS.
So this is the work you hide yourselves for.
SOUTHWELL.
Be steady. Begin.
[Ceremonies performed. Thunder.]
SPIRIT.
I am here. Speak.
JOURDAIN.
By the power invoked, you will answer truthfully.
SPIRIT.
Ask.
BOLINGBROKE.
What becomes of the King?
SPIRIT.
He shall be overthrown by one who yet lives, and shall not long remain after.
SOUTHWELL.
And Suffolk?
SPIRIT.
He dies upon the water.
BOLINGBROKE.
And Somerset?
SPIRIT.
He shall trust no fortress. The open field is safer for him than stone walls.
SOUTHWELL.
That is enough.
DUCHESS.
I will hear more.
SPIRIT.
No more is permitted.
[Thunder. The spirit disappears.]
HUME.
We are known. This place is no longer secure.
BOLINGBROKE.
End it. Now.

SCENE III. The same garden. Enter YORK and BUCKINGHAM with guards.
YORK.
Seize them.
BUCKINGHAM.
So this is the practice—hidden dealings within the court.
YORK.
Madam, you are taken in plain discovery.
DUCHESS.
You call it discovery. I call it inquiry.
BUCKINGHAM.
Take them away.
YORK.
Search every corner. Nothing is to be left behind.
[They gather writings.]
BUCKINGHAM.
These papers here.
YORK.
Read them.
BUCKINGHAM.
Prophecies touching the crown… and the King’s fall.
YORK.
Then the intent is clear enough.
DUCHESS.
Clear only to those who choose their reading.
YORK.
We read what stands before us.
BUCKINGHAM.
Remove them.
[Exeunt guards with prisoners.]
YORK.
This night has drawn much into the open.
BUCKINGHAM.
And still leaves much uncertain. The words are dangerous, though unclear.
YORK.
Dangerous enough.
BUCKINGHAM.
Especially where belief begins to follow them.
YORK.
Come then. We must lay this before the King.
[Exeunt.]

GENERAL. If you had witnessed what he accomplished—
VALERIUS. No more of that, I ask you.
When my own family praises me,
I feel less pride than discomfort.
I have only done what duty demanded.
Any man who wished as earnestly for victory
Has earned as much credit as I.
GENERAL. That is too severe a judgment.
The realm ought to know who secured this triumph.
To conceal worthy deeds serves no one.
Let me speak of them before the army,
Not as payment, but as simple truth.
VALERIUS. My wounds complain loudly enough.
They have no need of public remembrance.
GENERAL. Then perhaps they should complain of neglect instead.
Among all the wealth captured today—
horses, coin, and spoils of war—
you may choose a tenth share before any division is made.
VALERIUS. I am grateful, but I cannot accept.
The service of a soldier should not be bought afterward.
I ask only for the portion granted to every man who fought.
The soldiers erupt in cheers.
SOLDIERS. Valerius! Valerius!
VALERIUS. Enough.
You honour me beyond reason.
I happened to stand where danger was greatest;
that is all.
Many others showed equal courage and receive no such noise.
GENERAL. You are harder on yourself than any critic could be.
Still, whether you like it or not,
your actions deserve acknowledgment.
Before all present,
I give you my own warhorse,
known throughout the army.
And for your victory at Blackstone Gate,
let that place become part of your name.
Henceforth, be called Valerius Blackstone.
Trumpets sound.
ALL. Valerius Blackstone!
BLACKSTONE. Then I shall try to make the title worthy of itself.
GENERAL. Come. Reports must be sent before nightfall.
CAPTAIN. The messengers are ready.
BLACKSTONE. It seems I cannot escape owing a favour after all.
There is one thing I would ask.
GENERAL. Name it.
BLACKSTONE. During the campaign a poor townsman gave me shelter.
I saw him captured in the confusion of battle.
I ask that he be released.
GENERAL. Easily done.
For your sake, I would grant it without hesitation.
CAPTAIN. What is the man's name?
BLACKSTONE. I... cannot recall it.
War has left me more tired than I knew.
GENERAL. Then it is time you rested.
Come. The blood has dried upon your face.
They depart.
SCENE X. Outside the Fallen Fortress
Enter ARDEN with several battered soldiers.
ARDEN. The fortress has fallen.
SOLDIER. Negotiations are underway. Perhaps not all is lost.
ARDEN. Not all?
The walls are gone, the banners lowered.
I almost envy the men who conquered it.
Being among the defeated leaves little comfort.
They look toward the distant city as the scene ends.

“Step in, sir,” Mrs. Barton said. “You must be chilled after such a walk.”
“I’ve come from Ashcombe Hall,” I answered. “They’re making ready a room for me there, but before the evening ends I hoped to conclude a piece of business with Mr. Grey. I doubt I’ll have another chance soon.”
She stopped midway through the passage.
“With Mr. Grey?”
“Yes.”
A strange expression crossed her face.
“Then you haven’t heard.”
“Heard what?”
“That he died four months ago.”
For a moment I thought I had misunderstood her.
“Mr. Grey dead?”
She nodded quietly.
“Come and sit by the fire. I’ll tell you everything. You’ve not eaten, have you?”
I confessed that I had not. She disappeared into the kitchen, where an elderly servant could be heard muttering complaints about visitors, late hours, and the decline of manners. A few minutes later she returned with food and insisted I take it.
Only then did she begin.
I was called to Willow House shortly after your departure, she said. The request came unexpectedly, though I was glad enough to go because of Miss Clara. When I first saw her, the change startled me. She was thinner than before, less lively too, though she tried to conceal it.
Mr. Grey offered little explanation. He merely arranged matters so that Clara spent most of her time with me. At first it seemed a sensible plan. We filled the days with books and small occupations, and she appeared happier.
The improvement did not last.
As the weather warmed, she became increasingly impatient. Though she could walk through the gardens, she was not permitted beyond them, and the limitation weighed heavily on her. Worse still, I could not always keep her company. Whenever household duties drew me away, she complained of being abandoned.
There was also young Edward Hale. At first the two scarcely exchanged a word. He was quiet by nature and seemed content to remain unnoticed.
Gradually, though, Clara developed a habit of teasing him.
One evening she found him sitting by the fireplace as usual.
“Mrs. Barton,” she said, loud enough for him to hear, “do you think Mr. Hale ever dreams? Or does he simply sit and stare until bedtime?”
Edward continued reading.
“I cannot imagine such patience,” she went on. “If I spent my days that way, I should go mad. Tell me, sir—what do you think about all evening?”
Nothing.
“There,” she said triumphantly, “he refuses to answer because he has no answer.”
“Miss Clara,” I warned, “leave the poor gentleman alone.”
At that, Edward finally looked up. Whether he was irritated or secretly entertained by the exchange, I could never decide.

27. On the government of kingdoms by the will of God rather than by fortune
When nations rise to greatness or sink into weakness, people commonly search for visible explanations. Some praise fortune, others blame fate, and many imagine that success comes through the favor of various divine powers. Yet such explanations mistake effects for causes. The true source of authority and dominion lies in the providence of God, who appoints rulers, measures the duration of kingdoms, and orders events according to a wisdom that human beings perceive only in part.
Because His purposes are often hidden, men are tempted to think events occur at random. But what seems confused to us is not confused to Him. The sequence of ages, the growth of empires, and the fall of dynasties belong to an order known fully only by the one who governs it.
For this reason earthly prosperity should never be mistaken for the highest blessing. Power, riches, and victory are possessed by good men and bad alike. If such advantages belonged exclusively to the righteous, many would pursue virtue for reward rather than for its own sake. God therefore allows temporal goods to be shared widely, while reserving true happiness for those whose hearts are rightly ordered. That happiness depends neither on wealth nor rank and cannot be destroyed by changes in worldly circumstances.
28. How one nation demonstrated the sufficiency of the one God
There once existed a people whose history plainly illustrates this principle. Their beginnings were modest, yet they increased in number and strength. Unlike neighboring nations, they did not divide the functions of life among a multitude of gods. They did not seek one deity for harvests, another for war, and another for safe journeys. Nevertheless they prospered.
Their crops grew, their households expanded, and they survived dangers that might easily have destroyed them. What others pursued through elaborate rituals, they received through dependence upon a single divine ruler. Their success served as a lesson that the source of blessing is not multiplied by multiplying objects of worship.
In time, however, success bred complacency. Familiar prosperity weakened gratitude. They became attracted to foreign practices and gradually abandoned the principles that had distinguished them. Their decline followed this departure. The change did not reveal weakness in the God who had guided them; rather, it exposed the instability of human loyalty.
Even so, their story did not end with their downfall. Though dispersed among many regions, they carried with them traditions and writings that preserved the memory of earlier promises and predictions. In this way their misfortune became part of a larger design. What appeared to contemporaries as mere ruin later served as evidence of a providence working through centuries rather than moments.
Here, however, it is fitting to conclude. The remaining matters belong more properly to another discussion and would carry this one beyond reasonable bounds.

LADY MARGARET. Some women would risk far more for far less. Ah—but here comes company.
Enter Lord Steward.
STEWARD. Good morning, ladies. Have I stumbled into a secret council?
ELIZABETH. Nothing so important, my lord. We were only discussing our mistress and wishing her better fortune.
STEWARD. A generous subject. And perhaps not a hopeless one.
ELIZABETH. I pray you are right.
STEWARD. Since we speak of fortune, I carry a message that concerns you. His Majesty has taken particular notice of your conduct and desires to reward it. He intends to create you Countess of Wexford and provide an estate suitable to the title.
ELIZABETH. My lord, I scarcely know how to respond. Such generosity leaves me indebted beyond my ability to repay. All I can offer is my humble thanks and my continued prayers for the King's health.
STEWARD. They will be well received.
Aside.
The King is not alone in admiring her. Beauty and dignity seldom meet so comfortably in one person.
Aloud.
I take my leave.
ELIZABETH. You honour me, my lord.
Exit Steward.
LADY MARGARET. Well! There is justice for you. I have spent half my life chasing favour through corridors and antechambers, and what have I gained? A collection of disappointments. Yet you stand still, and honours come running toward you.
ELIZABETH. It feels very strange.
LADY MARGARET. Strange? Most people would call it delightful. A title, an income, royal approval—few recipes produce a bitter dish from those ingredients.
ELIZABETH. You are determined to laugh at me.
LADY MARGARET. Only because the situation insists upon it. Countess today; who knows what tomorrow may bring?
ELIZABETH. Please, enough. Such talk troubles me more than it pleases me.
LADY MARGARET. Then you possess a rare quality at court.
ELIZABETH. The Queen has troubles enough. I would not willingly become another source of pain for her. Promise me you will not repeat what was said.
LADY MARGARET. My discretion is occasionally better than my reputation suggests.
They depart.

SCENE IV. A Hall of Judgment
Trumpets announce the entrance of officials, clergy, and nobles. The KING takes his seat beneath a canopy, while the QUEEN sits at some distance.
FIRST CHANCELLOR. Let order be kept while these proceedings begin.
KING. We need not rehearse what has already been heard. The commission is known to everyone present.
FIRST CHANCELLOR. Very well.
CLERK. Summon those required before the court.
CRIER. Let all concerned attend and answer.
The hall falls silent.
SECOND CHANCELLOR. The court is assembled.
KING. Then let us not waste time.
FIRST CHANCELLOR. The record shall note that this hearing proceeds under lawful authority and with the consent of all required parties.
QUEEN. Authority is valuable. Justice is more valuable still.
FIRST CHANCELLOR. Both shall be observed, Your Grace.
KING. That is my desire as well. Let no one assume the matter settled before it is heard.
A few lords exchange glances.
SECOND CHANCELLOR. Then we may begin.
The hearing proceeds as the court watches in expectation.

“It feels impossible to sit still any longer,” said Miss Harrington. “Come, let us walk about the room.”
Charlotte joined her at once. The invitation had another purpose besides exercise, and that purpose soon succeeded. Mr. Ashford looked up from the book he had been reading. A moment later, he shut it altogether.
“You ought to join us,” Miss Harrington said.
“I think not.”
“And why?”
“Because your walk is prompted by one of two reasons, and I should interfere with both.”
Naturally, this produced immediate curiosity.
Charlotte laughed. “Pay no attention. He means to puzzle us.”
“That is not enough,” said Miss Harrington. “I demand an explanation.”
Mr. Ashford obliged.
“You are either exchanging secrets, which would make me an inconvenience, or you believe the room offers an excellent stage upon which to display yourselves. In that case, I prefer my current seat as a viewing position.”
Miss Harrington stared at him.
“Really, Mr. Ashford, you are impossible.”
“There is a simple remedy,” Charlotte said. “Laugh at him.”
“If only it were so easy. He never supplies the necessary material.”
“Then he possesses an enviable advantage,” Charlotte replied. “Most people are generous providers of nonsense. I enjoy discovering it.”
Mr. Ashford smiled slightly.
“That habit has ruined many fair judgments. A determined wit can find something ridiculous in almost anything.”
“Almost anything,” Charlotte agreed. “But not everything. I hope I know the difference between genuine merit and mere absurdity. It is the latter that entertains me.”
“And you suppose I have none?”
“I suspect you try very hard not to.”
“No one succeeds entirely.”
“Not even with vanity?”
“Certainly not.”
“Or pride?”
“At least pride may be controlled.”
That answer nearly made Charlotte laugh outright.
Miss Harrington, who had followed the exchange with growing impatience, interrupted.
“Well? What have you discovered about him?”
Charlotte considered.
“He appears to regard himself as nearly faultless.”
“I said nothing of the kind,” said Mr. Ashford. “I am perfectly aware of my defects. My opinions are slow to change. Injuries are slower still to leave my memory. Once I think poorly of someone, I rarely think well again.”
“A serious flaw,” Charlotte replied. “Though not a humorous one. I cannot mock you for it.”
“Every character has its weakness.”
“Then yours is distrusting people.”
He met her eyes.
“And yours is misunderstanding them on purpose.”
For a second neither spoke.
Miss Harrington broke the silence with visible relief.
“Enough philosophy. Let us have music. It is far more agreeable than being ignored in one's own drawing room.”

CAPTAIN. Carry this answer back to your commander.
Tell him we are soldiers, not courtiers.
Rain, mud, and hard marching have done their work on us.
The bright feathers are gone, the fine coats ruined,
and there is little splendour left in the army's appearance.
Still, appearances matter less than resolve.
My men believe that before night falls
they will either be dressed more richly than they are now,
or leave the field without need of clothing at all.
Should victory favour us,
ransom will take care of itself.
So save yourself another journey.
The only payment I promise is my own body,
and there is not much profit in that.
HERALD. I will deliver your words.
Exit.
CAPTAIN. Though I think he'll return before this business ends.
Enter LORD PEMBROKE.
PEMBROKE. Grant me the first advance, my lord.
CAPTAIN. Gladly.
Forward now.
Let Heaven determine the outcome.
They exit.

SCENE IV. Amid the Battle
Enter RALPH, dragging a captured officer. THOMAS follows.
RALPH. Yield yourself!
OFFICER. Sir, I surrender.
RALPH. Sensible at last.
Now tell me your name.
OFFICER. Captain Duval.
RALPH. A captain? Then you must be worth keeping alive.
Thomas, ask him what his freedom is worth.
THOMAS. My master wishes to know your ransom.
OFFICER. I will pay what I can. Only spare me.
RALPH. You hear that? The man values life highly.
Tell him he stands before a warrior of fearsome reputation.
THOMAS. I doubt that will be necessary.
RALPH. Do as you're told.
THOMAS. As you wish.
To the Officer.
My master would like you to be impressed.
OFFICER. I am chiefly concerned with surviving.
RALPH. A practical fellow.
Well then, what sum?
OFFICER. Two hundred crowns.
RALPH. Better.
Perhaps there is more where that came from.
THOMAS. Is there?
OFFICER. My family could provide additional funds.
RALPH. Then I grow merciful.
Tell him he may keep his throat uncut.
THOMAS. He accepts your offer and grants your release.
OFFICER. I am deeply grateful.
RALPH. What gratitude does he express?
THOMAS. He believes himself fortunate.
RALPH. A wise judgment.
Come along.
Ralph leads the prisoner away.
THOMAS watches them go.
THOMAS. It is remarkable how loudly some men advertise their courage.
The fellow talks like a conqueror and bargains like a merchant.
I've known quieter soldiers who would face twice the danger with half the fuss.
The world often mistakes noise for bravery.
Exit.

“Enough. Until next time.”
With that he left.
The excitement that had carried him through the conversation still lingered, but only faintly. It was an uncomfortable feeling—something between exhilaration and illness. For a moment he walked quickly, almost energetically. Then the strength drained out of him again. His limbs felt heavy. Even breathing seemed laborious.
The other man remained behind, motionless.
He sat for a long while without touching the untouched glass before him. Their conversation had altered something in his thinking. A suspicion he had entertained for days now seemed less convincing.
“No,” he finally decided. “People have misunderstood him.”
As Victor stepped out into the street, he nearly ran straight into Leon.
The collision was so sudden that both stopped short.
Leon stared.
Then exploded.
“So I've found you at last!”
His voice echoed down the stairwell.
“Do you know where I've been looking? Everywhere! I went to your room. Twice. I questioned everyone who might have seen you. I was beginning to think you'd vanished completely.”
Victor looked exhausted.
“I asked for none of that.”
“Because you wanted to be alone?” Leon said incredulously. “You can hardly stay upright.”
“Move aside.”
Instead, Leon grabbed his shoulder.
“Not a chance.”
Victor's patience snapped.
“You call this friendship, but it feels like punishment. Can't you understand that? I don't want to be watched, advised, rescued, comforted. Every one of you arrives determined to improve my life. Has it occurred to you that I might not want improvement?”
The words came more quickly now.
“You never stop. Questions, concern, advice—endlessly. It wears me down. Leave me alone.”
He ended almost breathless.
Leon released him.
For a moment he seemed genuinely thoughtful.
“All right,” he said. “Go to the devil if that's your wish.”
Victor took a step.
“Not yet.”
Leon folded his arms.
“I've something to say first. The lot of you are impossible. You treat every personal difficulty as though it were a matter of world importance. You brood over your troubles day and night until they become your entire identity.”
Victor remained silent.
“Yes, that's right. Brood over them. Worship them. Feed them. Then congratulate yourselves on being profound.”
His irritation was returning rapidly.
“And don't imagine you're unique. You're all copies. Every one of you thinks the same gloomy thoughts and makes the same speeches about isolation and suffering.”
This time Victor almost smiled.
Leon caught it immediately.
“Don't mock me. I'm making perfect sense.”
He jabbed a finger toward the street.
“I'm having people over tonight. They've probably arrived already. My uncle is entertaining them while I chase you around the city. If you possessed even the smallest amount of wisdom, you'd come with me instead of wearing yourself into the pavement.”
He shook his head.
“Honestly, a few hours among ordinary human beings might do you more good than a month alone with your thoughts.”

A rocky shore. SERAPHEL, exiled Queen of Valdris, stands apart. Her spirit LUMEN attends her.
SERAPHEL. They walk straight past me. Three years gone, and not one eye Knows mine. Go, Lumen — fetch my robes and crown From the cave. [Exit Lumen.] I'll put this beggar's skin Behind me, stand as I was. Be quick now; Thy service is nearly done.
Lumen re-enters bearing the robes, singing.
LUMEN. Where the wind turns, there turn I, Through the hollow reeds I sigh; Where the river meets the sea, There shall I at last run free. Merrily, merrily shall I fly Past the sun and past the sky.
SERAPHEL. Good Lumen. Thou shalt have thy liberty soon enough — But one last errand: go silent to the bay, Find the captain of that vessel sleeping Beneath enchantment still, and wake him. Send him to me here.
LUMEN. I am gone and back before The tide shifts a pebble. [Exit.]
DUKE ALDRIC. What place is this? The very air feels bent. Some power dwells here. God guide us out of it.
SERAPHEL. Look at me properly, Aldric. It is I — Seraphel. The queen you set aside. No spirit, no trick of the light — give me your hand. You feel that? That is blood, and it is warm.
ALDRIC. This cannot — thou wert lost at sea, three years, We saw the ship — thy pulse does beat — I am Not well, I think — some fever holds me still —
SERAPHEL. No fever. The isle kept me. I kept myself. And now I want what's mine returned — not seized By rage, not out of fury. I have had Three years to learn what fury costs, and it costs much. I ask it plainly: give me back my crown.
MONTARVO. [Aside.] She was always just. God, she was always just.
SERAPHEL. [Aside to Montarvo.] I know thy part in it. Have known these three years. Yet I've no blade for thee — only this offer: Come honestly before me and we're done with it.
MONTARVO. [Aside.] This is either mercy or it's witchcraft. And I think it mercy.
ALDRIC. If thou art truly living, tell me how. We watched the storm tear that ship into kindling — I have not — there is not a morning since I have not thought of it. The crown has been No easy thing to wear, I swear to thee.
SERAPHEL. I believe thee. Grief is something, Aldric — It is not nothing. I was thrown ashore here Half-dead, and the isle was strange, and then less strange. I will not tell the whole of it today; The story is three years long and the light Is going. Know only this: I lived. I lost things, Same as thee. We are both worn by it — Perhaps that is sufficient ground to start from. Take my hand again. This shore has served As court to me too long. Let us go home.

I had the better part of two days to kill in Gloucester before the coastal packet would carry me north, which meant finding a bed and a meal at a price I could actually meet. The night was raw — not a proper rain, just the kind of wet that works its way in gradually, so you're soaked before you've noticed a single drop. I knew no one in town. I'd counted my money twice already, walking, and it hadn't improved either time. All right then, I said to myself, ducking under a chandler's awning to watch the rain bead on the cobbles — the situation is plain enough. You need a roof, Nathaniel. Four walls, something hot, and the whole affair cheap. This is not the night for dignity.
The main street I walked as a kind of penance, knowing what I'd find. The Anchor and Crown had lamplight in every window and a fiddle going inside — too warm, too bright, no question. The Mariner's Rest stood further along, its sign showing a sailor in a hammock that no hammock had ever resembled, and from the open door came roasting meat on the air, persistent and persuasive. I talked myself past it. My left boot, which had been suggesting for some weeks that its useful life was behind it, now settled the matter and let the wet in.
I turned toward the water. It had always been sound logic in ports like this — cheapness and brine go together. The streets narrowed fast. The lamps gave out. Buildings leaned at each other overhead like men at a bar, and underfoot there was rope, a broken oar, the black slickness of old fish-scales on stone. I pushed open what I took for an inn on one corner, and found instead a roomful of men in heated argument over a net. They looked up, registered nothing of interest, and returned to their quarrel. I stepped back out.
Then, hard by the waterfront, wedged between a rope-walk and a warehouse as though it had grown there by accident, there was a narrow building with a board swinging on a rusted chain above the door. Whatever had once been painted on it — fish, anchor, who could say — the weather had long since made a blank of it. Beneath, in letters you could still just read: Harmon's — Beds and Supper. No embellishment. The building itself leaned slightly into the prevailing wind, the way old things do when they've simply stopped resisting. One window showed a light that might have been a candle in a back room, or a pipe-glow, or the idea of light rather than the thing itself. It had exactly the look of a place that expected nothing from you and therefore asked nothing beyond what was fair — which, on a night like that one, was recommendation enough. The door opened easily, without drama.

Drums and alarums. KING MALDREN enters with soldiers. His mother, LADY EDWYNN, intercepts him. LADY CORNYS, widow of Lord Hest, stands apart.
LADY EDWYNN. Stay, son. I will be heard.
KING MALDREN. Stand clear, good mother. The field waits on no woman's grief. Beat drum!
LADY EDWYNN. Beat every drum thou hast — drown out my words. The grief beneath them is too deep for that.
KING MALDREN. I cannot stop. Not now.
LADY EDWYNN. No, never stopped — Not from the hour thou first thrust into the world To wrench and break whatever lay in reach. Thou wert not born, but hurled, as men hurl stone From engines made for nothing else but ruin.
KING MALDREN. Enough, madam.
LADY EDWYNN. Enough? Name me one hour — One gentle hour, one moment of thy life — That ever earned a soft word from my lips. Thy boyhood was a ledger full of harm. Thy youth a sharper version of the same. And now thine age is every page together, Bound hard in blood.
KING MALDREN. You suckled me. Take comfort in that debt.
LADY EDWYNN. The breast that fed thee pays it back in sorrow. Go, then, and carry what I give thee now— A mother's curse—as thou wouldst carry armour: It will sit heavier than steel, trust that. My prayers ride with thine enemies tonight. The souls thou wronged are there before the throne Crying against thee. Maldren — thou art built of harm. And harm shall be the room wherein thou diest. [Exit.]
LADY CORNYS. She speaks for the dead and half the living both. I lack her years, and she outstrips me in fury, But every word she said — I say it too.
KING MALDREN. A moment, lady. There is business between us.
LADY CORNYS. There's nothing left. My lord is gone. My sons Are gone. What business could be left to do That grief hasn't already had its way with?
KING MALDREN. Your daughter — she is fair, well-born, worth much.
LADY CORNYS. Wouldst use her as a clasp to pin things shut? Then I'll undo the clasp before thou reach it — I'll call her baseborn, gotten out of wedlock, Dress her in slander till no court could want her.
KING MALDREN. Wrong not the girl; her blood is beyond question.
LADY CORNYS. That blood ran in my sons. I know its worth. See what their noble birth afforded them.
KING MALDREN. Their stars were set against them. Destiny.
LADY CORNYS. Thy hands were set against them. Call it that. No constellation killed them — thou didst, quietly, Before they found their voices to speak back. But mine is found, and grief has made it hard, And I will use it though thy every drum Beats on until the skins split through.
KING MALDREN. Lady, as I Would prosper in this war and see it ended — I mean your daughter and yourself more good Than all the harm that's fallen on you yet!

"Civil words!" she turned on her husband with a look that would have felled a steadier man than I to witness. "If you'd had the spine to stand your ground, you might have stayed in your study and spared us the performance. Exactly as I expected—exactly! You call your weakness patience and wonder why nobody respects it. No—I shan't open it. Not for you." She pressed back against the door with a kind of fierce pleasure in her own obstruction. "A year I've spent smoothing your quarrels and flattering your pride, and here is what it comes to—two fools in perfect agreement, blaming the one person in the house with any sense!"
Frederick went very white. He had never managed rage gracefully; it always drained out of him and left something worse behind—a cold, shaking helplessness I found more alarming than any outburst. He set one hand on the doorframe and said nothing, and Marian watched him with her chin up and something in her eyes that was half contempt and half a wild, unsettling pleasure.
"Your husband," said Ravenswood from his place by the window, with a short laugh, "appears to be rehearsing his death-scene." He looked at her as he said it, not at Frederick. That told me everything about the three of them I had not already understood.
What came next I could not have anticipated. Frederick—who had seemed to be folding inward toward some silent collapse—crossed the room and struck Ravenswood across the jaw with a force that surprised us all, Ravenswood particularly. He went back hard against the sideboard; the decanter tipped and shattered, glass fanning bright across the floorboards. Nobody moved.
"Go," said Marian. Not to Ravenswood. To her husband. Her voice had gone flat and strange.
Frederick went. His feet on the stairs were steady and deliberate, which was almost worse than if he'd run.
Ravenswood straightened and touched his jaw. His eyes had gone very dark and I knew that look, and I wanted nothing to do with what it was promising.
"He'll have gone for Dawson and the groom," I said quickly—not entirely a lie, it seemed probable enough. "Three of them at least. You'd do well to be elsewhere before they find you standing over a broken decanter."
He went, through the garden door, without looking back.
Marian dropped into the armchair and pressed both hands to her temples. "Lock that door, Joanna. And if you find Leonard, tell him I'm unwell and not to be disturbed." A pause. "I'm not unwell—not yet. But I shall be, if it comes to it. He'll arrive with his grievances all set in order and his feelings freshly honed, and I cannot answer for what I'll say. Tell him I was taken faint. Tell him I'm sleeping. Whatever keeps him at the other end of the corridor until I've had an hour." She looked up at me then, and the brightness had gone out entirely, leaving something raw underneath. "You don't think it my fault, Joanna? You were here from the beginning—you saw how it started."

CAVENDISH. This much at least: that we who lift our swords do so with hearts that will not disown the cause. Whatever follows.
Enter a Soldier, breathless.
SOLDIER. My lord — be ready. Lord Pemberton's van is moving. Now.
CAVENDISH. Good. He saves me from a speech I had no ending for. Here, then, is all of it.
He draws.
Let every man do what his worst enemy would dread to find him capable of. Strike well. And if this fellowship should not assemble twice — it has assembled once. That is not nothing. On.
Trumpets. They embrace and exexit.

The open field. Alarums. Enter LORD FENWICK and SIR THOMAS CRAY.
CRAY. Who are you, that you've followed me this far? What is it you want from me?
FENWICK. My name is Fenwick. I follow because they tell me thou art King.
CRAY. They tell thee right.
FENWICK. Then right shall do as well for my sword's purpose as the wrong ever did. The Earl of Mortham fell not half an hour past — thought he was striking thee. I'll not mistake. Yield, or the field has thee.
CRAY. Not from me. I have not been a king to end a prisoner.
They fight. Cray falls. Enter Cavendish.
CAVENDISH. Is it done?
FENWICK. Done. The King himself.
CAVENDISH. Look closer. I know that face. This is Sir Thomas Cray — brave man, well-dressed, not remotely the King. They've turned him out in royal colours to draw our swords off course and waste our afternoon.
FENWICK. A borrowed face. He bought it dear enough.
CAVENDISH. The King runs several copies in the field. Strike one and another rises.
FENWICK. Then I'll go through every coat he owns until I find the living man inside one.
CAVENDISH. Quickly, then.
Exeunt. Enter GREYWICK, alone.
GREYWICK. I'll be honest with myself, at least, here in this quiet corner of an otherwise extremely loud afternoon: I am not well. I have occupied three portions of this field that no man of reasonable disposition should have gone near. My company — such as survived the first half-hour — have redistributed themselves among the ditches and the hedgerows, where, I don't doubt, they are making their peace with God and examining their life's choices. I've done remarkable things today. Remarkable. I shall recall them warmly from a distance.
He notices the body.
Sir Thomas Cray. There's a homily in thee, friend. Dressed in the King's own livery, sent out to make a brave show of it — and here thou art, in a field that no ballad will name correctly for as long as ballads are sung. Honour. I have given honour serious thought today, primarily from positions of acute personal discomfort, and I find it best appreciated from the outside — like a large fire: warming at a distance, inadvisable to embrace directly.
Enter young PEMBERTON.
YOUNG PEMBERTON. Greywick — why art thou standing? Give me thy sword. Mine snapped. There are three of Fenwick's captains still loose and doing mischief. Thy sword, man.
GREYWICK. My sword — it's — there is a condition of the blade —
YOUNG PEMBERTON. Give it here!
GREYWICK. There. Though I warn thee it has grown somewhat theoretical about its purpose.
YOUNG PEMBERTON. This is — this is a flask.
GREYWICK. The cases are extremely similar. Any man, in haste —
YOUNG PEMBERTON. Is there a battle in which thou art not a fool?
He throws it at Greywick and exits.
GREYWICK. If Fenwick lives, I'll find him — eventually. If he finds me, I shall bear it with composure. There is an honour in mere continuance that nobody has ever set to music, which I have always thought a gap in the tradition.
Exit.

Another part of the field. Alarums. Enter KING EDWARD, young PEMBERTON, LORD MARSHAL.
KING. Harry — thou bleedest. Fall back, I tell thee. Lord Marshal, take him off the field.
LORD MARSHAL. Not I, my liege — not unless I'm bleeding half as badly.

Their reason gone, their terrors growing strong, The very branches seemed to do them wrong; Thorns caught at sleeves and briars snagged at hats, And every shadow moved like startled cats. I chased them further in, laughing as I flew, And left poor Cobweb knotted in the dew. So when the sleeper stirred beneath the oak It was the strangest face on which she woke.
SILVANUS. This falls out better than I'd dared to hope. But have you touched the other pair, and set The love-drops on his eyes, as I directed?
SPRITE. Found him sleeping under the thorn-tree — Done and finished. And the girl beside him, So that when he stirs she's the first thing seen.
Enter Celia and Florian.
SILVANUS. Keep still. That is the very man.
SPRITE. The girl's right. The man isn't.
FLORIAN. Why spend your breath so bitterly on me, Who've given you nothing but devotion? Save that for someone who deserves it — turn Those eyes on me for once without the frost.
CELIA. I'm mild now. I could be considerably worse. Thou hast given me reason enough for a curse. If thou hast crept upon Silvio sleeping And done him harm — speak it out. The sun Is not more constant to the day than he Was to me. That he'd slip away from me sleeping — I'd sooner believe the sea would dry to dust. Thou know'st what thou hast done. That face says so.
FLORIAN. This face is grief — thy grief, thy sharpness, thine. And thou the one who keeps on drawing blood And looking clear and bright as a morning star With nothing on thy conscience, apparently.
CELIA. I've no interest in that. Where is he? Wilt thou give him back?
FLORIAN. I'd sooner give his shadow to the wind.
CELIA. Thou dog. Thou faithless hound. Hast killed him then — say it plainly, once — And be among men never more. One word Of truth — was it sleeping that thou did it? A worm might manage that; an adder would. And thou hast an adder's tongue to match the deed — More forked than any serpent in this wood.
FLORIAN. Thou throw'st this fury at a wrong address. I've not touched him — nor do I know where he is — Nor whether he's alive or dead, for that.
CELIA. Then tell me he is living. That will do.
FLORIAN. And what do I get for the telling of it?
CELIA. That thou need never look at me again. And so — whatever hath become of him — I leave thee here with it.
Exit.
FLORIAN. There's no following her when she's like this. I'll stay then. Sorrow heaped on sorrow grows Too much for wakefulness to carry long — sleep is the one coin grief has left to spend before the night runs out. I'll spend a little.
He lies down.
SILVANUS. What hast thou done? Thou hast applied the drops To the one man who needed no amendment — Left true love confused and tangled worse, And done the false love not a bit of harm.
SPRITE. Well — fate seems determined that for every true heart A million others come apart. It's just the way of things.
SILVANUS. Go through the wood — faster than the wind — and find me Helena, who wanders pale and sighing out her colour for a love that hasn't looked at her. Fetch her by seeming — I'll treat his eyes before she comes in sight.
SPRITE. Gone already — no arrow travels faster.
Exit.
SILVANUS. Blossom bruised by careless sky, Let him see through different eyes — when his true love passes by let her be a morning star. Wake and wonder what thou art.
Enter Sprite.
SPRITE. Captain — Helena is nearly here, And the other one I muddled, making vows To whoever comes in range. Do we watch? Lord — there is nothing like a fool in love.
SILVANUS. Quietly — the noise will wake the sleeper.
SPRITE. Two suits pressed at once on the same soul — That is rare sport even by this wood's standards. Give me a tangle properly arranged And I am in my element entirely.

BARDOLPH. Ha! What madness is this? Dost thou stand before me and speak of honour, thou hollow-cheeked rogue that hast none? Away with you. The very smell of your pretence sickens me.
EVANS. I do beseech you most earnestly, thou scabrous and pestilent fellow — at my entreaties, my urgings, my solemn requests — to take up and eat this onion. Look you, I know you claim no love of it. Your humours, your constitution, your digestion, none of them welcome it. And therefore I desire you, particularly and without reservation, to eat it.
BARDOLPH. Not for Merlin himself and all his prophecies.
EVANS. Here is a prophecy for you. [Strikes him.] Will you be so gracious, thou mangy wretch, as eat it?
BARDOLPH. Thou wretched cur — I shall have satisfaction for this.
EVANS. You speak with great confidence. And so you shall, when Providence sees fit. Until that hour, I would have you remain among the living and eat your vittles. Here is something to season it. [Strikes him.] You called me yesterday a hill-dwelling pedant. I will make you today a scholar of the very lowest form. Fall to; if you can mock an onion, you can eat one.
FORD. Hold — you have humbled him enough, I think.
EVANS. I say I will see him eat the better portion of this onion, or I will beat his skull for a fortnight together. Chew, I pray you. It is sovereign for a fresh wound, and good too for that bloody foolish head of yours.
BARDOLPH. Must I chew?
EVANS. Yes — certainly, and without dispute, ambiguity, or any manner of reservation whatsoever.
BARDOLPH. By this onion, I will have a most terrible revenge. I eat, I eat, I swear it—
EVANS. Eat, I entreat you. Will you have more to accompany it? There is not enough onion here to make a credible oath upon.
BARDOLPH. Stay your stick. You see plainly that I eat.
EVANS. Much good may it do you, scurvy knave. Heartily, I mean it. Nay, waste nothing — the outer skin is wholesome for a cracked pate. When you encounter onions hereafter in your travels, show them every reverence. That is the whole of my instruction.
BARDOLPH. Agreed.
EVANS. Ay, onions are good things. Take this coin toward the mending of your head.
BARDOLPH. A coin! To me!
EVANS. In honest faith you will take it. Else I have a second onion about my person, and you shall eat that one too.
BARDOLPH. I take it as earnest pledge of my revenge.
EVANS. If I stand in your debt, I will pay in blows. You shall deal in timber and buy nothing from me but cudgels. God be with you — and preserve you — and mend your broken skull. [Exit.]
BARDOLPH. The very stars shall hear of this.
