FORD. Go, go. You are a dissembling, cowardly rascal. Will you make sport of an ancient custom, observed out of reverence and worn as an emblem of valour long past, and yet not venture to make good in your deeds one syllable of your boasting? I have seen you sneering at this gentleman two or three times now. You supposed that because he could not command English with a native ease, he could not therefore handle an English stick. You have found otherwise. Let a Welsh lesson teach you, henceforth, what an English gentleman ought to be. Fare you well. [Exit.]
BARDOLPH. Does Fortune make a fool of me at last? Word reaches me that Meg is dead in the hospital — carried off by the pox — and my last refuge with her. I grow old. From these weary bones all dignity has been beaten out. Well: I shall turn rogue, lend my hand to picking pockets on the road. To England will I creep, and there do my petty thieving; and I shall patch these beaten scars and swear to all the world I earned them on the fields of France. [Exit.]

He remembered someone had proposed handing him over to the authorities on the spot. He couldn't recall the face — couldn't place it even now — but he remembered turning and saying something in reply.... So that was what lay behind last night's terror. The most appalling part was that he had nearly ruined himself, come within a hair of destruction, over something so completely trivial. This man could tell them nothing except that he had asked about the apartment and the bloodstains. Ilya Petrovitch had nothing beyond that delirium, no real evidence, only this psychology that cuts both ways — nothing solid, nothing certain. And if no further facts came out (they must not, they simply must not!) — then what? What could they do to him? How could they convict him, even if they arrested him?
Besides, Ilya Petrovitch had only just learned about the apartment. He hadn't known before today.
"Was it you who told him... that I had been there?" he asked, a sudden thought seizing him.
"Told who?"
"The examining officer. The one running the inquiry."
"Yes. The yard-keepers wouldn't go, so I went myself."
"Today?"
"Two minutes before you arrived. I heard everything — how he went at you."
"Where? How is that possible?"
"The next room. I sat there the whole time."
"You were the surprise, then? But how — good God, how did it come to this?"
"The yard-keepers made it plain they wouldn't help," the man began. "Too late now, they said, and besides he might be angry we hadn't come sooner. It troubled me. I couldn't sleep. I started making inquiries myself, found out yesterday where to go, and came this morning. First visit he wasn't in. Second visit he couldn't see me. Third time they showed me through. I told him everything, just as it happened — and he started pacing the room, beating himself on the chest. 'What do you mean by this? If I had known, I would have arrested him on the spot!' Then he ran out, pulled someone into the corner, talked in a low voice, came back to me and started in with questions and reproaches. Scolded me thoroughly. I told him everything — told him too that you hadn't said a word back to me yesterday, hadn't even recognised me. He was off again then, pacing and striking himself on the chest. And when you were announced, he sent me into the next room. 'Sit there,' he said. 'Don't move, whatever you hear.' He locked the door himself. 'I may want you,' he said. Then after Nikolay was brought he let me out as soon as you'd gone. 'I'll send for you again,' he told me."
"And Nikolay — did he question him while you were still there?"
"He got rid of me first, same as you. Before he said a word to Nikolay."
The man stood motionless a moment, then bent down abruptly and touched the floor with his fingers.
"Forgive me — the evil thoughts I had, and the slander."
"God will forgive you," said Mikolayev. The man bowed once more, not so low this time, then turned and quietly left.
"Cuts both ways — all of it cuts both ways now," Mikolayev kept repeating, and he stepped out into the street steadier than he had felt in days. "Now we'll fight," he said, a cold smile on his lips as he went down the stairs. The coldness was for himself; he thought back on his near-collapse with something that felt very much like contempt.

PART V
CHAPTER I
The morning after that disastrous scene with Varya and her mother brought a certain unwelcome clarity with it. Hard as it was to swallow, Andrei Filippovich found himself forced — slowly, stubbornly, against every instinct — to accept as finished and irreversible what had struck him only yesterday as impossible. The snake of wounded pride had been at him all night. Getting out of bed, he went straight to the mirror. He was almost afraid to look — half-convinced, in some obscure way, that the night had yellowed his skin.

"One can't be expected to copy documents without a decent pen. And the ink they give us is barely fit for pigs."
"Don't quarrel with your bread and butter."
"What bread and butter? They pay next to nothing. What good is it?" He said it reluctantly, half to himself.
"And I suppose you'd rather be rich all at once?"
He looked at her strangely. "Yes," he said, after a brief pause. Firmly, too. "I would."
"Don't stare at me like that — you'll frighten me. Do you want the soup or not?"
"As you like."
"Oh, I nearly forgot. A letter came for you this morning."
"A letter? For me? From whom?"
"How should I know. I gave the boy two copecks out of my own pocket. You'll pay me back?"
"Bring it here — bring it now, for heaven's sake!" Volkov cried, suddenly beside himself. "Now, please!"
She brought it a minute later. He knew at once: his sister's hand, from the town of K——. The colour went out of his face. It had been a long time — and yet it was not only the surprise of it that struck him. Something else moved in him too, less easy to name.
"Fedosya, go — here are your two copecks, but please go, quickly—"
The envelope shook in his fingers. He couldn't open it with her still standing there. He needed to be alone with it. Only when her footsteps had shuffled away down the corridor did he lift the letter to his lips. Then he turned it over and looked at the address — that careful, upright hand, each letter formed with the deliberateness he had known since he was a boy. He paused. Something held him back, some dim reluctance he couldn't account for. At last he broke the seal.
Three sheets, covered on both sides in a close, neat hand.
"My dear Aleksei," his sister had written. "It is nearly three months since I last wrote, and I know that will have worried you. Please don't think too harshly of me once you've read everything I have to say. You know I think of you always. You and mother are all I have that is certain.
"I was so sorry to hear, through mother, that you have had to leave the office and that the tutoring too has come to nothing. It grieved me more than I can say. My own situation here has not allowed me to send you anything, much as I have wanted to. Of the thirty roubles I earn each year in this household, twenty were advanced to me when I first arrived, and I have been paying that back a little each month — I am only now nearly clear of it. The Starovoitovs are not unkind, on the whole, but they are careful with money after the provincial fashion, and there is no point in asking for more than what was agreed.
"But I have something to tell you now, Alyosha, which I think will be at least partly good news. The arrangement here is ending at Michaelmas, and I find I am not sorry. There are things I have kept from you — things I didn't want to put in a letter, not knowing whose hands it might pass through — but I will tell you all of it now, plainly and in order, so you understand what these months have really been like and why I said so little.
"When you wrote in the spring asking if all was well, and mentioned what you'd heard from Petrov — I barely knew how to answer. If I had told you the truth, I think you would have done something rash. I know your nature. You would not have stayed where you were. And what then? I couldn't be the cause of that. So I told you things were tolerable. Which was true enough, in a way.
"But now I can tell you everything."

LEONTES. I placed that ring on her finger myself — it was the first thing I ever gave her.
CAMILLO. I cannot speak to that. But such a ring — and I am certain it was hers — I saw on Polixenes this very morning, on his hand, worn openly.
LEONTES. If it be that—
CAMILLO. If it be that ring, or any jewel once belonging to her, it stands against her with the rest.
LEONTES. O, that the traitor had a thousand lives! One is not near enough — nothing near enough for what I feel. Now I see it plain. Mark me, Camillo — all my love, all of it, I throw from me into the empty air. Gone. Rise up, black hatred, from whatever pit holds thee. Love, yield thy throne within my breast to something harder. Fill, heart — fill with it — the poison is in, and it is of the bitterest kind.
CAMILLO. Yet hold — patience—
LEONTES. No patience. There is blood in this, Camillo.
CAMILLO. I beg you — wait. Your mind may cool.
LEONTES. It will not cool. As the great northern river in the grip of winter drives its current without pause or turning, pressing through narrows and past rocks to reach the open sea — so my thoughts move now with that same blind force and will not turn back toward love, will not soften or retreat, until a full and final vengeance has swallowed everything. Now, by the sky above me, I bind myself with a vow. [Kneels.]
CAMILLO. Do not rise yet. [Kneels.] Hear it, you fixed and watching stars — hear that here Camillo pledges all his cunning, all his hands, all his heart, to the service of a wronged king. Let him command, and obedience shall be my whole purpose, be the work never so dark. [They rise.]
LEONTES. I receive thy bond not with hollow thanks but with full acceptance, and will put thee to it this very hour. Within three days, tell me Polixenes lives no more.
CAMILLO. The man is as good as dead — say only the word. But let her live.
LEONTES. Damn her — false, corrupted thing! Damn her! Come, walk with me, and I'll find swift means to deal with this fair deceiver. From this hour you are my right hand.
CAMILLO. And yours I am, now and for ever. [Exeunt.]

SCENE III. Sicily. The outer courtyard of the palace. Enter Hermione, Paulina, and a Servant.
HERMIONE. Sirrah, do you know where Lord Archidamus is lodged?
SERVANT. I dare not say he lies anywhere, my lady.
HERMIONE. Why not?
SERVANT. A man of his standing — to say precisely where he lies at any given hour is to court contradiction, and contradiction is a quarrelsome companion.
HERMIONE. Never mind that. Where does he keep his rooms?
SERVANT. To tell you where he keeps his rooms, I must know where he keeps himself, and that shifts rather freely, my lady.
HERMIONE. Is there nothing useful in you?
SERVANT. This much I'll swear to: he is somewhere within the walls of Sicily, which does narrow the field considerably.
HERMIONE. Go and find him. Tell him I have spoken for him and that he should come to me at once.
SERVANT. That lies well within the reach of my legs, if not my learning, and I'll set about it directly. [Exit.]
HERMIONE. Paulina, I cannot think where I have put that brooch.
PAULINA. I couldn't say, madam.
HERMIONE. I would sooner have lost a purse full of gold. And yet — were my lord other than he is, were there any shadow of suspicion in his nature — a thing so small as this might be enough to kindle it. But he is not built that way.
PAULINA. He is not jealous, then? Not at all?
HERMIONE. Him? The very country that shaped him burned all such weakness out long ago.
PAULINA. Look — he comes.
Enter Leontes.
HERMIONE. I'll not leave him until I have spoken for Archidamus. My lord, how are you?
LEONTES. Well enough, my lady. [Aside.] God, what agony it is to wear this face. — And you, Hermione?
HERMIONE. Very well, my lord.
LEONTES. Give me your hand. It is warm.
HERMIONE. It has not yet known enough age or sorrow to make it otherwise.
LEONTES. Warm hands speak of a warm nature. Very warm. This hand of yours, I think, wants discipline — fasting, cool reflection, the check of prayer and devotion. There is something in it that doesn't readily submit.
HERMIONE. You may say so. It was that same hand that gave you my heart.
LEONTES. A generous hand. Once, a heart given was a heart kept. Now it seems the hand moves rather faster than the heart behind it.

O Fabian, If thou keep faith, I'll end this wretched strife And leave my father's house to be thy wife. [Exit.]

SCENE IV. The same. A street. Enter Valentine, Fabian, Curio, and Sebastian.
FABIAN. No — we slip out during supper, change our dress at my lodging, and return before anyone has noticed. The whole business fits in an hour.
VALENTINE. We've barely thought it through.
CURIO. We haven't even settled on torch-bearers.
SEBASTIAN. The thing's not worth doing unless it's done properly. Honestly I'd sooner leave it.
FABIAN. It's not yet five. We have time enough.
Enter Gobbo with a letter.
Gobbo — what have you there?
GOBBO. Break the seal, sir, and it will speak for itself.
FABIAN. I know this hand. A fine hand too — fairer than the paper it's written on, if you take my meaning.
VALENTINE. Love's work, plainly enough.
GOBBO. By your leave, sir—
FABIAN. Where are you off to?
GOBBO. To bid my old master the moneylender to supper tonight with my new master, who is a Christian gentleman of considerable appetite.
FABIAN. Here — take this. And tell sweet Isabetta I will not fail her. Say it to her privately. Go, gentlemen—
[Exit Gobbo.]
Will you get yourselves ready for tonight? I've settled the torch-bearer.
CURIO. Ay, I'll see to it at once.
SEBASTIAN. And I.
FABIAN. Come to Valentine's lodging within the hour.
CURIO. Done. [Exeunt Curio and Sebastian.]
VALENTINE. That was from Isabetta, was it not?
FABIAN. I'll tell you everything. She's laid out exactly how I'm to get her out of her father's house — what money and jewels she's put aside, what disguise she's prepared. If her father ever finds any mercy in this world, it'll be on her account alone, and God knows not for any deserving of his own. Come — read as we walk. Isabetta herself shall be our torch-bearer tonight. [Exeunt.]

SCENE V. The same. Before the house of Mosca, a usurer. Enter Mosca and Gobbo.
MOSCA. Well, thou shalt see it plain enough — see the difference between a house governed with sense and one thrown away in waste and idleness. What, Lisetta! Thou shalt not eat me bare as thou hast lately done — What, Lisetta! — lying abed till noon and tearing through good linen and leaving candles to burn themselves to stubs—
GOBBO. What, Lisetta!
MOSCA. Who told thee to call? I did not bid thee call.
GOBBO. Your worship has always said I'm fit for nothing without being told.
Enter Lisetta.
LISETTA. You called? What do you want?
MOSCA. I am bidden to supper, Lisetta. Take the keys. And yet — why should I go? They don't want me there; they flatter me to my face and laugh behind my back. But I'll go in spite of it, if only to watch that spendthrift's candles burn. Look to the house. I go against my own judgement. Something sits ill with me tonight — I dreamed last night of my strongbox, and not pleasantly.
GOBBO. Go, sir, I beseech you. My young master is expecting you, and has spoken of it.
MOSCA. As I have spoken of him.
GOBBO. There's entertainment planned as well. I won't say outright there's to be a masque — but if there is, it was no accident that my left eye began twitching on St. Crispin's eve at the stroke of midnight, which that year fell on a Wednesday that had been a fast day three years running.
MOSCA. A masque — what? Listen to me, Lisetta. Bolt the shutters. And when you hear the drum start up and those infernal pipes squealing, don't go climbing to the window to gape at painted fools in the street. Stop the house's ears. Let none of that nonsense in. I swear I have no appetite for feasting tonight — and yet I'll go. Go ahead, sirrah, tell them I am coming.
GOBBO. I go, sir. [Aside to Lisetta.] Mistress — keep one eye on the street all the same. There'll be someone worth a young woman's notice passing by before long. [Exit.]
MOSCA. What was that the wretch was muttering?
LISETTA. Only "farewell, mistress." Nothing more.
MOSCA. He's not a bad creature at bottom — but he eats like three men, profits like none, and sleeps away half the day besides. I keep no drones. I've already found him a new place, with a master who will no doubt let him eat his way cheerfully through a borrowed fortune.

The cold night air helped. My head cleared a little, and I walked toward the light in the window with something closer to resolution than I had felt all day. I knocked.
"Who's there?" An old voice — careful, unhurried.
"A stranger. I mean no harm. I ask only a moment's shelter."
"Come in."
The room was small and warm, a single lamp burning on the table. An elderly man sat by the hearth, hands resting on his knees, his eyes directed toward me without seeing.
"Forgive me for disturbing you," I said. "I have been walking a long while and am very tired. If you would let me sit by your fire for a short time, I would ask nothing more."
"Sit down," said the old man — Vernet, I later learned. "I am sorry I cannot offer you more. My daughter has gone to the village and will not be back before dark, and as you can perhaps see, I am not well placed to be of much practical help to a visitor."
"You have given me more than you know by simply opening the door. I have food — it is warmth and rest I need, nothing else."
He nodded. For a while neither of us spoke. I felt the time pressing on me, knowing I could not put off much longer what I had come to do — and yet I could not find where to begin. The old man spoke first.
"You speak in an unusual way," he said, not unkindly. "I cannot quite place you. Are you from this country?"
"No. I was raised in circumstances that gave me your language, but I have no country I could name. I am travelling to find certain people — people who mean everything to me — and from whom I hope, though I am not sure, to receive some welcome."
"Relations?"
"Not by blood. They are the only beings in this world toward whom I feel anything like attachment. I have no others. And these — they do not know me as I am. When they see me, I fear my hopes will end in a single moment."
The old man was quiet. "That is a heavy thing to carry," he said at last. "But do not give way before the moment comes. I have lived long enough to know the worst we imagine seldom falls out quite as we dread."
"You are kind. But my situation is not an ordinary one. These people have been given reason to fear what I am — or what they believe me to be. When they see me, that belief will not soften. I am afraid it will be confirmed in the worst possible way."
"And yet you mean to go to them."
"I have no other hope. And I have not spent my waiting time in idleness — for a long while, much longer than they know, I have done what quiet good I could for them, without acknowledgement or thanks. I had hoped that kindness, even when its source is unknown, leaves some trace in the heart that receives it."
"I believe that it does," said Vernet, slowly. "Goodness, whether it is recognised or not, is never entirely without effect." He turned his blind face toward me. "You say these people live nearby?"
"Very near."
A pause. Then: "If you were to tell me your full situation — plainly and without holding anything back — I might be able to help. I cannot see your face, so I must judge by your words alone. But there is something in the way you speak that inclines me to trust you. I have no influence, no resources to speak of — I have known exile and poverty well enough — but if there is anything I could do, I would do it willingly."
Something moved in me that I had not felt in a very long time. I could not speak immediately.
"You cannot know what it means to hear those words," I managed at last. "I will tell you everything. I will hold nothing back. I will trust in your goodness as I have not been able to trust in anyone's before now."
"Then tell me. But first — your friends. Will you give me their names?"
I stopped. Here it was — the moment everything had been moving toward, through all the cold and dark and loneliness beyond telling. I tried to answer. I formed the words and brought them forward and they came to nothing. The effort of it simply broke something in me. I felt myself fall back against the chair, and a sound came out of me — raw, helpless, beyond any controlling.
And at that moment I heard footsteps on the path outside.

"You were watching me... from the corner of the street?" Svidrigailov said at last, his voice almost too composed. The other man didn't reply, didn't even glance at him. The silence between them went on and on. "Why follow me here and then stand there saying nothing—what do you want?"
There was a slight fracture at the edge of his voice. The man turned and fixed him with a look of such cold, steady contempt that Svidrigailov felt it like a hand pressed flat against his chest.
"Thief." Quietly said. Unhurried, almost gentle—but landing with extraordinary precision.
Svidrigailov kept walking. His feet carried him forward by some automatic function separate from his will, and a strange numbness moved through his hands and up into his jaw. They went on together perhaps two hundred paces without a word, the man always at the same distance, like a shadow with its own intention.
"I don't understand you," Svidrigailov said finally. "You use that word as though you actually witnessed something."
"I did." Calm. Unhurried. Then the man stopped, turned into a narrow side street, and was gone.
Svidrigailov stood at the entrance to the lane. A cab rolled past. A woman with a basket crossed at the far end of the road. The city went on around him with complete indifference.
He returned to his rooms in a state he couldn't properly name—not fear, exactly. He had always prided himself on his relationship with fear, on having looked at it long enough to rob it of its authority. This was something more unsettling. The sensation of having been accurately read, like a letter found that was never meant to be.
He sat at his desk without lighting the lamp, the room darkening slowly around him. His thoughts came in fragments: a garden somewhere in summer, a corridor in a house visited years ago and never thought of since, the sound of water below, a child's shoe lying in mud. The images weren't distressing. They drifted past with a kind of dreary indolence.
He lit a cigarette eventually and sat smoking in the dark.
"A man who saw," he said aloud, to no one. "But what exactly did he see?"
He turned the question over with methodical patience—the way you take apart a mechanism to understand why it's stopped. The man's face had been composed. Not the face of someone acting on impulse or private grievance, but a face that had already concluded something, had long passed through whatever agitation the knowledge first produced and arrived at some settled, implacable resolution.
That was what troubled him. Not the accusation itself, which might be traced back and handled. The composure was what troubled him—it suggested something finished, already set irrevocably in motion.
He pressed the cigarette into the ashtray and watched the ember go out. "I should have been more careful," he said, in a tone that was neither regret nor self-reproach—just accurate retrospective assessment. "Though of course I was never the sort to be careful." He almost smiled. "That was always the difficulty."

GRUMIO. I told the steward your uncle was in Florence, expected here by Thursday at the latest.
FABIAN. Sharp work—here, take that for thy trouble. Now look to yourself, here comes old Orsino.
Enter Orsino and Valentio.
Good Signior Orsino, well met indeed. (To the Merchant) This is the very gentleman I spoke of, sir. Stand as a father to me now, I beg you, and help me win fair Sophia for my wife.
MERCHANT. Softly, son. Sir, your pardon—I came to Verona on a matter of debts when my son Valentio made me aware of this weighty business between himself and your niece. Given your good name, and the love they bear each other, I am content—as any father who wishes his son well settled—to hear the terms. You'll find me willing, and not hard to please.
ORSINO. I must speak plainly, sir, and I trust you'll not take offence. Your directness suits me. It is true enough that Sophia loves your son, or else they are both accomplished liars—and I do not think them so. If you mean to deal in earnest, with proper settlement and a sufficient dower, then the matter is concluded: she shall be his, and with my blessing.
FABIAN. I thank you heartily. But where shall we draw up these articles and set our hands to them, so that all is properly witnessed and binding?
ORSINO. Not here—servants have long memories and longer ears, and besides, old Curio will be sniffing about before the ink is dry.
FABIAN. Then at my lodgings, if that please you. My father is there; we'll conclude the whole affair tonight, quietly, and without interruption. Send for Sophia at once; I'll have a scrivener fetched directly. I warn you only—at such short notice, supper may be a slender affair.
ORSINO. That's no matter. You, Feste—ride home this instant and tell Sophia to make ready. And if you like, tell her why: Valentio's father has come to Verona, and she is as good as Valentio's wife.
VALENTIO. God grant it, with all my heart!
FABIAN. Save your prayers for later and get thee gone. Signior Orsino, shall I lead the way? A plain welcome tonight—we'll do better when we meet again in Padua.
ORSINO. I follow you.
Exeunt Fabian, Merchant, and Orsino.
GRUMIO. Valentio!
VALENTIO. What is it, Grumio?
GRUMIO. Did you see Fabian winking at you just now?
VALENTIO. I saw it. What of it?
GRUMIO. Nothing much—only he's left me here to make sense of it all, so here it is: Orsino is at this moment happily talking with a false father on behalf of a son who is nothing of the sort.
VALENTIO. And?
GRUMIO. His niece is to be brought to supper by you.
VALENTIO. Yes, and?
GRUMIO. The chaplain at Saint Ambrose's keeps no fixed hours and answers to nobody in particular.
VALENTIO. Come to the point, man.
GRUMIO. The point is this: that business indoors is a counterfeit, meant to keep everyone occupied. Your business is of a different kind—take the lady to the chapel, take the priest, take two honest witnesses. If that is not what you have been waiting for, I have nothing more to say, and you can say your goodbyes to Sophia here and now.
(Turning to go.)
VALENTIO. Grumio, wait—
GRUMIO. I cannot stop. I once knew a woman married on a Tuesday afternoon while she was sent out to fetch mint for a sauce—and you have more to work with than she did. My master has sent me ahead to Saint Ambrose's to have the priest ready and waiting.
Exit.
VALENTIO. I will go—gladly, if she is willing. And she will be. Why should I doubt it? Come what may, I mean to have her.
Exit.

The house wasn't yet in sight when the last of the evening withdrew behind the treeline, leaving only a pale residue of light along the horizon. But the lane was bright under the full moon—I could make out the ruts in the mud, the Queen Anne's lace trembling at the verge. The gate was unlatched, recently passed through or simply never fastened anymore, and I went through without difficulty.
The garden had been attended to since my last visit. I noticed the smell first—something sweet and almost embarrassingly abundant, roses perhaps, or honeysuckle on the near wall—and the grass had been cut low on either side of the path. The windows stood open to the warm air, curtains lifting at intervals, and though the evening was mild a fire burned somewhere within; I could see its red suggestion at the far end of the hall, and woodsmoke mingled with the garden.
I didn't go in. Something in the arrangement of light and voices checked my step, and I stood on the path a moment, unwilling to announce myself.
Two people occupied the room nearest the window. A young woman sat in the larger chair, feet drawn up, a book open but forgotten in her lap. A man—older by perhaps a decade, with a dark, careful face—sat across from her on a low stool, elbows on his knees, watching her with an attention he probably didn't know was visible. They were arguing, lightly, in the way people argue when they are more interested in each other than in the argument itself.
"You said yesterday you didn't mind it," the woman said.
"I said I didn't mind it then. That is a different thing entirely."
"You are always making these distinctions."
"One of us ought to."
She laughed. The sound came cleanly through the open window to where I stood, and I felt that particular kind of loneliness that is not quite sadness—closer to the sensation of arriving late somewhere you had expected to belong.
I went round to the back, as I had always done, and found the kitchen door open and old Mrs. Alderton at the table with her mending, spectacles low on her nose, a tallow candle burning beside her. She looked up, and her expression moved through surprise, pleasure, and reproof in quick succession.
"Lord above, Mr. Ashford—walking in like a ghost and no word sent! I could have put you up at the Lamb and Flag easily enough if only I'd known. I can't answer for the state of the room as it is."
"I shan't need much," I said. "I mean to leave again by Thursday—I only came to see how things stood. How long have you been keeping the house?"
She set down her mending and folded her hands in the manner of someone who has a great deal to say and is deciding where to begin.
"Since Michaelmas. After the other woman went—you'll have heard about that—and the new arrangement was settled. It's a different sort of place now, Mr. Ashford. Very different from when you were last here."
I pulled out the chair opposite and sat down. Without my asking, she rose to put the kettle on, which I took as a good sign.
"Tell me everything," I said.

No, upon my honour—purely for the occasion. Sir, there has newly come to this city one Silvano; a most accomplished gentleman, believe me, replete with every fine distinction, of gracious address and remarkable presence. To speak of him with any justice, he is the very map and measure of courtly virtue—in him you will find the full sum of whatsoever quality a gentleman might covet.
PRINCE. Your account of him loses nothing in the telling, sir, though to anatomise him point by point would exhaust the patience of a scholar and still fall short. But in the earnest spirit of praise, I hold him a person of extraordinary composition, his qualities of so rare a stamp that his only true likeness is his own reflection, and any who would follow his example must be content to walk in shadow.
CORVINO. Your highness does him perfect justice.
PRINCE. But what is the substance of it? Why do we dress the gentleman in so much ceremony?
CORVINO. My lord?
BENEDETTO. Is there no shorter road to a meaning? You will find one, sir, I am quite confident.
PRINCE. What is the occasion for naming this gentleman at all?
CORVINO. Of Silvano?
BENEDETTO. Every fine word he had is spent. The purse is empty.
PRINCE. Of him, sir.
CORVINO. I trust your highness is not unacquainted with the excellence of Silvano—
PRINCE. I would not dare claim it, lest I be thought to rival him. Besides, to truly know another man requires knowing oneself, which is a considerably longer undertaking.
CORVINO. I speak specifically of his skill with the blade, my lord. In that particular, by the estimation of those best placed to judge, he stands without equal.
PRINCE. What arms does he favour?
CORVINO. Sword and main-gauche.
PRINCE. Two arms. Go on.
CORVINO. The Duke has laid against him four Neapolitan chargers, to which Silvano has opposed four Venetian blades with full appointments—belts, chapes, all the furnishings—three of which are, I confess, extraordinarily fine, very sympathetic to the hand, and of the most ingenious workmanship.
PRINCE. What do you call the furnishings?
BENEDETTO. I knew we should need a gloss before it was over.
CORVINO. The furnishings, my lord—the belt-fittings and mountings.
PRINCE. The word might suit the matter better if we were equipping a siege. Let us say fittings. But proceed—four horses against four blades: that is the southern stake against the northern. What exactly are the terms?
CORVINO. In a course of ten passes, Silvano shall not surpass your highness by more than two hits. Ten against eight, my lord. It would come to trial at once, if your highness consented.
PRINCE. And if I should decline?
CORVINO. I speak only of your appearance in the contest itself, my lord.
PRINCE. I shall remain here in the gallery, sir. This is my customary hour for exercise; if it please the Duke, let the weapons be brought, let Silvano be willing, and I will do my best on the Duke's behalf. If I fall short, I lose nothing but my credit and the margin of hits.
CORVINO. Shall I carry this answer back?
PRINCE. To that effect—with whatever flourish your nature requires.
CORVINO. I commend my most devoted service to your highness.
PRINCE. Yours, entirely.
Exit Corvino.
He does well to advertise his own devotion. There is no one else who would think to do it for him.
BENEDETTO. The man hatched before he was ready and has been running about with the shell on his brow ever since.
PRINCE. He was making courtly speeches before he could properly walk. There are a dozen more of the same flock this age admires so extravagantly—fellows who have caught nothing but the pitch and fashion of the time, a frothy collection of surface manners with no substance beneath. Set them to any genuine trial and they dissolve like foam.
Enter a Servant.
SERVANT. My lord, the Duke commends himself to your highness, and desires to know whether your intention to meet Silvano holds, or whether you would have more time appointed.
PRINCE. My purpose does not change. I am at the Duke's disposal.

Methinks your belly, like a clock well-wound, Should toll the hour and bring you home to meat Without a boy dispatched to carry notice.
LEONATO OF MILAN. Come, Balthazar, these quips are poorly timed. Keep them for some occasion fit for mirth. Where is the letter that I gave to thee?
BALTHAZAR OF NAPLES. What letter, sir? You gave no letter me.
LEONATO OF MILAN. Have done with playing at confusion, come— And tell me plainly what you've done with it.
BALTHAZAR OF NAPLES. My charge was only this: to bring you straight From the exchange to supper at the Dolphin. Your cousin and his wife are waiting, sir.
LEONATO OF MILAN. Now by my faith, I'll have a clear account Of where you've put the paper in your keeping, Or I shall find a remedy for that grin You wear when I am vexed. The letter—where?
BALTHAZAR OF NAPLES. I carry marks enough upon my back From fetching and from being knocked about, But no such letter as you speak of, sir. Letters bring me nothing but more labour.
LEONATO OF MILAN. What cousin? Who waits? Whose relation is this?
BALTHAZAR OF NAPLES. Your worship's own, sir, lodging at the Dolphin— Who will not eat till you come in to supper And sends me out to fetch you every hour.
LEONATO OF MILAN. What, dost thou mock me to my face again? Take that for thy impertinence, thou rogue.
BALTHAZAR OF NAPLES. For pity's sake, sir—hold! Nay, if you will not, I'll trust my legs.
Exit Balthazar.
LEONATO OF MILAN. Upon my soul, there is some villainy here— Some trick by which this rascal has been turned Or plays this game of confusion for himself. They say this city swarms with subtle cheats, With forgers, cutpurses, and whispering rogues That take a man apart from what is his Before he knows which street he's standing in. If so, I'll not stay long. I'll to the Dolphin, Find out this fellow, and be satisfied.
Exit.

ACT II, SCENE I. A room in the Dolphin.
Enter Beatrice, wife to Leonato of Naples, and Marta, her younger sister.
BEATRICE. Neither my husband back, nor yet the boy I sent in haste to find him? Two o'clock, Marta—the bells struck it not a moment past.
MARTA. Some merchant of the city may have kept him; The exchange pulls men aside at every turn. Come, sister, let us eat and think no more on it. A man goes where occasion calls him out, And time itself the only rein he knows. He'll come when it suits time. Be easy, sister.
BEATRICE. Why should their ease be always more than ours?
MARTA. Their business leads them ever out of doors.
BEATRICE. When I stay out, he takes it very ill.
MARTA. He is the governor of your own will.
BEATRICE. None but an ass submits to such a bit.
MARTA. But headstrong will is sorely lashed for it. There's nothing under heaven's open eye But has its bound—on earth, in sea, in sky. The hawk, the hound, the horse beneath the goad Are subject to the hand that rules the road. And man, that calls himself the lord of these, Master of land and of the rolling seas, By nature's own decree is set above, And woman by his side—so cease to chafe, my love.
BEATRICE. Such doctrine's why you've never taken a husband.
MARTA. Not doctrine—what I've watched men do with one.
BEATRICE. Were you once married, you'd assert your share.
MARTA. First I would learn to bow before I dare.
BEATRICE. And if your husband wandered far and wide?
MARTA. I'd hold my tongue until he came inside.
BEATRICE. How very still—and cheap the virtue is When you've no faithless mate to make it his. A soul bent double underneath a weight Is hushed by those who never bore the same; But load those patient shoulders as I'm loaded And patience shrinks to nothing, I'll be bound. You, with no wound to keep you up at night, Bid me be calm—it costs you nothing. Right. But live to know such usage, sister, once, And see how long this cherished peace endures.
MARTA. I may be wed one day and put it to the proof. But look—your servant comes. Your husband's near.

Like men possessed they hauled and heaved, every muscle cracking, until the cry went up—"Now, Queequeg!—drive it home!" The lance flew. "Back water!" The oars bit hard in reverse, and in that same instant something scorched and singing swept the length of every man's forearms. The line, alive and running.
A breath before, Flask had whipped three fast turns round the loggerhead, and from those turns, by the violence of its speed, a thin grey smoke twisted up into the salt air. Where the line burned round the post it burned also through both of Flask's palms, the cloth he'd wrapped there having worked loose and fallen away. It was the sensation of gripping a wire stretched to breaking—not quite pain at first, but a heat so immediate it registered somewhere beyond ordinary feeling.
"Water on the line!" Flask shouted. The nearest man flung his bailing can across the thwart and sloshed a gout of sea over the smoking hemp.
More wraps were taken. The line steadied. The boat leapt forward with a violence that seemed to lift her clean off the surface, throwing spray in white continuous sheets from her bows while the stern churned behind. Flask and Queequeg changed places—a desperate, lunging exchange in that pitching shell of a boat, each man passing the other in a space barely wide enough to stand. Stem to stern the line ran rigid as iron, and the whole craft shuddered along its length at every surge. A man could have believed she had no keel at all, that she was simply a thing of tension held to the water by the angle of her strain.
Nobody spoke. The spray came constantly. Ahead, the dark shape of the whale moved through the swells like a travelling hill—immense, purposeful, apparently without end.
Then, gradually, the pull began to slacken.
"Haul in—haul in!" Flask called forward, and the bowsman worked hand over hand, and the others bent to their oars, and foot by foot the boat closed with the whale until they came up along his side—close enough to see the scarred hide, the barnacles, the slow heave of him. Flask braced his knee against the forward thwart and drove his lance again and again into the rolling flank, the boat backing and ranging at each command, always keeping clear of that tremendous tail.
The blood came freely. It ran in dark streams down the whale's sides and spread across the water behind them in a great stain that the afternoon light turned deep copper. The men's faces caught that reflection from the surface; looking at one another across the thwarts, each face was red, as though they laboured not by daylight but by the light of something burning. The whale's breath came in great convulsive jets—white vapour erupting upward at long intervals, each one followed by a silence that felt like suspension—and Flask breathed almost with it, his own exhalations coming hard and fast as he worked.
At last the animal began to slow in earnest. To wallow rather than run. Flask leaned out over the bow and set the long lance with both hands and drove it deep, working it with slow deliberate turns—searching, careful, unhurried now that the moment had come. He was looking for the life that lay deepest in the animal. The centre of it. The place from which nothing returns.
He found it.
The whale convulsed with a violence almost beyond reckoning—a rolling, churning detonation of blood and water that obliterated everything within thirty yards—and the boat was driven back hard through the white chaos, every man holding on with his eyes shut, until they broke into the clear air beyond. Behind them the sea was a wide, spreading stain. Quiet now. The long business finished.

Margaret had little time to dwell on her first surprise before another followed it. Mr. Hartwell, whose manners had once seemed guarded almost to coldness, was speaking with her aunt and uncle as though no awkwardness existed between them at all. She had expected him to be civil; she had not expected this.
When her aunt mentioned their connection to her, Margaret watched him almost involuntarily. She wondered whether the knowledge would alter his behaviour. If it did, the change was slight. A momentary look of surprise crossed his face, but it passed quickly. Rather than withdrawing, he turned again to her uncle and continued the conversation with evident interest.
The effect on Margaret was immediate. It pleased her more than she cared to admit. There was comfort in seeing that those nearest to her could meet his notice without causing embarrassment to either side.
She listened closely. Her uncle spoke with his usual good sense, easy humour, and quiet confidence. Whenever Mr. Hartwell responded warmly to one of his remarks, she felt a private satisfaction she could not entirely suppress.
The conversation drifted to the estate and the surrounding country. Mr. Hartwell pointed out several recent improvements and urged Mr. Benson to examine them whenever he wished. He offered plans of the grounds, spoke of future projects, and seemed genuinely eager to continue the discussion.
Mrs. Benson caught her niece's eye. The look she gave required no explanation.
Margaret, however, had none to offer. She was still asking herself the same question. What accounted for this difference? His reserve had not disappeared altogether, but it no longer governed every word and action. She could not persuade herself that she was the cause. The idea seemed too improbable.
Their party changed formation a short while later. Mrs. Benson, tired by the walk, took her husband's arm instead, and Margaret found herself beside Mr. Hartwell.
For several moments they walked in silence.
At last she remarked that his arrival had been unexpected. She had been assured he would not return until the next day.
He admitted as much and explained that business with his steward had brought him back sooner than planned.
“The others will arrive tomorrow,” he said. “Some of them are already known to you.”
Margaret acknowledged this with a slight inclination of her head.
After a pause, he added, “There is another person among them whom I hope you will allow me to introduce. My sister has a strong wish to make your acquaintance while you remain here.”
The proposal startled her. For a second she could think of no answer at all.
Whatever interest his sister might have taken in her, it was impossible not to suspect the source. The thought carried a quiet satisfaction. At the very least, it suggested he did not view her with the unfavourable opinion she had once feared.
They continued on without much conversation. Margaret was not entirely at ease, but she was undeniably pleased. The invitation itself felt significant.
Before long they reached the carriage, well ahead of the others. Mr. Hartwell invited her to go inside and rest, but she declined. The air was mild, the evening calm. They remained on the lawn together, saying little, while each seemed occupied by thoughts better left unspoken.

The effort to master himself was beginning to fail. Though he sat motionless by the fire, there was no mistaking the strain in him. His hands shook now and then, and his gaze remained fixed on the embers as if he expected them to answer him. The room had grown very still.
I watched longer than I should have. At last I spoke.
“You grieve for her now,” I said, “but you gave her little peace while she was here.”
He looked up. The expression might once have intimidated me; grief had worn it thin.
“Go,” he said quietly. “Leave me.”
“I will, in time.”
The answer escaped me before prudence could intervene. “But do not pretend you alone have the right to remember her. Others loved her too.”
For a moment he said nothing.
I continued, emboldened by his silence. “You speak as though devotion excuses everything. It does not. She saw more than you believed she did.”
That reached him.
He rose suddenly, and I retreated a step toward the door.
“Enough,” he said.
Still I persisted. “Had she lived, she might have judged you differently in the end.”
A book was the nearest object to his hand. He snatched it up and threw it. It struck the wall beside me with a force that ended the conversation more effectively than any argument.
I was already backing away.
At the threshold I turned once.
“People obey you because they fear you,” I said. “That is not the same thing.”
Then I was gone.
I heard furniture crash behind me, followed by a sound that was neither wholly rage nor wholly sorrow. I had no wish to discover which. Through the yard and across the fields I ran without stopping.
Only when the house had vanished behind the hills did I slow. The air was sharp and cold, but it felt like freedom. I would gladly have spent the night out on the moor rather than return.
The narrator rested for a moment before continuing.
Not long afterward she left the area altogether. She settled far away with relatives, and for a few years letters arrived with some regularity. Later they became rarer. We eventually learned she had remarried and had a daughter of fragile health, though by all accounts a cheerful disposition.
The man she left behind remained much the same to outward appearances. Occasionally he would ask after her. Sometimes he sought news indirectly, pretending little interest in the answer.
“Let her stay where she has chosen to be,” he said to me once.
The words sounded indifferent enough. Yet there was something in the way he spoke them that suggested indifference was the last thing he felt.
As the years passed, her name came up less often. When it did, he usually fell silent. Time had changed many things around him, but not that.

Enter ARISTON alone.
ARISTON.
Let them feast without me.
The same men who crowded round my table will scarcely spare a thought now that my coffers are empty. While the wine flowed, I was their dearest friend. Now the gold is spent, and so are their affections.
How readily I believed them.
Every hall that once rang with greetings and laughter seems different in memory. I mistook noise for loyalty. A pleasant word, a flattering smile, a show of concern—these passed for friendship in my eyes.
No longer.
Enter STEWARD and two SERVANTS.
STEWARD.
My lord, we have searched everywhere for you.
ARISTON.
And why?
STEWARD.
The house is in disorder. Creditors press their claims. The gates are watched, the servants uncertain what is to become of them. We feared you had left us altogether.
FIRST SERVANT.
It troubles us deeply to see your fortunes changed so suddenly.
ARISTON.
Do not waste pity on me. What has happened has at least taught me something. Prosperity gathered people around me; adversity has shown me who they were.
The borrower forgets. The flatterer disappears. Those who swore they loved me cannot even remember the words they spoke.
SECOND SERVANT.
Surely not everyone is false.
ARISTON.
Perhaps not. Yet I looked for sincerity and found performance. This city values appearances above all things. Gratitude is traded away as easily as coin.
STEWARD.
What will you do now?
ARISTON.
Leave.
Let others chase favour and fortune. I have no appetite for either. The forests are more honest than crowded streets. Trees ask nothing, promise nothing, and deceive no one.
FIRST SERVANT.
Allow us to come with you.
ARISTON.
No. You must think of yourselves. Keep what work you can find and make better use of trust than I did.
STEWARD.
Then may heaven watch over you.
ARISTON.
Heaven has been a distant observer. Still, farewell.
Exit ARISTON.
FIRST SERVANT.
I never thought I would see him brought to this.
SECOND SERVANT.
His wealth failed him; his friends failed him sooner.
STEWARD.
Come. There is still much to set in order. When fortune leaves a house, it takes most of its visitors with it. What remains is seldom comfort, but sometimes it is truth.
Exeunt.

If such customs are defended as acts of religion, they become an argument against themselves. Human beings do not entirely lose their sense of right and wrong, even when vice has become familiar. However corrupt a society may be, people still recognize virtue when they encounter it. Because of this, those who wish to mislead others rarely present evil in its true form. They dress it in respectable language. Temperance is praised, while excess is tolerated. Humility is admired in speeches, but pride receives the applause.
The greater influence comes not from what is taught but from what is displayed. A quiet lesson reaches a few listeners; a public spectacle reaches thousands. When honorable principles are confined to words and dishonorable conduct is turned into entertainment, the example outweighs the instruction. People remember what they see celebrated.
One may observe this in public festivals. Crowds gather, sacred ceremonies are performed, and divine names are invoked with solemnity. Yet alongside these rites appear amusements that excite vanity, appetite, and passion. Those attending are encouraged to regard such things as acceptable, even admirable. It should surprise no one if they leave less disciplined than when they arrived.
Human beings learn powerfully through imitation. If modesty is praised but shamelessness is put on display, the display leaves the deeper impression. If self-restraint is commended while indulgence is rewarded with attention and admiration, indulgence becomes the model that many follow. The public example quietly defeats the spoken rule.
What sort of spirit delights in this arrangement? Certainly not one devoted to goodness. No sincere guide would encourage corruption while claiming to improve character. It is like offering poison under the name of medicine.
Nor should we wonder when societies suffer the consequences. People become diligent in ceremonies yet careless about virtue. They fear neglecting a festival more than neglecting justice. They concern themselves with outward observances while greed, dishonesty, and excess spread unchecked.
A nation may possess strong defenses, wealthy institutions, and every appearance of stability. Yet when moral discipline decays, the danger is already within. External enemies can do great harm, but the corruption that receives public approval is often more destructive than any army at the gates.

At first she felt a little uneasy. The two strange creatures had edged so close that she could hear one of them breathing through its nose. The other stared at her with such interest that she wondered whether she had accidentally become part of the entertainment.
Still, they seemed harmless enough, so she went on with her story.
They listened without interruption until she described the clock that insisted on announcing tomorrow before today. That detail produced a strong reaction.
“Well, that is odd,” said the larger creature after a moment.
“Odd?” said the smaller. “It is wonderfully odd.”
“The clock truly did that?”
Alice nodded.
“I should like to hear her recite something,” the larger creature decided.
His companion immediately pointed at her.
“Stand up and say a poem.”
Alice thought this extremely unfair. Everyone she met seemed determined to assign her lessons.
Even so, she stood and tried to comply. The trouble was that her mind was still occupied with impossible clocks, and the poem emerged rather differently than intended:
“The curious Bee beneath the sea
Polished his wings with jam;
He borrowed a hat from a fishing cat
And feasted on lavender ham.”
“That sounds wrong,” remarked the larger creature.
“It certainly sounds unusual,” said the smaller.
Alice sat down again.
“What does it mean?” asked the larger creature.
“I have no idea.”
“Then why recite it?”
“Because you asked me to.”
Neither creature seemed able to argue with that, though both looked disappointed.
“Another verse,” said the smaller one.
With a sigh, Alice continued:
“Beyond the hill where teacups grow,
An Owl and a Minnow played cards;
The Owl lost twice, and the Minnow paid
In buttons and peppermint shards.”
“Can a minnow carry buttons?” the larger creature wondered aloud.
“On special occasions, certainly,” replied the smaller.
This led to a debate so lengthy and so confusing that they soon forgot what had started it. When at last the discussion collapsed under its own weight, the larger creature announced that poetry had been exhausted and that music was required.
“A song!” cried the smaller creature enthusiastically.
Without further ceremony he launched into one:
“O marvelous Pie, so round and high,
Drifting across the sky!
Who would not trade a treasure chest
For one delightful bite of Pie?
Pie in the morning,
Pie in the rain,
Pie by the windowpane!”
He was preparing to repeat the chorus when a distant shout interrupted him.
“The inspection is starting!”
The effect was immediate. Both creatures leapt up.
“The inspection!” they cried.
And before Alice could ask what was being inspected, they had vanished, leaving her alone and no nearer to understanding anything than she had been before.

The ship drove hard across the swells, her bow throwing up sheets of spray as she carved through the water. Every beam and spar complained under the strain, but there was something eager in her motion. She seemed to lean into the chase. Around the deck the crew worked with growing impatience, their attention returning again and again to the horizon.
A cry rang down from the masthead.
“There! Off the larboard bow!”
At once the mood changed. Men stopped what they were doing. A few stepped to the rail. Others shaded their eyes and searched the distance. The call had awakened the same hope in all of them.
The captain looked ahead without speaking. After a brief pause he gave his order.
“Hold the course. Keep your eyes on that water.”
Far off, a patch of white turbulence marked the sea. It was small enough to vanish if one looked away for a moment, yet everyone aboard understood what it meant. Something immense had surfaced and slipped below again.
The sight was enough. Days of uncertainty faded from memory. Fatigue no longer mattered. The chase, which had stretched on through long watches and empty horizons, suddenly felt alive again.
Wind pressed heavily into the sails. The vessel answered, surging forward. The rigging hummed, the masts flexed, and a faint shudder ran beneath every footstep on deck.
Then the lookout shouted once more.
This time nobody doubted.
The whale burst from the sea in a tremendous leap. Water streamed from its body as it rose, suspended for a heartbeat against the bright sky. Then it crashed down. The impact rolled across the water like cannon fire, and a wide circle of foam exploded around it.
A roar went up from the crew.
Men pointed and called to one another. Some laughed from sheer excitement. The creature seemed almost unreal—less a living thing than a sudden manifestation of the ocean itself.
The captain watched in silence.
At last he spoke.
“There is the one we seek.”
His words carried across the deck. No speech was needed after that. Every man knew his part. Drawn together by the same purpose, ship and crew pressed on through the wind and sunlight, following the dark shape moving ahead across the open sea.

“There is nothing uncertain about it,” said Marianne. “I mean every word. He asked me to marry him, and I agreed.”
Her sister looked at her for a long moment.
“Marianne, I can hardly comprehend it. Not very long ago you spoke of him with so little favour.”
Marianne smiled.
“Then you must forget what I said before. I have done so myself. People change their opinions when they learn more of one another. At least, I have.”
The explanation did not entirely remove her sister’s surprise.
“You truly mean it?”
“Entirely.”
“My dear Marianne,” she said, squeezing her hand, “then I am happy for you. But allow me one question. Are you quite certain this attachment is strong enough to make you happy?”
“It is stronger than I ever expected it could be.”
That answer seemed to satisfy her.
“Then I have no further concerns. Still, you have been remarkably secretive. I see now that there were signs, but I never understood them.”
“I scarcely understood them myself,” Marianne admitted. “The change happened so gradually that I cannot point to any exact moment when it began.”
The conversation continued well into the evening. Together they revisited recent events, discovering meanings that had escaped them at the time. By the end, there was little left unexplained.
The next morning their mother happened to glance from the drawing-room window.
“Well!” she cried. “Here come our visitors again.”
She watched them approach the house with evident amusement.
“One might think they had no other destination. Not that I object. Their company is always agreeable enough.”
Marianne lowered her eyes, hoping no one would notice the colour rising in her cheeks.
When the gentlemen entered, greetings were exchanged all around. One spoke with particular warmth to her sister, while the other directed most of his attention toward Marianne, though with studied restraint.
Before long their mother proposed a walk.
“You should all go to the hill beyond the meadow,” she said. “The air is pleasant, and the view is worth seeing.”
Her younger daughter immediately shook her head.
“No, thank you. I would rather stay here.”
“Then stay,” said her mother. “The rest need not be deprived of an outing on your account.”
The proposal appeared perfectly innocent. Yet the quiet looks exchanged across the room suggested that some among the party found it especially welcome.

“You need something to occupy you,” said the doctor. “Work, study, any clear purpose. A man recovers faster when he has somewhere to direct his thoughts.”
Nikolai gave a faint nod.
“Yes. I suppose that is true. I will return to the university before long. Perhaps once I fall back into a routine, things will seem less complicated.”
The doctor appeared pleased by the answer, though only for a moment. When he glanced at his patient, he thought he caught a hint of amusement—or perhaps mockery—in his face. It disappeared so quickly that he could not be certain.
His mother intervened before the silence became awkward.
“We are deeply grateful to you,” she said. “Especially for calling on us last evening.”
Nikolai looked up.
“You visited them last night? Then you have not had much rest yourself.”
“It was no great sacrifice,” the doctor replied lightly.
Nikolai frowned and looked down at his hands.
“What puzzles me is why everyone concerns themselves so much with me. I know I ought to be thankful. Still, I cannot quite understand it. The kindness itself becomes a burden when I feel I have done nothing to earn it.”
The doctor laughed.
“There is no mystery. Young doctors become attached to their first patients. We take an unreasonable interest in them. And, to be honest, I am not so busy that I can afford indifference.”
Nikolai gestured toward his friend.
“And he has endured even more. Trouble, inconvenience, endless demands—nothing else.”
“Nonsense,” his friend said at once. “You speak as though I have been performing a great service.”
His sister remained quiet. She had been watching him all this time. There was warmth in what he said, yet something restrained beneath it, something she could not quite name.
After a moment he turned to his mother.
“I understand now how worried you must have been yesterday. At least a little. I did not think of it before.”
Then he reached out his hand to his sister.
Nothing dramatic accompanied the gesture, but it was enough. She took his hand immediately, her face brightening with relief. The quarrel that had stood between them seemed to dissolve without another word.
Their mother could hardly contain her happiness.
For a few moments she simply looked at him. His clothes were shabby, his face drawn from fatigue, and yet she found herself thinking how dear he was to her. No outward disorder could alter that.
The thought stirred her into speech once more.
“You have no idea what yesterday was like for us. We arrived eager to see you and learned almost at once that you had gone out while ill. They said you were wandering about in a fever. People were searching everywhere.”
She paused, remembering.
“I imagined every possible disaster. The longer you were gone, the worse my fears became. I do not think I have ever passed such anxious hours.”
No one interrupted her. The depth of her distress was evident, and the room grew quiet as she spoke.

The journey was my own idea, though Clara agreed to it reluctantly. What troubled her was not the travel itself but the thought of my being left alone with my thoughts. She had arranged for Ashton to accompany me, hoping his good spirits would keep me from sinking further into melancholy. It was a thoughtful plan, though at the time I doubted anyone could lighten the mood in which I found myself.
On the morning of my departure she tried to appear calm. A few words passed between us, neither of us saying quite what we meant. Then I was gone. The road led through pleasant country—wooded hills, bright rivers, fields warmed by the sun—but I paid little attention to any of it. My mind was occupied elsewhere, fixed on the purpose that had drawn me from home.
I waited several days at a frontier town before Ashton arrived. From the moment we resumed our travels together, the difference between us became impossible to ignore. Everything interested him. A marketplace, a ruined tower, even a crowded ferry landing could hold his attention for an hour. He had a way of finding something worth admiring wherever he looked.
One evening, while we walked beside the river, he laughed and told me I passed through the world half asleep.
“There is beauty everywhere,” he said. “You look directly at it and never seem to see it.”
The criticism was fair. I had become so accustomed to my own worries that I scarcely noticed what surrounded me. Still, as he pointed out the fading colors in the sky and the reflections scattered across the water, I found myself listening with unexpected interest.
We traveled onward by boat. The river wound through fertile country where vineyards climbed the slopes and old stone fortresses stood watch above the banks. Sometimes the scenery was wild and austere, marked by cliffs and dark forests. A bend in the river would reveal an entirely different prospect—open fields, villages, and church spires rising from among clusters of trees.
Harvest season was underway. Songs drifted from the fields. We saw wagons heavy with fruit and workers gathering crops beneath the late afternoon sun. There was something reassuring in these ordinary scenes. For the first time in many months, I felt a faint easing of the pressure that had weighed upon me.
One afternoon clouds gathered over the western hills as we floated beneath a line of wooded heights. The breeze freshened, rippling the surface of the river. Leaning against the railing, I watched the changing light and, for a little while, forgot to dwell on what lay ahead. The relief was brief, but it was real.
Ashton remained delighted by everything. Every town suggested a story, every distant road another destination. He spoke eagerly of future travels and places he hoped one day to see. Listening to him, I envied the openness with which he met the world.
As darkness settled over the water, he made a remark that stayed with me.
“People spend their lives searching for wonders,” he said. “The rarer gift may be never losing the ability to be surprised by them.”
The river disappeared into the night, but those words remained long after the last light had gone.

For a decade now, the West has wrung its hands over Xi Jinping. They have good reason to.
Xi and his inner circle have spent the last ten years growing steadily bolder. They've militarized artificial reefs in the South China Sea, dismantled Hong Kong's democratic guardrails, and weaponized trade to punish their critics. Through it all, Beijing has stopped pretending it cares what the West thinks. Why should it? Expansionism has cost them very little.
Lately, the world has been getting a masterclass in Chinese confidence. Tech supply chains are bottlenecking as Beijing squeezes critical mineral exports. Fighter jets routinely cross the median line in the Taiwan Strait. And as this happens, it’s worth asking how Western policymakers misread Xi so completely—and why they kept ignoring the weapons he had quietly stockpiled.
Chief among those weapons is a staggering leverage over the global supply chain, specifically rare earth elements.
It’s the worst-kept secret in global manufacturing. The US and Europe are hopelessly dependent on Chinese minerals to build everything from an iPhone to an electric vehicle. The recent export curbs didn't just expose an economic vulnerability; they laid bare a massive geopolitical risk.
Beijing could ease these quotas tomorrow. Analysts point out that flooding the market would stabilize global manufacturing and calm panicked tech sectors ahead of the green energy transition. But from Xi's perspective, what's the incentive?
China is using its market dominance to exact political silence. State media hasn't been subtle about it, heavily implying that if Washington drops its tech sanctions and tariffs, the minerals will flow again. They blame the current supply chain mess entirely on American protectionism. It's a strategy designed to force a choice: secure your economies, or keep lecturing us about Taiwan. Chinese officials even suggested recently that showing "mutual respect" regarding their territorial claims would magically "stabilize" the global economy.
But this isn't just about global leverage. It feeds a story the CCP has been telling its citizens for years: the West is dying.
"The core of the narrative is that democracies are paralyzed and failing," one East Asian geopolitical analyst told me recently. Ten years ago, the CCP used that argument defensively, mostly to deflect human rights criticism. Now, they point to America's polarized elections and Europe's stagnant growth as proof. They aren't just defending their authoritarian model anymore; they're marketing it as a superior alternative.
You have to look at this through the lens of the CCP's historical grievances. Xi's entire mandate is built on the idea of "national rejuvenation"—avenging the "Century of Humiliation."
Look at his track record through that specific filter. The blue-water navy build-up, the debt-trap loans in the global south, the aggressive intercepts of foreign aircraft. It’s the playbook of a leader desperate to claw back his country's status as the center of the world, perfectly happy to run over naïve Western diplomats to get there.
"Since the 2008 financial crisis, the CCP leadership decided the global balance of power had permanently fractured," notes a prominent scholar based in the region. Once Xi consolidated power, he accelerated the timeline. "That’s why you see coercive diplomacy and intellectual property theft happening out in the open. Beijing views this as a zero-sum strategic competition. The West is still trying to manage it like a tricky trade dispute."
That disconnect—treating China as an economic rival rather than a systemic threat—has bred a uniquely weak Western foreign policy. Xi takes aggressive steps, the West protests, and nothing fundamentally changes.
The primary failure of the US and Europe has been treating each Chinese provocation as a one-off event. They miss the forest for the trees. Beijing has absolutely no interest in integrating into the American-led international order. They want to rewrite the rules entirely.
And they are getting louder about it. When Beijing cuts off minerals to punish a political rival or slaps sanctions on European parliamentarians, they do it proudly. The era of "hide your strength, bide your time" is dead.
For the nations actually bordering China, the West's slow learning curve is terrifying. Regional allies constantly warn that Washington's internal disunity is a gift to Beijing. Xi is an opportunist; he exploits every crack in Western alliances.
By refusing to disentangle our economies, and by failing to understand exactly what Xi wants, the West has inadvertently helped build a geopolitical colossus. We spent ten years worrying about China's rise, but our reluctance to actually confront it may have just handed Xi the keys to the future.

Europe's top court just handed Germany a massive ultimatum: stop expanding the Garzweiler open-pit coal mine, or pay €600,000 every single day.
The ruling came down on Monday. It follows a fierce lawsuit from environmental coalitions and local municipalities, which resulted in a European Commission order to immediately halt land-clearing operations in North Rhine-Westphalia. Yet, German authorities aren't backing down. Despite the staggering daily fine, the regional government made it perfectly clear that the excavators will keep digging.
"We are not halting Garzweiler," a spokesperson for the regional government stated bluntly. Their argument is essentially that pulling the plug on the mine right now would severely compromise the energy grid across the country's industrial heartland.
For RWE, the energy giant operating the mine, Garzweiler is a critical asset. It feeds several major power plants and supports thousands of jobs in an area historically reliant on fossil fuels. But the site has become a massive environmental flashpoint. Carved into a densely populated stretch of western Germany not far from the Dutch border, the mine's relentless creep has sparked international backlash—which is especially awkward given the continent's broader push toward green energy.
Germany might have ambitious climate goals, but the International Energy Agency notes the country still leans heavily on coal to keep the lights on during the winter.
Things reached a boiling point when RWE confirmed it would bulldoze several villages, including the tiny hamlet of Lützerath, to get at the coal underneath. The plan triggered massive protests. Attempts to negotiate a compromise ultimately collapsed as RWE refused to budge on its timeline.
Unsurprisingly, environmental groups are thrilled with Monday's ruling. Climate advocates took to Twitter to celebrate, promising to hold the government’s feet to the fire regarding compliance and carbon cuts.
On the ground, the anxiety is palpable. Locals worry about toxic dust, the structural safety of their remaining homes, and the total erasure of centuries-old communities.
"They are swallowing our villages whole," one resident said in an interview last year. "The machines just keep getting closer. It's surreal to lose your family home in the 21st century for a dirty fuel we're supposed to be leaving behind."
Both RWE and German officials defend the expansion, pointing out that the digging is fully covered by permits issued decades ago. The company insists this coal is a necessary bridge to keep power flowing while they scale up wind and solar alternatives.
Still, the climate cost is impossible to ignore. Garzweiler produces lignite—a soft, brown coal that ranks as the absolute dirtiest and least efficient fossil fuel on the market. It flies directly in the face of the European Union's push to phase out coal by 2030. Germany has technically signed on to that goal, but as the active excavators at Garzweiler show, getting there is proving to be a messy, deeply contested process.

The Democratic Party spent the early 2000s banking on a simple demographic theory: a diversifying country would naturally deliver them a permanent electoral majority. That dream collided with reality pretty quickly. The party lost the presidency in 2016 and only squeaked by in 2020. A major piece of the math simply didn't add up because of bleeding support among White working-class voters.
But the last few election cycles have highlighted a totally different headache for the left. Asian American voters, who represent one of the fastest-growing segments of the electorate, are shifting rightward. And judging by recent polling—along with turnout numbers from this week's New York primaries—the GOP’s inroads are sticky. 
Take New York, a state with a massive Asian American population. According to Census data, there are five state assembly districts in New York City alone where Asian Americans represent at least half the citizen voting-age population. Look closely at the numbers in the most concentrated of those districts, located in Flushing, Queens. In 2016, voters there backed Hillary Clinton by a whopping 40 points. Fast forward to 2020, and Joe Biden won it by just 15 points. That 25-point swing isn't a typo. It was a massive political earthquake.
Now, primaries are weird. You aren't watching a Democrat run head-to-head against a Republican. But relative primary turnout tells a compelling story about voter enthusiasm. In New York's closed primary system, we can easily track which party is drawing voters to the polls.
Across those five heavily Asian assembly districts, 32% of primary voters pulled a Republican ballot on Tuesday. On its face, that might not sound earth-shattering. But rewind to the 2018 midterms, when just 14% of primary voters in those same neighborhoods bothered to vote on the Republican side.
The Flushing district is the starkest example. A staggering 35% of its primary ballots were cast for Republicans this week. In 2018? An almost negligible 8%. That is a 27-point rightward lurch in primary participation.
To put that in perspective, the rest of the city barely moved. Citywide, Republican primary ballots accounted for 22% of the total vote, up slightly from 18% four years ago. So the shift toward the GOP in heavily Asian neighborhoods outpaced the citywide trend by nearly seven to one.
Of course, New York is a deeply blue state, and this is just one primary. But zoom out, and the Empire State doesn't look like an outlier.
Nationally, the Republicans seem to be holding onto the demographic ground they gained in 2020. While we can't definitively say Asian Americans are rushing toward the GOP faster than the rest of the country, the trend lines are clear.
Look at generic congressional ballot polling from CNN/SSRS, Fox, and Quinnipiac University. On average, Democrats are currently holding a 27-point advantage among Asian American voters.
For context, look back at House races in 2020. Based on estimates from Catalist, Pew, and the CNN exit poll, Democrats won the Asian American House vote by about 34 points.
That implies a 7-point swing toward Republicans among Asian American voters. It slightly outpaces the 5-point national swing we're seeing among the general electorate (where Republicans currently lead the generic ballot by 2 points, after losing the national House vote by 3 points in 2020).
Here is the rub for the left: Democrats underperformed with Asian American voters in 2020 relative to their national baseline—clocking their worst margins with the demographic since 2014. Merely stopping the bleeding isn't enough to feel secure; they actually need to win these voters back.
President Biden's approval numbers don't offer much of a lifeline. Across those three major polls, Biden is treading water with Asian Americans, boasting an average net approval of +8. Yes, that is 23 points better than his dismal -15 net approval overall.
But compare that to Election Day 2020, when Biden’s margin with Asian American voters was a full 30 points better than his national margin.
Relatively speaking, the President is losing his grip on this demographic faster than he's losing the general public. It's a sharp reversal from his early days in office when his standing with Asian Americans was a distinct bright spot.
This lines up with what data analysts have been seeing for months. FiveThirtyEight’s Geoffrey Skelley, for example, recently pointed out that Biden’s approval ratings have tanked disproportionately among non-White voters.
All of this volatility suggests something fundamental is shifting. The political allegiances of Asian Americans are fluid, and their votes are increasingly up for grabs. Both parties have a real opening here. While the demographic still leans Democratic, Republicans have proven they can make serious inroads.
Expect both sides to spend a lot of time—and money—chasing this growing voting bloc in the upcoming midterms.

The American election was, at its core, a raw referendum on Donald Trump. While the final tallies are still trickling in, the early data makes one thing obvious: the former president has pulled off a massive victory. Despite months of catastrophic predictions, Trump's Republicans successfully defended key governorships in crucial swing states and look poised to expand their grip on Congress. The most telling blow came in a traditional "blue wall" state, where the GOP flipped a Senate seat that Democrats had comfortably held for over a decade. Winning a national election with that kind of momentum is tough under any circumstances. Doing it while juggling a mountain of felony indictments and civil trials is a political comeback that defies almost every rule of modern American politics.
A lot has changed since Trump slumped out of Washington after his 2020 defeat. Back then, the country was paralyzed by a bitter partisan gridlock, and an exhausted public seemed desperate for a return to normalcy. But Trump’s 2024 campaign tapped into a different kind of exhaustion. He leaned hard into a blunt, "America First" economic message, gambling that voters cared more about soaring grocery bills than democratic norms—a bet that paid off handsomely against an incumbent administration that completely misread the depth of working-class anger over inflation.
The sheer volume of baggage Trump carried into this race should have been disqualifying.
