He spent the campaign leaning into aggressive, retributive rhetoric, openly questioning the integrity of the voting system, and alienating the precise suburban moderates who cost him the White House four years ago. His operation faced constant fire for alleged self-dealing and selling access to mega-donors. Just weeks before the vote, reports surfaced that he had privately discussed using the Department of Justice to systematically target his political rivals. On top of that, his aggressive tariff threats panicked international markets, and his inner circle was plagued by high-profile purges and leaks. By all accounts, the country was at a breaking point, and Trump's chaotic style seemed like the last thing voters would want.
So how did he manage to turn a referendum on his character into a landslide?
The short answer is that pocketbook anger washed away his scandals. Trump turned the election into a single-issue debate on the economy, promising that massive import taxes would instantly resurrect domestic manufacturing. It was a simplistic, economically risky sales pitch, but it resonated deeply with a public tired of feeling broke.
More fundamentally, Trump has completely re-engineered the American political map. Millions of voters who historically viewed the Republican Party with deep suspicion now see it as a completely different animal than the country-club conservatism of George W. Bush or Mitt Romney. The old alliance between fiscal discipline and hawkish foreign intervention has been thoroughly dismantled. The clearest proof is in the old industrial rust belt, where union members overwhelmingly backed a billionaire real estate mogul. By combining economic isolationism, an appetite for government intervention, and an unapologetic, flag-waving patriotism, Trump has built an alliance anchored in working-class resentment—one that looks nothing like the polished, globalist establishment that ran Washington for decades.
Of course, this victory relies heavily on a deeply polarized electorate, which suits Trump perfectly. His brand of politics thrives in rural and exurban communities, and those voters are distributed efficiently enough to secure the Electoral College, making the progressive majorities in deep-blue cities practically irrelevant to his path to power. But the view from the coasts is entirely different. In places like California and New York, the incoming administration is viewed with pure horror, setting up a massive cultural and political wall between red and blue America.
That disconnect guarantees a turbulent four years. We are likely looking at an immediate wave of political warfare in Washington as progressive states try to use local laws to block executive orders from the White House. The risk of a genuine constitutional crisis between blue-state governors and federal agencies is higher than it has been in a generation.
Down the road, the economic bill will come due. Trump's tariffs are bound to drive up consumer prices eventually, and his combative approach to foreign trade could trigger retaliatory measures that pinch ordinary Americans. For now, Trump has played the political system masterfully. But he did it by supercharging a politics of grievance and driving a massive wedge between different halves of the country. It’s an approach that might keep his movement in power for a generation, but it leaves the United States more fractured than ever—a dynamic that could carry devastating social and economic costs long after Trump leaves the stage.

It's a grim picture: nearly three-quarters of women serving in the Canadian Armed Forces have faced sexual harassment, discrimination, or a toxic workplace culture. That's according to a blistering new parliamentary report released Tuesday.
Calling the inquiry "long overdue," the defense committee revealed that 71% of the women they surveyed reported experiencing harassment, bullying, or discrimination at some point during their military careers. Over 3,500 active-duty members and veterans came forward to share their stories. The details, spilling out in both public hearings and closed-door sessions, are harrowing. Women recounted incidents of sexual assault, targeted intimidation, and the routine exploitation of junior recruits. Many described a pervasive "locker room" mentality where degrading female colleagues was treated as a joke. Worse, several women said they faced professional retaliation for rejecting advances. They watched it happen to their friends, too, but admitted they were far "too terrified of retaliation to speak up."
"The armed forces have made superficial progress recently, but it is abundantly clear that the military remains, structurally, a boys' club," said Marie-France Lalonde, the committee's chair, in a written statement. She didn't mince words about the military's internal grievance process either, calling it "fundamentally broken" and a system that actively silences victims.
While she acknowledged that some leaders are genuinely trying to fix the culture, Lalonde pointed out that the chain of command is ultimately betraying female personnel. The committee heard numerous accounts of commanding officers quietly burying allegations. Why? To protect the institution's public image and preserve their own upward mobility.
One of the report's major takeaways is a push to strip the military of its jurisdiction over these crimes. The committee strongly recommended permanently transferring all serious sexual misconduct cases to the civilian justice system. Given that military tribunals consistently produce lower conviction rates than civilian courts, Lalonde argued that victims in uniform are simply being "denied basic justice."
This level of transparency is rare. To make the comprehensive review happen, the Department of National Defence had to grant a special exemption allowing active-duty members to bypass usual protocols and speak directly to parliamentary investigators.
Shortly after the report dropped, the Department of National Defence issued a standard response on social media. "Misconduct and toxic behavior have no place in the Canadian Armed Forces," the post read, adding that the department is "dedicated to rooting out all forms of harassment."
Interestingly, despite the brutal reality outlined in the testimonies, the vast majority of the women surveyed said they remained fiercely proud of their service. Many would still encourage other women to enlist.
"Now that the systemic failures have been laid bare, it is time we started defending those who defend our country," Lalonde concluded. "We need to enact reforms that strengthen our military, for everyone who wears the uniform."

For Eddy Alvarez, reaching the Olympics was never just a passing thought; it was a lifelong mission.
"Growing up, I always said, 'I'm going to be an Olympian,'" Alvarez tells CNN Sport. His advice to the next generation is blunt but deeply encouraging: "You gotta go and you can do it. Nothing's stopping you."
Alvarez didn't just reach that goal—he crushed it twice. After taking home a silver medal in short-track speedskating at the 2014 Winter Games in Sochi, he made the rare pivot to summer sports, snagging another silver in baseball at the Tokyo 2020 Olympics. Shortly after, he made history again as the first Cuban American to carry the US flag at an Opening Ceremony.
But Alvarez wasn't a lone standout in Tokyo. As the Games unfolded, Team USA’s Hispanic and Latino contingent quietly delivered some of the summer's most iconic performances.
There was Richard Torrez Jr., who punched his way to a historic silver in super heavyweight boxing. Devin Booker, fresh off an NBA Finals run, helped anchor the men’s basketball team to gold. Diana Taurasi, who proudly embraces her Argentine roots, cemented her legacy by leading the US women's basketball squad to their seventh straight gold—becoming the first basketball player ever to win five Olympic gold medals. And in the pool, Anita Alvarez dazzled in artistic swimming.
By the time the closing ceremony rolled around, over a dozen Hispanic and Latino athletes had medaled, directly fueling Team USA's dominance at a Games overshadowed by the Covid-19 pandemic.
This recent surge in representation didn't come out of nowhere, however. It was built on the backs of pioneers who fought through an era when the Olympic pipeline was far less welcoming.
Back in the 1980s and 90s, athletes like Tracee Talavera and Oscar De La Hoya were breaking ceilings. Talavera, a Mexican American gymnast, helped lead her team to silver at the 1984 Los Angeles Games during a time when elite gymnastics was overwhelmingly White and notoriously expensive to access. Years later in Barcelona, De La Hoya fought off skepticism about his bicultural identity to win boxing gold for the US, fulfilling a famous promise to his late mother. 
"At the time, I didn't think much of it. I just felt like any other California girl representing my country," recalls Lisa Fernandez, the Cuban-Puerto Rican American softball legend who dominated the 1996 Atlanta Games. "But afterwards? The amount of support and pride I felt from the Latino community was incredible. I was totally taken aback."
For Fernandez, seeing earlier trailblazers like Cuban American swimming icon Dara Torres made all the difference. "She was a huge role model for me. Having that shared Hispanic heritage gave me someone I felt I could actually emulate."
Fernandez eventually became that exact role model for the next generation. Since her golden era on the diamond, Hispanic athletes have increasingly found the podium across diverse sports—from Laurie Hernandez's sparkling gymnastics run in Rio to Ryan Lochte's swimming dominance.
Fast forward to Tokyo, and the landscape looks entirely different. While the London 2012 squad featured a relatively small handful of Hispanic athletes, the 2020 roster saw dozens proudly representing their heritage. Hispanics now make up roughly 19% of the US population. While Olympic representation is still catching up to that demographic reality, the momentum is undeniable.
For athletes like Torrez Jr., the shifting demographics are obvious at the grassroots level. "We train out here in California, and just within our little boxing community, I see so many Mexican Americans," he says.
Today's Olympians are also increasingly embracing their multiracial and dual-heritage backgrounds. Devin Booker, who is of Mexican and African American descent, points out that the training gyms are finally starting to reflect the actual makeup of the country. "It's our job as current athletes to inspire that and push for that diversity," Booker notes.
Historically, the high financial barrier of entry for elite sports, coupled with lingering cultural stereotypes, kept many Hispanic Americans out of the Olympic pipeline. But the current wave of competitors is aggressively challenging that status quo.
And they’re doing it with a sense of longevity and grit, perhaps best embodied by Taurasi. At an age when most basketball players have long since retired, she remains one of the most lethal competitors on the planet.
"In my sport, age can be a disadvantage," Taurasi says matter-of-factly. Her takeaway, however, applies to anyone chasing a podium: "It's not too late, and you can still achieve your dreams. Whatever you aspire to be, just go after it."

Here is a look back at the extraordinary life and reign of Queen Elizabeth II.
Born Elizabeth Alexandra Mary on April 21, 1926, in London, she wasn't actually born to be queen. Her parents, King George VI and Queen Elizabeth, raised her and her younger sister with private tutors at home. But her trajectory completely shifted in December 1936. Following the scandalous abdication of her uncle, King Edward VIII, Elizabeth’s father suddenly became king. Overnight, ten-year-old "Lilibet" became the heir presumptive.
She didn't spend her youth entirely insulated from the world. During the Second World War, she actively served in the Auxiliary Territorial Service, training as a mechanic and driver. Shortly after the war ended, she married Philip Mountbatten at Westminster Abbey in November 1947. Their marriage would span an incredible seven decades, ending only with Philip's death in 2021, and together they had four children: Charles, Anne, Andrew, and Edward.
Her reign began unexpectedly soon. On February 6, 1952, while she was on a royal tour in Kenya, King George VI passed away. At just 25 years old, Elizabeth ascended to the throne. The following year, her coronation at Westminster Abbey made broadcast history. As the first to be fully televised, the ceremony brought the ancient rituals of the monarchy directly into the living rooms of millions.
Her decades on the throne were marked by grand celebrations, but also intense public hardships. While 1977 saw joyous nationwide street parties for her Silver Jubilee, she famously dubbed 1992 her "annus horribilis." That year brought the collapse of three of her children's marriages and a devastating fire at her beloved Windsor Castle. Five years later, the tragic death of Princess Diana in August 1997 tested the institution yet again, with the Queen facing intense public backlash over her initial silence and retreat to Balmoral.
Through the 2000s, however, Elizabeth cemented her role as a stabilizing constant for the British public. She celebrated her Golden Jubilee in 2002—despite the heartbreak of losing both her mother and sister earlier that year—and charmed the world during her 2012 Diamond Jubilee by filming a James Bond sketch for the London Olympics. By September 2015, she had made history, surpassing Queen Victoria to become Britain's longest-reigning monarch.
Her final years were a testament to her endurance. In February 2022, she marked her Platinum Jubilee, an unprecedented 70 years on the throne. She passed away just months later on September 8, 2022, at Balmoral Castle at the age of 96. Her state funeral eleven days later brought London to a standstill, drawing billions of viewers worldwide before she was finally laid to rest at St. George's Chapel in Windsor.

(CNN)British Columbia is underwater. After days of relentless, punishing rain from an atmospheric river, the Canadian government has officially declared a state of emergency across vast stretches of the province. Thousands are fleeing their homes.
The deluge started Tuesday. By Thursday, things went from bad to disastrous. Critical dikes in the Pacific Northwest simply gave way. Swollen rivers breached their banks, triggering catastrophic flash floods that swallowed entire communities.
The damage spans from the Fraser Valley all the way to Vancouver Island. BC's Minister of Public Safety, Mike Farnworth, confirmed Friday that the provincial and federal governments have already signed off on multiple disaster declarations.
Miraculously, there are no widespread casualties to report just yet. But Farnworth wasn't mincing words during Friday's press conference: "We are moving closer and closer to an inevitable tragedy."
His message to the public was blunt. "We cannot emphasize this enough. Do not put yourself in danger, and do not put the rescue agencies in danger."
For thousands of residents, the nightmare peaked in the dead of night. Families were jolted awake and forced to evacuate as water levels surged. Premier John Horgan noted that another 5,000 people—mostly around Abbotsford—might need to pack up and leave by Saturday.
"This is nothing like we've seen in our lifetimes," Horgan said. He called the destruction in the hardest-hit zones a once-in-a-century event. Even for places used to high water, like the Fraser Valley, it’s a one-in-50-years catastrophe.
For Mark and Sarah Henderson, the timing couldn't have been more cruel. Thursday was supposed to mark their 20th wedding anniversary. Instead, they watched their dairy farm in Sumas Prairie get completely wiped out. The floodwaters took everything—their home, their equipment, and most of their livestock. Mark's sister, Emily, quickly launched a GoFundMe to help the couple get back on their feet. In a single day, over 2,100 people chipped in, raising nearly $120,000 CAD (about $95,000 USD).
Footage of a massive barn floating down a submerged highway in the area has been making the rounds on social media. (CNN hasn't independently verified if it belonged to the Hendersons).
"We have just been blown away with people's generosity," Emily wrote on Facebook. "We are all so grateful."
Emergency responders are stretched to the absolute limit. Since Tuesday, the provincial emergency agency has fielded over 8,000 calls for help and pulled off more than 900 water rescues. Armies of volunteers and first responders are still wading through flooded neighborhoods trying to reach trapped locals.
The visuals coming out of the province are staggering. Backyards look like lakes. Major highways are buried under waist-deep water. Over in Merritt, locals were seen pulling stranded pets from the freezing rapids, and CBC News reported that an entire bridge was swept away by the crushing current.
Horgan's plea to residents was simple: listen to the authorities. Stay off the roads. If you're told to evacuate, go.
"This is different," the Premier warned, specifically addressing locals who might think they can ride it out. "What we're going through is unprecedented compared to what you've been through for the last 50 years. Please take it seriously."
Officials are still trying to tally the exact number of destroyed homes and washed-out roads. But Horgan already knows the final bill will be steep, calling the damage "catastrophic."
Help is on the way. The disaster declaration unlocks federal and provincial cash, meaning victims can access subsidies for lost livestock and low-interest loans to rebuild.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau took to the airwaves Friday to announce the activation of federal disaster relief. Households dealing with major injuries or severe property damage can claim a $2,000 CAD emergency payout.
"The terrible storms and floods that have been ravaging British Columbia for the past few days are taking a heavy toll," Trudeau said, adding he’s been in constant contact with Premier Horgan. He commended the military personnel and local rescue crews, urging everyone to keep their heads down and follow instructions.
Unfortunately, the sky isn't clearing up anytime soon. Environment Canada's Armel Castellan warned that another atmospheric river is barreling toward the coast. In the worst spots, 48 hours of rain could dump three times the typical November average. The Fraser River is swelling to levels not seen since the historic floods of 1894.
Come Monday, over 200 schools across BC will keep their doors shut, with officials noting that many need extensive repairs. Thursday might offer a brief window of relief as the downpours finally transition to scattered showers.
But drying out is just the beginning. Emergency Management BC officials told CTV News that the cleanup will take months. With river levels receding at a crawl, recovery operations are expected to stretch well past the winter holidays.

There won't be any tickets sold to the general public for the Beijing 2022 Winter Olympics. Organizers confirmed on Monday that the ongoing complexities of the global Covid-19 pandemic have forced them to shut out regular fans, opting instead for a highly restricted spectator model.
The decision was finalized after consultations between the International Olympic Committee (IOC) and local Beijing officials. In a joint press release, the groups made it clear that international travel for the event was officially off the table. Citing the need to protect the safety of athletes and locals alike, they stated that "tickets should not be sold anymore to spectators from outside China's mainland."
Even within China, average citizens won't be able to just buy a ticket. Only specific, targeted groups of domestic spectators will be invited to attend the events, and they will be subject to intense Covid-19 protocols.
The announcement coincides with a fresh wave of localized lockdowns across several Chinese cities, triggered by a weekend flare-up in virus cases.
It’s a familiar scenario for the Olympic community. Just months prior, foreign spectators were completely banned from the Tokyo Summer Olympics. To avoid a major outbreak, Beijing is implementing a massive "closed-loop" bubble system. State media reports that this isolation network will go into effect long before the opening ceremony, completely separating athletes, media, and event staff from the wider Chinese public.
For the average sports fan, it means a total lockout from standard ticketing.
Organizers expressed their dismay over the situation, issuing a formal apology to international fans who had hoped to make the trip. They stressed that the sweeping measures are simply unavoidable if they want to stop the virus from spreading through the Olympic village. "Everyone is encouraged to watch the Games at home," the statement noted.
Officials plan to keep a close eye on the data, leaving room to tweak protocols if the infection rate shifts dramatically in the coming months.
The Winter Olympics are slated to kick off on February 4, wrapping up on February 20.

The Oregon Ducks' remarkable run in the NCAA Women's Tournament is over, and they didn't even get to take the court for their final game. A severe virus outbreak has forced the team to withdraw from the Final Four, sending the South Carolina Gamecocks straight through to the national championship.
Early Sunday morning, the NCAA officially called off the highly anticipated semifinal matchup. Citing strict health and safety guidelines, officials declared the game a no-contest.
"This decision was made based on the recommendation of the Tournament Medical Staff and the local Department of Public Health," the NCAA shared in a brief statement. Because of the cancellation, South Carolina automatically advances. The organization expressed deep regret for the players and coaching staff, acknowledging how hard the team fought to reach this stage of the tournament.
Citing medical privacy laws, officials declined to share specific details about the players' conditions or the exact nature of the outbreak.
For Oregon, the news is a crushing blow.
Athletics Director Rob Mullens didn't mince words, calling the past two days "incredibly heartbreaking." In a released statement, he praised the team's resilience. "This is a devastating way to have their historic season come to an end. My heart goes out to the student-athletes, coaches, and staff. We appreciate their dedication all year long, as well as the unwavering support from Ducks fans."
The warning signs were already there on Friday. During their gritty Elite Eight win over Stanford, the Ducks were severely depleted, managing to pull off the victory with a roster of just seven healthy players.
Now, South Carolina moves on. They will face the winner of the UConn-Iowa matchup for the national title on Tuesday night in Dallas. The Gamecocks are certainly no strangers to this stage, having recently won the championship in 2022.
Sadly, Oregon joins a growing list of college programs derailed by sudden medical emergencies. Over the past few seasons, Texas A&M men's golf, Florida State women's soccer, and Ohio State wrestling all saw their title hopes abruptly dashed by unexpected health protocol withdrawals.

(CNN)Liam Thorne wasn’t defined by osteosarcoma. He made sure of that.
The obstacles in front of him were relentless, but he refused to be an object of pity. Instead, he fiercely carved out a life that mattered. He sparked difficult, honest conversations about what it means to be young and dying, and he did it while bringing a rare, chaotic joy to the people in his orbit.
He died Sunday morning. He was 23. The cause was systemic organ failure, brought on by sepsis shortly after he began an experimental immunotherapy trial.
'Embrace the present'
Liam practically grew up on oncology wards, and the medical staff eventually just became an extension of his family. He’d prank the nurses, leaving a trail of neon stickers stuck to the walls behind his wheelchair. He’d proudly display his acoustic guitar and movie posters, insisting that his hospital room feel like a real sanctuary, not a sterile clinic. He even managed to make his surgeon blush while asking for dating advice after his leg amputation.
He always assumed he would die young. Then, out of nowhere, a clinical trial offered a sliver of a future.
His parents, Sarah Davies and Mark Thorne, split up when Liam was 7. But when it came to their son, they were a united front. His mother says Liam taught her how to stop fearing the inevitable. "He showed me how to embrace the present," she said.
Davies ended up shifting to freelance work just to accommodate Liam’s unpredictable hospital stays. Through it all, she marveled at his energy. He had an uncanny ability to lift up the people around him, even when he was the one in the hospital bed.
She still thinks about a conversation they had back in 2021 about what he’d leave behind. "When I'm gone," Liam told her, "I'll be in the music you listen to and the art you see, because I'm leaving a piece of myself in all of it."
Those words stuck with her. They were a gift.
Trusting Liam
Osteosarcoma is rare. The American Cancer Society notes that only about 1,000 new cases are diagnosed in the U.S. each year. Survival rates vary wildly depending on how far the cancer has spread—though things are much better now than decades past, when limb-salvaging surgeries almost never worked.
The disease usually starts in the long bones of the arms and legs, spawning aggressive tumors that love to travel to the lungs. It strips away mobility. It demands brutal rounds of chemotherapy. Eventually, if it can't be stopped, it leads to respiratory or systemic failure.
There is no magic cure for the advanced stages. Patients endure grueling physical therapy and radiation just to manage the spread. Sometimes, if you're lucky, an immunotherapy trial buys a little more time.
Mark and Sarah had to learn to trust their son. Liam was fiercely independent, and he knew his body better than anyone—what worked, what didn't, and exactly how much suffering he was willing to tolerate.
So, when he turned 18 and announced he was done with traditional chemotherapy after his second relapse, his parents had to swallow their pain and back him up.
"I had to be realistic," Liam wrote on his blog at the time. "It strips away my quality of life, and it's just not for me anymore."
A shift in perspective
He took his illness seriously, but he absolutely refused to take himself seriously. Once, he actually snuck out of his hospital ward to catch a local indie rock show. He found the absurdity of his situation hilarious—especially the strangers in coffee shops who would confidently prescribe him "miracle cures," insisting that alkaline water or a vegan diet would fix his mutated cells.
He wanted to go out on his own terms. He traveled when he could, did podcast interviews, and poured his remaining energy into the non-profit he started at 17, right after losing his right leg. He was also shooting a documentary, swearing up and down that it wouldn't be "just another sad cancer story."
During a university symposium last year, Liam explained how the disease had actually sharpened his focus.
"Life isn't just about existing comfortably, and it's not about avoiding pain," he told the crowd. "It's about what you're creating while you're here. It's about finding a deep satisfaction in the connections you've made."
But his perspective shifted earlier this year. The cancer spread rapidly to his lungs, stealing his breath and, with it, his ability to play music. He realized he wasn't done creating yet. So, he decided to look into experimental trials—a terrifying but welcome pivot for everyone who loved him.
'Let's do this!!!'
Getting into the trial wasn't simple. He needed the right genetic markers, and he had to be just healthy enough to survive the massive immune response the drugs would provoke. Friends worried his body was already too battered for such an aggressive regimen.
Liam didn't care. He tackled the screening process with total tunnel vision. During a clinical meeting at the Dana-Farber Cancer Institute, he sat there quietly recording the session, a "Keep Moving Forward" sticker slapped prominently on his guitar case.
He listened to the terrifying list of potential side effects and didn't blink.
"None of it scared me," he said later. "Now that I'm looking at it as a real chance to stick around, I'm willing to face the fire."
He got the green light in late October, sharing the news with his thousands of online followers. Fevers and fluctuating blood counts delayed the start, but by mid-November, his numbers finally leveled out.
He posted a photo with his dad, both of them beaming, right after the first infusion was scheduled. He had been drinking extra protein shakes on doctor's orders, joking that he was finally going to get ripped.
On November 20, the treatment began.
"Let's do this!!!" he posted on Instagram from his hospital bed in Boston. "See you guys on the flip side."
At first, it worked. The initial infusions went smoothly, and his dad reported that the tumors were actually shrinking. Liam even posted a video of himself playing his guitar in the clinic to celebrate.
Then everything collapsed. Liam contracted a severe, unforeseen infection that threw his body into septic shock. He was rushed to the ICU and put on a ventilator. Despite massive doses of antibiotics and what his father called "heroic efforts" to stabilize his blood pressure, his organs simply couldn't recover. Following his advance directives, the medical team withdrew life support. He died peacefully with his parents beside him.
"They welcomed him into this world with boundless love and surrounded him with it as he left," Sarah Jenkins, the director of Liam's non-profit, wrote online.
His family plans to keep the Thorne Foundation running to financially support teenagers dealing with cancer and limb loss.
'Make some noise'
In one of his final videos, Liam showed a side of himself he usually kept hidden.
Normally a sarcastic, brave optimist, he let his guard down. Facing the brutal reality of the clinical trial, he admitted just how desperately he wanted to live.
"It is terrifying to make this video," he told the camera, allowing himself to be vulnerable. "I didn't realize how much I was banking on this working. I didn't expect to feel this hopeful again. The phase of just accepting the end is over."
He knew he had emotional support, but he was terrified of bankrupting his family with prolonged ICU stays. He asked his followers for help raising awareness for his foundation, just so he could clear his head for the fight ahead.
Before turning the camera off, his voice shook just a little bit. But that signature crooked smile was still there.
"Go make some noise. Seriously. Create something," he urged. "Go do it, 'cause there are people fighting like hell just for the chance to try."

Three of Canada's biggest provinces are slamming the brakes. Facing a surge of a highly contagious new viral variant, authorities have ordered strict stay-at-home mandates, scrambling to get the infection numbers under control.
Starting Sunday at midnight, Toronto plunged into a two-week lockdown. Montreal followed suit shortly after, preparing for a ten-day shutdown to begin Monday night. Both cities join Vancouver, where heavy restrictions are already a daily reality for residents.
Between them, these metropolitan hubs account for over 12.5 million people. The federal health ministry reported 4,320 active cases by Tuesday morning, with Ontario bearing the brunt of the infections.
Not long ago, Canada was taking a victory lap for its sky-high vaccination rates and newly reopened borders. But that early success seemingly bred complacency. Provincial governments, in particular, are facing intense criticism for failing to shore up hospital capacity. Now, instead of managing small clusters, officials are relying on the same sweeping emergency restrictions they used at the height of the pandemic.
In Toronto and the broader Greater Toronto Area, the rules are strict: stay home. After contact tracers uncovered widespread community transmission bleeding in from neighboring regions, exceptions to the lockdown have been strictly limited to essential errands. Montreal's rules are looking very similar. The country's second-largest city has mandated remote work, allowing residents out only for medical care, groceries, or solo exercise.
Toronto alone is home to nearly 3 million people, and they're looking at restrictions lasting until late next month. Out west, Vancouver just tacked another 48 hours onto its current protocols. The ripple effects are being felt everywhere. Fearing the strain will jump provincial borders, health officials have slapped capacity limits on businesses nationwide, impacting upwards of 25 million Canadians.
To fight the surge, authorities announced late Monday that vulnerable groups can now get a modified booster dose. Physicians, backed by a newly expanded public liability initiative, are being urged to authorize the shots to help kickstart a sluggish campaign. Frontline healthcare workers and long-term care staff no longer have a choice; for them, the boosters are mandatory.
"I am entirely confident in the rollout across the healthcare sector," Canada's chief public health officer assured national broadcasters on Tuesday.
Historically, Canada has relied on targeted lockdowns, widespread rapid antigen testing, and strict mask mandates to keep hospitalizations from spiraling. But this latest booster campaign has been a logistical mess.
Just two weeks ago, supply chain bottlenecks forced officials to restrict the newest vaccine formulations entirely to high-risk individuals. Everyone else was told to settle for standard doses—a major, and highly scrutinized, shift in public health strategy.
So far, only about 15% of the Canadian population has received the updated booster. Compare that to 35% in the UK or 40% in Israel. Right now, Canada's numbers look a lot more like those in Western Europe—regions that boasted high initial vaccination rates but ultimately found themselves totally unprepared for the speed of this latest wave.

The tension inside Miami’s Kaseya Center is going to be thick enough to cut with a knife this Saturday. UFC 287 has finally arrived, bringing with it a middleweight title rematch that combat sports fans have been obsessing over for months. Alex Pereira and Israel Adesanya are officially running it back.
It’s only been five months since their last meeting at UFC 281, but the fallout from that November night still lingers. Pereira, the heavy-handed Brazilian kickboxer, shocked the world by stopping Adesanya with a brutal fifth-round TKO to snatch the belt. Before that sequence, Adesanya had ruled the 185-pound division with an iron fist for three years. Now, he wants his throne back.
Neither man really needed to sell this fight—their bitter kickboxing history does that on its own. Still, Thursday’s press conference in Florida gave the raucous crowd exactly what they came for: icy glares and plenty of bad blood.
Adesanya seemed completely unbothered by the pressure. "I wouldn't say I'm treating this any differently," he shrugged, acknowledging Pereira's raw power before quickly reminding everyone of his own championship pedigree. "He has to live with the fact that I have ruled this division for a reason."
Pereira, meanwhile, has been relentless in his trash talk. A few weeks ago, he openly questioned his rival's mental toughness and promised another devastating finish. "I'm going to give the people what they want," Pereira recently told reporters, swearing he wouldn't let the judges decide the outcome. He even went as far as suggesting that another knockout would send the former champion straight into retirement.
During the press conference, the newly crowned king didn't back down an inch. Speaking through a translator, Pereira claimed Adesanya was letting his ego drive him into a fight he couldn't win. "He avoided the reality of that knockout," Pereira said, adding that Adesanya's pride forced his hand into taking an immediate rematch instead of rebuilding his confidence against other contenders.
Just before the main event, the Miami crowd will get to roar for one of their own. Hometown hero Jorge Masvidal is stepping into the cage against the always-dangerous Gilbert Burns for a high-stakes welterweight showdown.
Unlike the main event, the build-up here has been almost entirely respectful. Both veterans shared a quiet nod and a handshake during their staredown. Masvidal knows exactly what he's up against, praising Burns' lethal grappling resume and noting he'll need to stay sharp from the opening bell to avoid a submission.
If you're looking for fireworks outside of the marquee names, keep an eye on the bantamweight scrap between Rob Font and Adrian Yanez. That fight has "Fight of the Night" written all over it. Kevin Holland taking on Santiago Ponzinibbio at welterweight is just the icing on the cake to round out the main card.
The main action kicks off at 10 p.m. ET, with the prelims setting the stage at 8 p.m. ET.
Fans in the US can catch the pay-per-view on ESPN+, while UK and Irish viewers can tune in via BT Sport 1. Australian fans can watch on Kayo or Main Event, and Canadian viewers have access through TSN and major cable providers.

Extreme heat and suffocating humidity have been absolute nightmares for athletes at these Olympic Games. And as we head into the closing weekend in Paris, there’s no real relief in sight.
While it’s no secret that August can get hot, the levels we're seeing right now are historic. Heatwaves happen, sure, but Paris doesn't usually get slapped with the kind of oppressive, tropical humidity that defines summer in cities like Tokyo or Atlanta. Olympic organizers have been scrambling for weeks, trying to dodge the worst of it by tweaking event schedules and mandating cooling breaks. Still, the brutal weather is going to be a massive problem for the upcoming marathons. 
It really is a punishing combination. When soaring temps crash into high humidity, the conditions become downright dangerous for endurance athletes. 
Here's why: when your core overheats, you sweat. That sweat evaporates, cooling the skin and bringing your body temperature back down. But when the air is already thick with moisture—when relative humidity spikes—that sweat has nowhere to go. It just clings to you. The evaporation process stalls out, and your body essentially loses its natural air conditioning system. 
To beat the heat, organizers had to get creative with the marathon schedule. The men race on Saturday, and the women follow on Sunday. But the blistering heat isn't just a localized problem; it's baking the entire country. Paris is looking at morning lows hovering around 72 degrees and miserable afternoon highs pushing into the mid-90s—easily 10 to 15 degrees hotter than a normal Parisian summer. 
By the time the runners hit the pavement, temperatures will already be sitting somewhere between 75 and 85 degrees. Factor in the 60% to 70% humidity, and it’s going to feel significantly hotter. To mitigate the risk, the starting gun for both races was bumped to 8 a.m.
“Athletes' health and well-being are the organisers' top priorities,” read a joint statement from World Athletics, the IOC, and the Paris 2024 committee, noting that medical experts signed off on the revised schedule.
But honestly, is an 8 a.m. start going to be enough? The sun rises over Paris around 6:30 right now. That means the pavement will have been baking in direct sunlight for an hour and a half before the athletes even take their first strides.
Think about it like this: if you live on the East Coast and try to sneak in a morning run during a heatwave, starting at 8 a.m. means you’re finishing closer to 10:30. By then, the sun is high and the air is heavy. It's far from ideal for an elite marathon.
The only sliver of hope is a weak cold front drifting toward the city, which might drop a few scattered showers over the weekend.
These Games have already been rocked by one brutal heatwave, leading to scary scenes of athletes collapsing after finishing their events. Now, thanks in part to the Urban Heat Island effect trapping the hot air inside the city limits, this second wave is perfectly timed to disrupt the final weekend of competition.
A high-pressure system is expected to park itself right over the French capital by Sunday morning. The worst of the heat will likely peak Saturday afternoon and roll right into Sunday. Thankfully, some cloud cover and a breeze might roll in just in time for the Closing Ceremony, offering a tiny bit of relief before the Games officially wrap up at 9 p.m. local time.

Six people died Tuesday when a twin-engine plane slammed into an empty warehouse in suburban Dallas.
According to authorities, the aircraft had just taken off from Dallas Love Field and was en route to the Florida coast when it went down near a busy transit hub.
Witnesses reported hearing a massive explosion as the plane struck the three-story commercial building. Thankfully, the structure was completely vacant ahead of a planned demolition.
The impact sent a thick plume of black smoke into the sky and sparked secondary fires that engulfed several trucks parked in an adjacent alley. Despite the severe wreckage and the proximity to the commuter station, first responders confirmed there were no other casualties on the ground.

Novak Djokovic won't be competing at the Miami Open next week. The reason, however, is a happy one: the birth of his third child.
On Friday, the Serbian tennis star's agent, Edoardo Artaldi, confirmed the news to CNN. While he verified that Jelena Djokovic had indeed given birth—prompting Novak's sudden withdrawal from the Florida tournament—Artaldi kept the specifics under wraps and declined to comment further.
The Miami draw is certainly taking a hit this week. Just hours before Djokovic's announcement, 22-time grand slam champion Rafael Nadal also pulled out, citing a need to focus on his physical recovery rather than competing.
Djokovic and his high school sweetheart, Jelena, married back in the summer of 2014. The couple already shares a seven-year-old son, Stefan, and a four-year-old daughter, Tara. At this point, the family hasn't revealed whether the newest addition is a boy or a girl.
Balancing a grueling ATP tour schedule with raising kids is something the 34-year-old has spoken about candidly. In a Guardian interview last year, he touched on the chaotic beauty of fatherhood.
"It can be a challenge because the older ones are always developing new interests and needing your guidance," he explained. The dynamic at home is constantly shifting. "Stefan just wants to play tennis and run around the whole time, whereas Tara is a little bit more drawn to arts and crafts. It's a beautiful dynamic, but you really have to balance your travel schedule with being present."
It's been a turbulent few years for the current world No. 1. Back in 2018, he needed a medical intervention for a lingering elbow issue to get his career back on track. More recently, a high-profile visa cancellation forced him out of this year's Australian Open, paving the way for Nadal to capture the first grand slam title of the season.
With 20 major championships under his belt, Djokovic can easily afford to take a brief hiatus to focus on his growing family.

A stray visor tear-off—of all things—was the culprit behind Charles Leclerc’s frustrating Sunday at the Belgian Grand Prix. His Ferrari team confirmed the discarded plastic strip basically torpedoed the title contender's chances of fighting for a podium. 
Leclerc had to dive into the pits much earlier than planned because the front-right brakes on his F1-75 were overheating to dangerous levels. 
Ferrari’s team principal, Mattia Binotto, explained that the plastic tear-off had lodged itself perfectly inside the brake duct during the chaotic opening stint. Not only did it cause massive temperature spikes, but it also ended up damaging the car's sensor system, forcing the team to bring him in purely for safety. 
Driving the #16 Ferrari, Charles Leclerc hits the pit box during the Belgian Grand Prix. 
Read More: Verstappen puts on a masterclass to win at Spa
Starting down in 15th due to engine penalties, Leclerc fought hard but ultimately had to settle for a disappointing sixth-place finish. 
"The start was so hectic. We managed to avoid a few crashes, and honestly, the battles through the pack were pretty fun," the 24-year-old admitted. "But getting hit with a time penalty at the very end was just incredibly frustrating."
The grandstands at Spa-Francorchamps were absolutely packed. After a couple of years of tight pandemic restrictions and limited capacities, organizers finally opened the floodgates to a record crowd.
Ironically, the tear-off that ruined Leclerc's race likely came from his main championship rival. Max Verstappen, who started just ahead of Leclerc and went on to easily win the race for Red Bull, noted the chaotic visibility on the first lap. "It's your worst nightmare that stuff like this happens," the reigning world champion said. "But with all the dirt getting kicked up, everyone is pulling off tear-offs. It’s just unfortunate."

Organizers of a popular Chicago half-marathon are issuing major apologies this weekend. The reason? Saturday's race route came up nearly a full mile short.
The Chicago Spring Half Marathon was supposed to be a triumphant return to the city's lakefront following last year's weather-related cancellation. Instead, thousands of runners crossed the finish line much earlier than expected, missing out on the standard 13.1-mile distance.
"We would like to apologize to our race participants that the course today measured 0.8 miles too short," organizers admitted in a Facebook statement. They added they were "incredibly disappointed" by the blunder but hoped it didn't completely ruin the community event.
The shortened route actually impacted the podium. Sarah Jenkins took the women's division with a time of 1:08:12, edging out Maria Lopez at 1:08:45. Because Jenkins had been trailing Lopez right up until the premature finish, social media quickly lit up with debate.
"The runner who was leading was caught off guard when the finish line appeared early," one spectator pointed out on Twitter. "She was pacing for a longer race."
On Sunday, race director David Chen stepped up to explain what went wrong. It boiled down to a simple, costly mistake: a volunteer placed a crucial turn sign in the wrong spot.
"Despite the half-marathon route being mapped correctly in the lead-up to race day, a basic human error in placing a turn sign meant that the final course was shortened," Chen explained. The mistake wasn't caught until the leading pack was already crossing the finish line, making it impossible to fix in real time. Officials promised to provide runners with adjusted pacing metrics and add an asterisk for qualifying times.
Of course, this is hardly the first time a major race has fumbled its measuring tape. Back in 2021, a 10K in Portland, Oregon, accidentally tacked on an extra 400 meters when course marshals sent the pack down the wrong street. Organizers scrambled to apologize and review their protocols.
Even professional cycling isn't immune. During an Italian classic in 2018, a police escort famously drove off the circuit during the final kilometer, taking breakaway leader Thomas De Gendt with them. The Belgian rider lost precious seconds to the chasing group but somehow managed to salvage the victory by a few tire lengths.

UN Secretary-General António Guterres didn't mince words during his annual State of the Planet address on Tuesday. Instead of a standard policy briefing, he used the platform to deliver a stark warning about the climate crisis and how it continues to deepen global inequality.
Taking the podium at the General Assembly in New York, Guterres framed the environmental collapse as a "generational test." It’s a crisis, he argued, that isn't just destroying ecosystems—it’s actively unraveling international alliances and pushing countries toward isolationism.
"Our capacity for global solidarity is severely fractured," Guterres warned the delegates gathered in the hall and the millions tuning in worldwide. He pointed to a growing, dangerous reflex among nations to pull up the drawbridge. "There is an alarming trend to retreat inward, to prioritize national interests, to abandon the difficult work of multilateralism and building sustainable solutions together."
Ironically, the weather itself disrupted the keynote address for the second year in a row.
While diplomats were finally allowed back inside the UN headquarters—a stark contrast to 2024's virtual-only event—the room was visibly empty. A massive, late-season hurricane battered the eastern seaboard on Monday, grounding flights and triggering record-breaking rainfall across the region.
Just hours before his main speech, the UN Chief held a private, closed-door climate summit with roughly 500 leaders and policy experts, navigating the immediate logistical chaos of the storm outside.
When he finally addressed the wider assembly, Guterres pointed out that the climate emergency is actively poisoning diplomatic dialogue. Too many nations, he said, are chasing short-term economic wins instead of committing to the grueling, necessary work of transitioning to green energy.
"What would our future look like without the relentless advocacy of the countless individuals who fight to keep our ecosystems and vulnerable communities intact?" he asked the room. "In this era of climate disruption, we are witnessing this necessity more than ever."
A major focal point of his speech was money. Guterres pleaded with wealthier nations to unlock their resources, specifically demanding that climate adaptation funds finally reach the developing world.
He called on governments to step up, urging them to distribute essential funding and clean technology to the regions bearing the brunt of the crisis. "Support those who tirelessly dedicate themselves to rebuilding communities, the displaced, and the most marginalized among us," he urged.
Perhaps the most sobering moment of the address was his observation about public numbness. According to the UN leader, catastrophic weather events have become so routine that people barely register the headlines anymore. Demanding an immediate end to fossil fuel subsidies, he ran through a grim list of regions currently fighting for survival, from the drought-stricken Horn of Africa and the burning Amazon basin to the sinking island nations of the Pacific.
Before stepping down, the 76-year-old Secretary-General made a final, urgent pivot to the human cost of the crisis. In what stands as one of his most impassioned pleas to date, he spotlighted the escalating displacement of indigenous populations and the rapidly growing, yet often ignored, crisis of global climate refugees.

A Canadian lawmaker is pushing back against what she calls "archaic" workplace rules after being barred from bringing her newborn onto the legislative floor. 
British Columbia MLA Sarah Lin was reprimanded by the Sergeant-at-Arms on Thursday for bringing her two-month-old daughter to a voting session. Following the incident, Lin received a formal memo from the clerk’s office pointing to a recently updated standing order: "Non-members, including dependents, may not occupy a seat on the floor during official proceedings." The ban, she was informed, also extends to broadcasted committee rooms. 
The clash has prompted the Speaker of the House to ask the Committee on Parliamentary Privilege to review the assembly's childcare guidelines.
Taking to Twitter shortly after being told to leave, Lin didn't mince words. "Working mothers are told to lean in, but are literally locked out of the room," she wrote.
Speaking to local news networks on Friday, Lin highlighted the impossible bind she found herself in. With no drop-in childcare available at the legislature and a nursing infant who can't be left at home, she felt completely cornered. "I need to be present to represent my constituents, but I also have a nursing infant who relies on me," she said.
Lin found it particularly frustrating that a sleeping baby is viewed as a breach of decorum, pointing out that lawmakers frequently disrupt proceedings with loud phone calls without facing any consequences. To her, the rule smacks of an outdated political culture built around "older men with stay-at-home spouses."
While a spokesperson for the Legislative Assembly released a standard statement emphasizing the need to "accommodate needs while maintaining the formal environment," they confirmed they are in active discussions with Lin to find a solution.
For her part, Lin drew a sharp distinction between bringing her newborn and her energetic four-year-old son, who she joked would "undoubtedly disrupt the proceedings and run down the aisles." The infant, however, was completely silent. Having brought her first child to caucus meetings years ago without any pushback, she questioned what had actually changed—other than the enforcement of institutional barriers that actively keep young parents out of politics.
This isn't Lin's first fight over family-friendly policies. Earlier this year, she unsuccessfully petitioned the administrative board to allow remote proxy voting during her maternity leave. When a conservative pundit recently suggested she should just stay home and focus on motherhood, she quickly fired back online. Without a proxy system, she noted, staying home means her district loses its voice entirely.
The debate over babies in legislative chambers is hardly unique to Canada. In 2018, U.S. Senator Tammy Duckworth made history by casting a vote with her newborn after successfully lobbying to change Senate rules. New Zealand’s Parliament has also embraced the shift; in 2019, MP Tāmati Coffey brought his infant to a debate, and the baby was happily cradled by the Speaker of the House.
Still, progress is uneven. Politicians like European Parliament member Licia Ronzulli have faced both praise and heavy criticism for bringing their kids to work, proving that the global debate over balancing high-level politics with motherhood is far from settled.

A massive software glitch at UEFA headquarters forced a complete redraw of the Europa League knockout stage on Monday. The result? A heavyweight clash between Barcelona and Arsenal.
The initial draw was scrapped entirely after Juventus was improperly placed in the wrong pot. The Italian giants are probably breathing a sigh of relief, though. They were originally slated to play Barcelona, but thanks to the redraw, they've drawn Portugal's Sporting CP. It's a slightly softer landing, even if Sporting has proven to be a tough out in Europe lately.
Europa League Knockout Draw
	•	Sevilla vs. PSV Eindhoven
	•	Sporting CP vs. Juventus
	•	Bayer Leverkusen vs. Monaco
	•	Ajax vs. Union Berlin
	•	Shakhtar Donetsk vs. Rennes
	•	FC Salzburg vs. AS Roma
	•	Manchester United vs. Real Betis
	•	Barcelona vs. Arsenal
While the redraw robbed fans of seeing Robert Lewandowski go up against the Juventus defense, the new headline fixture easily makes up for it. Barcelona now has to navigate a high-flying Arsenal squad currently sitting near the top of the Premier League. Mikel Arteta has the Gunners playing some of their best football in years.
The matchup also comes with plenty of built-in drama. Pierre-Emerick Aubameyang will be making his highly anticipated return to the Emirates Stadium to face his former club. He'll be joined by Héctor Bellerín, who spent years flying down the right flank for Arsenal before heading to Camp Nou this season, though he's struggled to find consistent minutes under Xavi.
Looking at the rest of the bracket, Jose Mourinho’s AS Roma—fresh off winning the inaugural Conference League—drew FC Salzburg. Unbelievably, that was the only matchup that survived the voided first draw.
Manchester United is set to battle Real Betis as the Red Devils chase another European trophy, while tournament specialists Sevilla face off against PSV Eindhoven. Rounding out the heavy hitters, a talented Ajax side takes on Union Berlin, and Bayer Leverkusen gets Monaco in what promises to be a highly entertaining tie.
The opening legs will kick off on February 16 and 23, with the return fixtures locked in for March 9 and 16. The tournament concludes with the final in Budapest on May 31.

Fourteen people are dead after their inflatable dinghy flipped off the coast of Gran Canaria on Tuesday. According to Spain's maritime rescue service, it is just the latest in a string of deadly migrant crossings in the Atlantic this month.
Emergency crews scrambled to launch a search and rescue operation south of the island overnight, deploying both helicopters and patrol boats to scour the waters.
For migrants fleeing West Africa and the Maghreb, Spain remains a major gateway into the European Union. With authorities heavily policing the Mediterranean, the notoriously treacherous Atlantic route has seen a massive spike in crossings since 2019.
Local news agency EFE confirmed the recovery of 14 bodies: ten men, three women, and a toddler.
Rescue officials suspect the dinghy departed from the coast of Western Sahara carrying roughly 75 people. It is still not entirely clear what caused the boat to capsize in the open ocean.
Spain's Interior Minister, Fernando Grande-Marlaska, placed the blame squarely on human smuggling rings. In a press release, he condemned the networks for showing "zero regard for human safety," accusing them of packing vulnerable people into flimsy, unseaworthy vessels without any life jackets.
The Gran Canaria disaster capped off a grim 48 hours for the region. Just hours earlier on Tuesday, Spanish officials confirmed they had pulled eight bodies from the water after a wooden migrant boat sank near Tenerife. While 82 people were pulled to safety in that operation, preliminary reports suggest the vessel had originally set sail from Senegal.
This comes just days after another tragedy over the weekend, when a separate dinghy carrying an estimated 60 migrants deflated off the coast of Fuerteventura. Dozens still remain missing.

For a long time, the FAANG acronym—Facebook, Amazon, Apple, Netflix, and Google—ruled Wall Street. They were the undisputed heavyweights of the tech sector. But with Meta's digital ad struggles and Netflix's subscriber volatility, throwing all these mega-caps into the exact same bucket doesn't make much sense anymore.
"FAANG really had its moment, but that era is fading," explains Sarah Lin, a senior analyst at Horizon Wealth.
The market is already pricing in the shift. Legacy tech darlings are facing brutal downgrades, and Wall Street is waking up to the fact that not all tech giants are created equal. The fact that the broader tech sector has been under pressure since the post-pandemic boom of late 2021 has only accelerated this trend.
"Seeing a titan like Meta shed that much market cap so quickly was a real wake-up call," Lin noted.
So, what happens to the tech-heavy mutual funds and ETFs that relied heavily on these five pillars? It's complicated.
"Clients are definitely asking about how to pivot their tech exposure," says Marcus Thorne, head of equities at Vanguard Partners. "Right now, it's a lot of wait-and-see as indexes gradually rebalance away from the old guard."
Lin's advice to investors is simple: look at the fundamentals of the individual company, not the catchy acronym. "Differentiation is everything right now. You can't just buy a basket of tech stocks based on a buzzword," she said. "Lumping Apple's hardware empire in with Netflix's streaming model just because they're both 'tech' is completely absurd."
If the market still demands an acronym, it might be time to adopt MATANA (Microsoft, Apple, Tesla, Alphabet, Nvidia, and Amazon) or simply embrace the "Magnificent Seven."
Adding AI powerhouse Nvidia and software giant Microsoft to the mix reflects a permanent shift in market leadership, according to David Vance, managing partner at IndexIQ. "Are people going to blindly buy the old FAANG group again? Probably not," Vance told CNN Business. "Growth is happening elsewhere, particularly in artificial intelligence, and money follows growth."
Vance pointed out that investors are actively hunting for replacements in cloud computing and semiconductor manufacturing. While he jokes that "Wall Street just loves a good acronym," he notes that enterprise companies like AMD, Broadcom, and Salesforce are drawing serious attention from institutional money.
But betting on consumer-facing tech remains a highly risky play, especially for companies tied directly to volatile digital ad spending.
Ultimately, the prevailing strategy has moved from buying the entire sector to picking the right horse. "Investors still view mega-cap tech as a crucial portfolio building block," says Elena Rostova, a US investment strategist at TradeNet. "But the landscape is fracturing. People have been nervous about social media stocks since the big privacy updates in 2021, and that anxiety is forcing investors to be much more selective today."

The collapse came so quickly that many athletes barely had time to react. One week they were attending training sessions and planning future competitions; the next they were hiding from view, deleting online accounts, and destroying anything that connected them to international organizations.
As conditions worsened, an informal group of coaches, former players, volunteers, and human rights advocates began trying to get them out. They were scattered across different countries and time zones, communicating through phones and laptops, often with incomplete information. None of them knew whether the effort would succeed.
For the people involved, this was never just about sport. Many had worked with the athletes for years. They had seen firsthand how participation in organized athletics created opportunities for education, employment, and personal freedom. The teams had become symbols of possibility in places where such opportunities were often hard to find.
“Sport was the starting point,” one coach said. “What really mattered was what came with it—confidence, independence, and the belief that young people could shape their own futures.”
The rescue effort quickly turned into a race against time. Volunteers stayed awake deep into the night coordinating paperwork, verifying names, and tracking changing security conditions. Messages arrived constantly. Some carried hope; others brought news of new obstacles.
Meanwhile, the athletes faced dangers of their own. Several spent days moving between temporary shelters. Families waited in crowded areas with limited supplies, uncertain whether they would be allowed to continue. Every decision carried consequences, and none of the options felt safe.
After days of uncertainty, dozens of athletes, staff members, and relatives were finally evacuated through a complex operation involving governments, aid organizations, and international partners. When photographs arrived showing them safely aboard departing flights, many of the organizers were overcome with emotion.
Relief, however, was only part of the story.
The people who helped coordinate the evacuation spoke openly about exhaustion and guilt. Even after a successful outcome, they remained focused on those who had not been able to leave. Several described a lingering sense of responsibility that did not disappear once the flights were airborne.
The athletes themselves now faced a different challenge: starting over. New countries offered safety, but also unfamiliar languages, cultures, schools, and communities. Support groups immediately began helping with housing, counseling, and long-term settlement plans.
Still, those who knew the athletes best were optimistic. They had watched them overcome barriers before and believed they would do so again.
“These are incredibly resilient people,” one advocate said. “They’ve already faced circumstances most of us can hardly imagine. What happens next won’t be easy, but they have proven time and again that they can adapt and keep moving forward.”
For the volunteers and coaches, the experience left a deep mark. The immediate crisis may have passed for some, but the larger questions remained. Their work had begun as an effort to save lives. It ended with a renewed awareness of how fragile opportunity can be—and how much responsibility people feel once they become connected to those whose futures are suddenly at risk.

Team Asia held on to win the International Women's Golf Challenge on Sunday, narrowly defeating Team North America 14.5-13.5 at Seaside Links in California. The outcome wasn't decided until the closing moments, when rookie Hana Kim rolled in a pressure-packed six-foot putt on the final hole to beat Brooke Jensen and effectively secure the trophy for her side.
The celebrations began almost immediately. A halved result in the last match was enough to confirm the victory, and players rushed onto the green to celebrate with captain Mi-Young Park. Having also led the team to success in the previous edition, Park became the first captain to guide a team to back-to-back titles in the event.
Afterward, Park reflected on the achievement and suggested her time as captain may be coming to an end. She spoke warmly about the group and the memories created over the years, adding that many former players would be capable of taking on the role in the future.
Tributes soon appeared across social media. One of the most notable came from men's captain David Reynolds, who praised the team's resilience and competitive spirit ahead of his own international competition later this month.
The final day had started with Team Asia holding only a slim advantage. Early victories in the singles matches seemed to put them firmly in control, but North America refused to fade away. As the afternoon progressed, the home team collected several important points and steadily reduced the deficit, keeping the contest alive until the very end.
That left Kim facing one of the biggest moments of her young career. Surrounded by spectators and fully aware of the situation, she delivered when it mattered most. The 24-year-old, who broke through with a tournament victory in South Korea earlier this year, admitted afterward that the significance of the moment had not fully sunk in.
She said her focus was simply on following her routine and committing to the putt. Once it dropped, relief and excitement quickly took over.
While Kim delivered the decisive moment, rookie Mei Lin was arguably the team's most consistent performer throughout the week. Unbeaten across five matches, she collected four points and played a major role in giving Team Asia momentum whenever it was needed.
Park was quick to recognize her contribution, but she avoided singling out individuals for too much praise. In her view, the title was the product of a complete team effort rather than any one standout performance.
As often happens in major team golf events, there was also a moment of controversy. A ruling during Saturday's play involving a ball resting close to the hole sparked confusion and led to a lengthy discussion among officials. Footage spread rapidly online, and golf fans spent the rest of the weekend debating whether the decision had been interpreted correctly.
A number of well-known players joined the conversation, offering differing opinions. Some supported the ruling, while others questioned it. By the time the tournament ended, the debate was still ongoing, providing one final talking point after an event filled with tension, momentum swings, and a dramatic finish.

After days of uncertainty, two athletes from South Sudan have arrived in the host city for the World Athletics Championships, organizers confirmed Friday. Their journey came only after a coordinated international effort helped them leave an area affected by growing instability and continue their preparations for the competition.
Sprinter Daniel Lado and 800-meter runner Mary Ajak spent the past week training in Italy after departing South Sudan. Until recently, there were serious doubts about whether either athlete would be able to attend. Travel disruptions and security concerns had complicated plans for the national delegation and raised fears that both competitors would miss the event entirely.
World Athletics President Sebastian Coe said athlete safety remained the central concern throughout the process. What initially seemed unlikely, he noted, became possible through cooperation among governments, sports bodies, and humanitarian organizations.
A number of groups assisted with transportation, accommodation, and training arrangements while the athletes were in transit. Organizers said the effort reflected the willingness of the international sporting community to support competitors facing extraordinary circumstances.
Their absence earlier in the week had attracted attention as delegations from other countries began arriving. News of their safe arrival was met with relief, and messages of encouragement quickly appeared online from fellow athletes and fans.
For Mary Ajak, competing at the championships represents more than just another race. The women's 800-meter runner said there were times when the situation felt unpredictable, but she continued training whenever possible and held onto the hope that she would eventually reach the event.
"I kept believing there was still a chance," Ajak said. "A lot of things were outside my control, but I wanted to stay ready if the opportunity came."
Lado, who is scheduled to run in the men's 400 meters, described the journey itself as unforgettable. Simply making it to the championships, he said, already feels like a major achievement.
Officials added that both athletes will continue receiving support during their stay and after the competition if necessary. With their arrival now secured, attention can finally shift from travel difficulties to what they came to do: compete on one of athletics' biggest stages.

(CNN) Tennis officials say players are continuing to benefit from the sport's financial growth, a point reinforced by the announcement of a major new commercial partnership unveiled this week. Mark Reynolds, one of the sport's senior administrators, described the agreement as a significant milestone that could influence professional tennis for years to come.
The deal, signed with an international technology firm, is expected to inject substantial funding into the sport beginning next season. Organizers say prize money across top-level events will reach record levels, while developmental tournaments and smaller competitions will also receive increased financial support.
The timing is notable. Debate has intensified over whether professional tennis should embrace alternative competition formats, particularly after reports of a privately funded circuit that would bring together many of the world's highest-ranked players for a condensed schedule of marquee events.
Advocates argue that such a model could boost player earnings and create more frequent clashes between the sport's biggest names. Not surprisingly, the idea has attracted attention from both athletes and fans.
Grand Slam winner Alex Moreno recently weighed in on the discussion. He noted that spectators generally enjoy seeing elite players compete more often but cautioned that any new structure would need to consider the demands already placed on athletes.
"There is obvious appeal in more top-level matchups," Moreno said. "At the same time, players value having some control over their schedules. Finding the right balance is important."
Comparisons are often made between tennis and major American sports leagues, where revenue-sharing agreements are more formalized. Reynolds argued that those comparisons only tell part of the story. Tennis operates under a very different system, he said, and player compensation should be viewed within that context.
In his view, athletes already have access to significant earning opportunities through prize money, sponsorships, and bonus programs. As a result, he believes the sport remains financially attractive despite ongoing conversations about rival circuits.
Reynolds also stressed that the newly announced partnership was years in the making and should not be interpreted as a reaction to outside competition. Negotiations, he explained, had begun well before recent speculation about alternative tours emerged.
While the increase in prize money has attracted the most attention, officials say the broader investment strategy may have an even greater long-term impact. Funding will be directed toward coaching, sports science, player wellness programs, and resources aimed at helping athletes develop throughout their careers.
Younger players and those competing on developmental tours are expected to benefit from expanded support as they attempt to move into the professional ranks. Organizers also plan to strengthen services related to fitness, nutrition, injury management, and mental health.
Another area of focus is accessibility. The federation intends to increase investment in wheelchair tennis and community-based initiatives that encourage wider participation in the sport.
For Reynolds, that broader vision is what makes the agreement particularly important. Prize money may dominate the headlines, but he argues that the real goal is to build a stronger and more sustainable future for tennis at every level.

(CNN) Andy Murray's opening match at the Geneva Open featured an unusual discussion with the chair umpire after changing weather conditions briefly disrupted play.
Getting ready to serve during the second set, Murray stopped his routine when a light shower moved over the court. As he waited a few moments for the rain to ease, the umpire issued a warning related to the time allowed between points.
Murray questioned the decision, pointing toward the sky as he explained his frustration. He remarked that while players are responsible for their shots, the weather is obviously beyond their control. The exchange drew laughs from spectators before play resumed.
The interruption did little to change the outcome. Murray was eventually beaten 6-4, 3-6, 6-3 by Spain's Roberto Carvajal, who produced a composed performance in the deciding set.
The result adds to a challenging stretch for the former world No. 1. Physical setbacks and uneven form have made it difficult for him to gain momentum this season, particularly with several important clay-court tournaments approaching.
Carvajal, meanwhile, continued a strong run of performances. The win over Murray was one of the biggest of his season and further strengthened his record against experienced, high-profile opponents in recent months.

(CNN) Team International made a powerful start to the Continental Cup on Friday, building a 7-3 advantage over Team Europe after an opening day largely controlled by the hosts in Melbourne.
Backed by loud support from local fans, the home side wasted little time taking charge. Three victories from the four morning matches gave them an early cushion, and they rarely looked under serious pressure afterward. Europe did manage to pick up a point through Viktor Hansen and Marco Silva, but most of the momentum belonged to the International team.
The most one-sided match came from rookies Ethan Cole and Liam Brooks. Facing the experienced pairing of Daniel Hart and Oliver Jensen, the two newcomers played with remarkable confidence and closed out a 6&5 win. Other home pairings also delivered, including Ryan Carter and Ben Matthews, who won a closely fought contest, while Lucas Grant and Michael Reed added another point to the scoreboard.
Europe hoped to regroup in the afternoon, yet the pattern of play remained largely unchanged. Grant and Brooks continued their strong run with another victory, and Carter combined effectively with Jason Walker to stretch the lead even further.
Not every match went the hosts' way. Hansen and Stefan Berg battled back in a thrilling encounter, and Berg's birdie on the last hole secured a crucial half-point for Europe. The final match of the session was equally tense. Marco Silva and Oliver Jensen led for much of the contest before the International side mounted a late comeback, leaving the teams to share the point.
The atmosphere around the course had been intense from the start. Fans filled viewing areas long before the opening tee shots, and the energy carried through the entire day.
Morning conditions were favorable, allowing players to attack pins and take advantage of scoring chances. As the session unfolded, the International team repeatedly capitalized on those opportunities. Strong iron play and timely putts helped turn close matches into victories.
Cole and Brooks attracted particular attention. Their aggressive start put immediate pressure on their opponents, and they never relinquished control. For many observers, their performance was the highlight of the morning.
The challenge increased later in the day when winds began sweeping across the course. Club selection became more complicated, and mistakes were punished more severely. Even so, the home side seemed to adjust more comfortably than the visitors.
By the end of play, Team Europe faced a significant deficit, but captain Henrik Olsen remained confident. He acknowledged that his team had work to do while emphasizing that the contest was far from decided.
"We've got another two days ahead of us," Olsen said. "The position isn't ideal, but nobody in our team thinks this is over. We've seen bigger comebacks before, and we're ready to fight."

Editor's Note: This report is part of a broader series exploring women's experiences in public, cultural, and religious environments.
(CNN) Women from several countries have described experiences of harassment during a major religious pilgrimage in South Asia, saying that crowded conditions sometimes created situations in which inappropriate behavior went unchecked.
Their accounts surfaced after a widely shared social media post encouraged others to speak about incidents they had previously kept private. Many of the women said they had never discussed what happened until they saw similar stories from fellow pilgrims online.
The event draws millions of worshipers each year and holds deep spiritual significance for those who attend. Managing crowds on that scale presents obvious challenges, especially around popular ritual sites where movement can slow to a standstill.
Some of the women interviewed said the harassment occurred in exactly those areas. A few sought help from security personnel, while others stayed silent. Several said they felt uncertain about how a complaint would be handled, and some worried about negative reactions from their communities.
Organizers said they maintain a zero-tolerance approach to harassment and have increased security measures, including the presence of more female officers. They encouraged anyone experiencing misconduct to report it.
The following testimonies have been condensed and edited for clarity. Certain names have been withheld at the request of the interviewees.
Anonymous woman, Malaysia
