I was attending with family members in 2018. At one point, we became separated in a very crowded section. While trying to make my way through, I noticed someone repeatedly touching my side and lower back.
Initially, I dismissed it as accidental contact. There were people everywhere. But when it kept happening even after I changed position, it became clear it wasn't random.
The person disappeared into the crowd before I could react. What stayed with me afterward was the surprise. I had never imagined I would encounter something like that during a religious journey.
Anonymous woman, Turkey
My experience started with comments. A man began speaking to me and a group of friends as we walked toward a prayer area. We ignored him, but he continued following us for a while.
Later, someone grabbed my arm in a crowded section of the site. It happened quickly, and I pulled away immediately.
I didn't report either incident. At the time, I felt it would be pointless, although now I'm not so sure.
Anonymous woman, Nigeria
I was attending for the first time when I was 19. The scale of the crowd was overwhelming, especially during one of the processions.
A man behind me kept pressing forward despite there being enough space not to. I remember feeling trapped because moving away wasn't easy.
For years I questioned my own interpretation of the situation. Only after reading other women's accounts did I realize my experience wasn't unique.
Sara Ahmed, Canada
One of my most uncomfortable moments happened near a busy market area after prayers.
A stranger started asking personal questions and continued following me when I chose not to answer. Eventually I moved closer to a larger group of women, and he stopped.
Compared with some stories, it may seem minor. Even so, it changed how I felt for the remainder of the trip. I became much more cautious about where I went and who was around me.
Anonymous woman, Jordan
I experienced unwanted touching during my first visit to the pilgrimage site. It happened in a densely packed area where people were moving slowly.
I never mentioned it to anyone. I was embarrassed and worried that the conversation would somehow focus on my behavior rather than the actions of the person involved.
Years later, I finally talked about it with friends. To my surprise, several had stories of their own. That realization made me understand why more women are choosing to speak publicly about these experiences today.

(CNN) Sweden is approaching a significant political transition. After more than ten years as prime minister, Sofia Lindberg is expected to step aside, bringing to a close a period of stability that has defined much of the country's recent political history.
Until recently, many assumed her center-right Alliance Party would remain firmly in control even after she left office. That confidence has weakened in recent months. A new poll showing the Progressive Green Movement ahead of the Alliance sparked fresh discussion about whether the balance of power may be shifting.
Party officials downplayed the result, arguing that support for the Greens had been boosted by the recent selection of a new leader. Yet polling trends suggest the change may be part of a larger pattern rather than a short-lived fluctuation. Environmental issues, housing affordability, and social policy have become increasingly important to many voters, particularly in larger cities.
As a result, few analysts now doubt that the Greens will have substantial influence after the next election. Whether they finish first or not, they are widely expected to be a key player in any coalition negotiations.
According to political scientist Helena Markström, the debate has moved beyond simple electoral arithmetic. The more important question is how much leverage the Greens could gain in shaping government priorities.
A Green breakthrough would carry symbolic significance across Europe, although it would not necessarily trigger a dramatic policy revolution. Many proposals once associated primarily with environmental parties have gradually been adopted by mainstream political groups over the past decade.
Even so, developments in Sweden are being watched closely elsewhere in the European Union. The country often plays an influential role in discussions surrounding climate regulation, technology policy, and economic reform.
The potential rise of the Greens also reflects a wider trend. Across Europe, long-established parties have found it increasingly difficult to hold together the broad coalitions that once guaranteed electoral success. New political movements continue to attract voters from different directions, reshaping national debates.
Some are motivated by concerns over climate change and social inequality, while others are drawn to anti-establishment or nationalist messages. The result is a more fragmented political landscape than many European governments have been accustomed to managing.
Officials in Brussels are paying close attention. Several major elections are approaching across the continent, and political volatility in one country often has implications beyond its borders.
Meanwhile, neighboring countries have experienced their own political shifts. New parties have gained ground, and issues such as migration, energy costs, and economic competitiveness remain central topics of public debate.
A stronger Green presence in Sweden could reinforce support for ambitious environmental policies within the EU. At the same time, it might expose differences among member states on questions related to industry, trade, and energy strategy.
Still, observers caution against expecting dramatic upheaval. Sweden's coalition-based system tends to reward compromise, meaning any future government would likely need to balance competing priorities.
What seems less uncertain is that European politics is becoming harder to predict. Voter loyalties are no longer as fixed as they once were, and parties across the spectrum are adapting to a changing electorate.
"The political environment is much more fluid than it was a decade ago," Markström said. "Success increasingly depends on understanding how quickly public expectations can change."

(CNN) Opening night could hardly have unfolded better for the Denver Nuggets.
The evening began with a celebration of last season's championship success. Fans filled the arena early to watch the unveiling of a new banner and the distribution of championship rings, marking one of the most significant achievements in franchise history. Video tributes played throughout the ceremony, and players took time to acknowledge the crowd that had supported them during their title run.
The rings themselves were packed with symbolic details. According to the team, the design incorporates references to Denver's championship season, playoff accomplishments, and the organization's long journey to its first NBA title. Personalized engravings were also included, giving each player a unique reminder of the achievement.
Once the formalities ended, the Nuggets quickly turned their attention to basketball—and looked every bit like defending champions.
Nikola Jokić led the way with a triple-double of 29 points, 13 rebounds, and 11 assists as Denver defeated the Dallas Mavericks 118-101. The game remained relatively close early on, but Denver's control became more evident as the night progressed. A strong third quarter created separation, and the Mavericks never fully recovered.
Luka Dončić kept Dallas within striking distance for stretches, yet the Nuggets consistently found answers on both ends of the floor. Their ball movement, defensive discipline, and experience were on display throughout the second half.
After the game, Jokić spoke briefly about the banner ceremony. While he admitted it was a meaningful moment, his focus had already shifted toward the long season ahead.
"The memories will always be there," he said. "But now it's about the next challenge."
Elsewhere around the league, the season started on a less positive note for the Phoenix Suns.
Hosting the Minnesota Timberwolves, Phoenix fell 115-108 despite productive nights from its two biggest stars. Devin Booker scored 31 points and Kevin Durant added 28, but the Suns struggled to find enough support from the rest of the roster.
Minnesota gradually took control after halftime. Anthony Edwards paced the visitors with 27 points and delivered several important baskets during the closing stages, helping seal the win.
Even after the victory, Edwards was not entirely satisfied with the performance. He pointed to several areas that still need improvement but acknowledged that beginning the season with a win against a strong opponent was encouraging.
One reason for Minnesota's success was the contribution it received across the lineup. Different players stepped up at different times, allowing the Timberwolves to maintain their intensity throughout the game.
Denver, though, was the team that ended the night with the spotlight. The championship banner now has a permanent place in the arena, and the convincing win that followed served as an early statement that the Nuggets intend to remain firmly in the title conversation this season.

(CNN) Professional soccer player Alex Morgan has revealed that he has been diagnosed with stage one testicular cancer and will take a break from the game while beginning treatment.
The 26-year-old Bristol City defender shared the news on social media Thursday, explaining that the condition was discovered after a recent series of medical tests.
In a written statement, Morgan said he would start treatment shortly and remain focused on making a full recovery.
The diagnosis came as an unexpected development for both the player and his family. Even so, Morgan said doctors have given him reason to be optimistic. Testicular cancer is often highly treatable when identified at an early stage, and medical specialists have reportedly provided encouraging assessments of his prognosis.
"My priority now is getting through treatment and recovering properly," he wrote. "I'm hopeful that I'll be able to return to football once that process is complete."
Morgan had recently pulled out of international team activities after experiencing symptoms that required further investigation. He credited medical personnel for identifying the issue quickly and helping him receive specialist care.
He also expressed gratitude toward the doctors, nurses, and healthcare staff who have assisted him since the diagnosis, as well as the medical teams at both club and national level.
The announcement prompted an immediate response from across the football world. Fellow players, supporters, and clubs sent messages of encouragement, while Bristol City publicly pledged its support.
In a statement, the club emphasized that Morgan's health comes before any football considerations and said he would be given all the time necessary to focus on treatment and recovery.
Widely regarded as one of the country's emerging defensive talents, Morgan has become an important figure for both club and national team in recent years. For now, however, football has taken a back seat.
He ended his statement by thanking supporters for their kindness and asking that his family's privacy be respected during the months ahead.
"The support I've received already means a great deal," he said. "I'm looking forward to the day I can return, but right now my focus is entirely on getting well."

(CNN) Emma Richardson was on the road with her college volleyball team when messages from friends and relatives began arriving on her phone. Back home in eastern Tennessee, severe flooding had caused widespread damage across the community where she grew up.
The images were difficult to process.
"You know every street and every landmark," Richardson said. "Then suddenly you're looking at pictures and it barely resembles the place you remember."
Days of intense rainfall had overwhelmed parts of the region, damaging homes, businesses, and public buildings. Many residents were forced from their properties as emergency crews responded to the flooding.
For Richardson, the disaster struck close to home in every sense.
"My family is there. A lot of my closest friends are there," she explained. "It's always been the place I think of when someone asks where I'm from."
Not long after her team's match concluded, she began looking for ways to help. Rather than waiting for others to organize relief efforts, Richardson set up an online fundraiser aimed at supporting affected families and local recovery programs.
She promoted the campaign through social media, and support arrived quickly. Donations came from teammates, alumni, sports fans, and people who had never even visited the town.
The fundraiser eventually surpassed $120,000, money that will be directed toward relief organizations and rebuilding projects within the community.
Still, Richardson felt there was more she could do.
Earlier this week she traveled back to Tennessee and joined local volunteers working on cleanup efforts. Her days were spent clearing debris, delivering supplies, and meeting residents beginning the difficult process of rebuilding.
"Being there changes everything," she said. "You hear people's stories firsthand, and you realize how much they've lost."
Despite the destruction, what stood out most to her was the response from the community itself. Neighbors were helping one another, businesses were providing resources, and volunteers continued to arrive from surrounding areas.
According to Richardson, that sense of solidarity has always been part of the town's identity.
"People don't wait around for someone else to step in," she said. "They help however they can."
For the athlete, returning home was about more than volunteering. It was also an opportunity to support a community that had encouraged her throughout her life and athletic career.
"This town helped shape who I am," Richardson said. "If I can give something back during a time like this, then that's exactly what I want to do."

(CNN) Across Canada, discussions about Indigenous rights and historical injustice have taken on new urgency in recent years. For many Indigenous people, the period has been marked by both greater visibility and lingering frustration over how slowly change can occur.
The discovery of unmarked graves near former residential schools became a turning point in the national conversation. Communities that had long spoken about the legacy of those institutions suddenly found their experiences receiving widespread public attention.
"It was emotional for a lot of families," said community advocate Rachel Whitecloud. "There was sadness, but there was also a feeling that people were finally willing to hear what Indigenous communities had been saying for generations."
At first, that shift generated optimism. Some believed it could lead to deeper reforms and a stronger commitment from governments and public institutions.
Over time, however, many advocates began questioning whether awareness was translating into action. Problems involving housing, healthcare, education, and basic infrastructure remain pressing concerns in numerous communities.
The role of the media has become part of that discussion as well. While Indigenous issues receive more coverage than they once did, critics argue that attention often peaks during major controversies and fades afterward.
"We tend to become visible during moments of tragedy," said lecturer Daniel Cardinal. "What gets missed are the everyday stories—the achievements, the culture, the people building positive change in their communities."
At the same time, Indigenous voices have become more prominent on platforms outside traditional media. Social media creators, podcasters, and independent journalists are reaching audiences directly, often without relying on established institutions to tell their stories.
One of them is Maya Stone, whose online content focuses on Indigenous identity and public affairs. She says audiences are increasingly interested in hearing firsthand perspectives.
"For a long time, the conversation was happening about Indigenous people rather than with Indigenous people," Stone said. "That's starting to change."
The broader debate now extends beyond historical recognition. Many activists argue that symbolic gestures, while important, are not enough on their own.
According to Whitecloud, public understanding has improved noticeably, but expectations have grown as well.
"People want more than acknowledgment," she said. "They want to see results."
Government officials point to a range of programs and commitments designed to address long-standing inequalities. Yet many community leaders remain skeptical, arguing that progress often moves more slowly than promised.
Still, there is little doubt that the national mood has shifted. Topics that once occupied the margins of public debate now receive regular attention from politicians, educators, and the media.
Whether that momentum produces lasting change remains an open question. What seems clearer is that Indigenous communities are playing a larger role in shaping the conversation themselves.
As Cardinal put it, "The expectation now is accountability. Once people start paying attention, it's much harder to look away."

A version of this story appeared in CNN's Royal News newsletter, which follows developments involving the British royal family and public life at the palace.
London (CNN) A handful of recent appearances by Prince George alongside his parents have once again sparked curiosity about the future of the monarchy.
Photos from official events circulated widely online, prompting familiar questions from royal enthusiasts about how the young prince is being prepared for a life that will almost certainly involve significant public responsibility.
George, the eldest child of the Prince and Princess of Wales, is currently second in line to the British throne. Yet despite the attention surrounding him, his constitutional position is remarkably straightforward. Like every other member of the royal succession, his place was determined automatically at birth.
Observers often speculate about future kings and queens, but succession is governed by law rather than personal choice. Neither popularity nor preference determines who comes next.
One notable reform arrived in 2013, when rules were updated so that birth order—not gender—would decide succession among royal siblings. That change ensured Princess Charlotte would remain ahead of Prince Louis despite being female, a move widely viewed as a modernization of centuries-old tradition.
Still, legal arrangements only explain part of the public fascination. Many people are equally interested in how younger royals navigate life under constant observation.
In recent years, the monarchy has found itself at the center of debates about tradition, accountability, and its place in contemporary Britain. Supporters see stability and continuity; critics question whether hereditary institutions still fit modern democratic societies.
For now, those arguments remain largely separate from George himself. His future role is tied less to public discussion than to constitutional reality.
WHAT ELSE IS GOING ON?
The King honors community volunteers
King Charles traveled to Manchester this week to recognize volunteers whose work has supported local communities across the country.
Addressing guests at the ceremony, he praised their commitment and highlighted the importance of civic participation in strengthening communities.
Waleses focus on youth programs
The Prince and Princess of Wales visited a youth center in Birmingham where they met students and staff involved in mentoring initiatives.
The engagement centered on education, skills development, and support programs designed to help young people transition into employment.
New royal documentary draws viewers
A television documentary exploring the history of royal residences premiered this week.
Featuring historians and heritage experts, the program examines how several well-known royal properties evolved over time. Viewer interest has been strong since its release.
PHOTO OF THE WEEK
During a visit to a coastal conservation project, King Charles met volunteers working to restore natural habitats and protect vulnerable stretches of shoreline.
Environmental causes have long been among the King's priorities, and he used the visit to highlight the importance of long-term conservation efforts.
FROM THE ROYAL VAULT
This week also marks an anniversary connected to Buckingham Palace's summer opening to the public.
What began as a relatively novel initiative decades ago has since become a fixture of the tourist calendar. Each year, thousands of visitors pass through rooms that were once accessible only to invited guests and officials.
The opening offered a rare look inside one of Britain's most recognizable buildings—and helped reshape the relationship between the monarchy and the public in subtle but lasting ways.

Paris, France (CNN) — French President Emmanuel Macron called the current moment a "decisive turning point" for climate action and mounted a defense of the Paris Agreement framework in an exclusive interview Wednesday with CNN's Becky Anderson, speaking on the sidelines of a major environmental conference in the French capital.
Macron didn't shy away from the frustration simmering among developing nations — those absorbing the worst climate impacts while having done the least to cause them. "We have to be honest," he said. "The countries suffering most are not the ones responsible for the bulk of emissions. That asymmetry is a moral problem, and it has to sit at the center of everything we negotiate."
He welcomed recent pledges on methane and deforestation, but was quick to add a caveat. Commitments on paper, he said, are only as good as the legislation and financing that follow. "Announcements are not action. We have made that mistake before."
France's nuclear energy policy drew pointed questions, and Macron didn't flinch. "Nuclear gives us low-carbon baseload power. I will not apologize for that. The climate doesn't care about ideology — it cares about emissions." He noted that France was pressing ahead with offshore wind and solar investment at the same time, framing the two as complementary rather than contradictory.
China's absence from key summit sessions hung over parts of the discussion. Macron urged patience, insisting bilateral conversations were ongoing, but acknowledged the gap plainly. "I will not pretend there is no difference between what the science demands and what some major emitters are currently willing to commit to."
On hope — the question reporters keep putting to leaders — Macron resisted both poles. "Despair is not a strategy. Neither is blind optimism." Young people, he said, are right to demand honesty, about the scale of the crisis and about whether governments are genuinely responding to it.
Domestically, the transition has not been painless. The 2018 Yellow Vest protests, ignited in part by a fuel tax hike, cast a long shadow. Macron acknowledged the tension directly. A green transition that falls hardest on working people, he argued, is one that will eventually collapse under its own political weight. "Social justice and climate justice are not in opposition. They have to be built together."

(CNN) Marcus Osei, a former youth academy player at Leicester City Football Club, has come forward with allegations of racial abuse — saying a member of the coaching staff made derogatory remarks referencing his Ghanaian heritage when he was 17.
Osei made the claims in an interview with Sky Sports, describing a training session in which a senior coach criticized his defensive positioning before making the comments.
"I was completely taken aback. No one had ever spoken to me like that in a professional setting. I just went quiet," he said.
Leicester City told CNN on Wednesday it had not previously been aware of the allegation. "We take all allegations of racism and discrimination extremely seriously and are committed to conducting a thorough and transparent investigation," the club said.
CNN has reached out to Osei for additional comment. He told Sky Sports he never reported the incident at the time.
"You think about saying something. But I was 17, still trying to prove myself — I didn't want to jeopardize my place at the club," he said. The experience, he added, followed him: "I started doubting whether I truly belonged there. In team meetings I just kept my head down."
The allegations surface as English football faces wider scrutiny over racism in its academy system. The club's director of youth development stepped down earlier this month amid separate complaints from former players about the culture inside the academy.
A parliamentary inquiry into racism across professional football academies is expected to open hearings next month. Following high-profile cases at several Premier League clubs, the Football Association announced in October a review of its safeguarding procedures for youth players.

(CNN) When Momiji Nishiya stepped onto the podium at the Tokyo Olympics, she was 13 years old and already a gold medalist. The women's street skateboarding event was making its Olympic debut, and Nishiya won it in front of empty stands — Covid-19 had banned spectators — though you wouldn't have known the arena was quiet from the energy among the skaters themselves.
Her performance was technically sharp and eerily calm for someone barely a teenager, drawing open admiration from her competitors. Funa Nakayama, also Japanese, took silver. Brazil's Rayssa Leal claimed bronze at the same age as Nishiya — 13 — completing a podium that looked less like a traditional Olympic lineup and more like a middle school reunion.
'We're all friends out here'
One of the more striking things about the street skateboarding final wasn't the difficulty of the tricks. It was how the skaters treated each other. After every run, they clustered together to cheer, hug, and celebrate — regardless of scores, regardless of country. For Leal, who had become a global sensation years earlier after a video of her skating in a fairy costume went viral, this was just skateboarding being skateboarding. "We grow up skating together, watching each other's videos," she told reporters. "When your friend lands something amazing, you're happy for them. That's just how it is."
Three teenagers on the podium. All three skating since they were toddlers. It felt less like a passing of the torch and more like confirmation that the torch had already changed hands.
Finding the love early
Leal's route to Tokyo was built on concrete — literally. Growing up in Vitória, Brazil, she started skating at seven after watching videos online and convincing her father to buy her a board. There were no elite training centers, no coaching pipelines. Just streets, a board, and a kid who couldn't get enough of it. That foundation, she says, still drives everything. "I never want to lose the feeling of just skating for fun. That's where everything started."
The silver medal changed her life in the ways you'd expect — sponsors, campaigns, the sudden weight of public attention. But she has kept returning to the same idea: visibility matters most if it opens doors for other girls, especially in places where sports infrastructure barely exists.
What comes next
Paris 2024 was already on Leal's mind before the Tokyo podium ceremony was over. She has been broadening her competitive range while also leaning into her platform, collaborating with brands and fronting campaigns aimed at drawing more girls into skating globally.
She's still a teenager. Most of her career remains ahead of her. And she seems, genuinely, unbothered by the magnitude of that. "I just want to keep skating, keep learning, keep having fun," she said. "If I do that, I think everything else follows."

(CNN) Arsenal's long wait for a Premier League title got longer on Tuesday. A surprise home defeat to relegation-threatened Wolverhampton Wanderers — settled by a second-half header from Matheus Cunha — left them eight points adrift of Chelsea, who also hold a game in hand.
The two title rivals meet at Stamford Bridge on Saturday, a fixture that had been circled for weeks as a potential turning point. At this rate, it may already be too late. "We have to be honest — right now the gap is very big," midfielder Thomas Partey said after the final whistle.
Arteta's side had arrived at the Emirates in decent form, three straight wins behind them, and faced a Wolves team scrapping to stay in the division. None of that seemed to matter. Arsenal looked labored and disconnected for long stretches, managing just two shots on target all night.
It continued a worrying pattern at home, where they have won only once in their last five league matches. Captain Martin Odegaard didn't sugarcoat it. "We were not sharp, not aggressive enough. Wolves wanted it more than us and that showed," he said.
Arteta cited injuries and fixture congestion, pointing in particular to the three-week absence of Bukayo Saka, whose pace has been badly missed in the final third. "We are managing a very thin squad," he said. "But that cannot be an excuse. The performance tonight was not acceptable."
Chelsea pull further clear
While Arsenal stumbled, Chelsea had a near-perfect evening. A 3-1 win over Aston Villa at Stamford Bridge — goals from Raheem Sterling, Cole Palmer, and a late Nicolas Jackson — stretched their winning run to eleven games across all competitions and sent the home crowd home in good spirits.
Head coach Enzo Maresca refused to let his squad settle into self-congratulation. "We are in a good moment, but I never allow my players to feel comfortable," he said. A seventh clean sheet in eight league games had gone begging only thanks to a late consolation from Villa.
Sterling, who opened the scoring just before halftime with a composed finish, kept his focus firmly on Saturday. "Every game feels like a final at this stage," he said. "We know Arsenal are coming and it won't be easy — but we feel confident in how we've been playing."

(CNN) Antonio Conte says he is "deeply concerned" by the racist abuse directed at Inter Milan striker Marcus Thuram during Tuesday's Champions League group stage win over Dynamo Kyiv, which Inter took 2-0.
Thuram, the French international and son of World Cup winner Lilian Thuram, reported being subjected to monkey chants from a section of the away support in the 58th minute — shortly after he opened the scoring. He reacted visibly, teammates quickly surrounded him, and the referee paused play briefly before allowing the match to continue.
UEFA's three-step protocol for racist incidents calls for play to be paused, then suspended, and ultimately abandoned if abuse persists. On Tuesday, the first step was triggered once. The match was neither suspended nor abandoned, despite further reported incidents after the restart.
Conte spoke with Thuram in the dressing room after the final whistle and described him as "shaken but strong."
"Marcus is a strong man and a strong player. But no one should have to experience that — not in football, not anywhere," he told reporters. "What happened tonight is not acceptable. UEFA has rules. I want to see those rules applied fully and without compromise."
He didn't stop there. Conte questioned, bluntly, whether governing bodies were serious about enforcement. "We keep seeing these incidents. We keep hearing the same statements afterward. And then next week, another match, another incident. At some point you have to ask: are the punishments actually working? Because from where I'm standing, the answer is no."
Inter confirmed they would file a formal complaint with UEFA and called for a full investigation. Dynamo Kyiv's response was brief — denying their supporters had engaged in organized chanting and pledging to cooperate with any inquiry.
UEFA said it had opened disciplinary proceedings and that the referee's report formally documented the incidents. A hearing before the Control, Ethics and Disciplinary Body was scheduled within two weeks.
Thuram addressed the night on social media without naming anyone directly. "I play this game because I love it," he wrote. "Hate will never change that. But it has to stop." His father, Lilian — long one of football's most prominent anti-racism voices — shared the post with a single line: "Proud of my son. This fight is not over."
CNN has reached out to both Dynamo Kyiv and UEFA for further comment.

A version of this story appeared in CNN's Meanwhile in China newsletter, a three-times-a-week update exploring what you need to know about the country's rise and how it impacts the world. Sign up here.
Shanghai (CNN) — Five weeks into a lockdown of one of the world's largest cities, Chinese authorities are showing no sign of backing down — and the 25 million people confined inside Shanghai are running out of patience.
Since late March, residents have been restricted to their homes or residential compounds, allowed outside only for mandatory PCR testing or medical emergencies. Food distribution, managed through neighborhood committees, has been inconsistent enough that some people have gone days without adequate supplies. Videos of residents shouting from apartment windows, banging pots in protest, and confronting hazmat-suited officials have spread rapidly on Chinese social media — before being taken down. The volume of public dissent is unusual in a country where such expressions are tightly controlled.
When the lockdown began, officials described it as a targeted, rolling measure covering two districts in sequence. It now covers the entire city, with no clear end date.
Tens of thousands of cases are being recorded daily. The overwhelming majority are asymptomatic or mild. That distinction doesn't matter under China's zero-Covid policy, reaffirmed publicly by President Xi Jinping as recently as last month: every positive case must be transferred to centralized quarantine, regardless of how sick — or not sick — the person actually is. Exhibition halls, stadiums and schools have been converted into quarantine centers. Reports of overcrowding, poor sanitation and insufficient food have filtered out, despite efforts to control the narrative.
The economic ripple effects extend well beyond city limits. Shanghai's port is the largest in China and among the busiest in the world. Weeks of disruption have deepened backlogs and added strain to global supply chains that were already fragile after two years of pandemic turbulence.
A diverging world
Elsewhere, the direction of travel is clear. Across Europe, North America and much of Southeast Asia, governments have moved on — dropping mask mandates, reopening borders, scaling back testing. Britain lifted all legal restrictions in February. The US ended its public health emergency in May. In Asia, South Korea dropped its outdoor mask mandate in April. Japan has cautiously reopened to tourists. Thailand and Vietnam, once among the region's more aggressive suppressors, have pivoted toward living with the virus as vaccination rates climbed.
China stands alone among major economies still committed to elimination. That position has become harder to sustain as Omicron — far more transmissible than earlier variants — has led many epidemiologists outside China to conclude that zero-Covid is no longer a realistic long-term goal. The question, in most of the world, has shifted from whether to transition away from the policy to how and when.
Beijing, for now, isn't engaging with that question publicly. State media frames the lockdown as necessary sacrifice. Officials point to China's comparatively low death toll as proof the approach has worked. But with growth forecasts being cut, factories sitting idle, and citizens more openly frustrated than at almost any point in recent memory, the pressure is building — quietly, and not yet visibly, but building all the same.
CNN's Beijing bureau contributed to this report.

(CNN) College basketball's 2023 season has given fans just about everything they could ask for — packed arenas, raucous student sections, and the kind of rivalry matchups that turn March into its own national holiday.
Saturday brings the best of it. Top-ranked Connecticut and third-ranked Marquette meet in the Big East Championship at Madison Square Garden, the self-declared mecca of the sport, and both programs have something serious to play for.
The Husky Machine
UConn has been here before. Five national championships, a tradition of tournament excellence, and Dan Hurley, now in his sixth season in Storrs, who has quietly turned the Huskies back into one of the sport's most feared programs.
This season followed a familiar script. Four players left for the NBA Draft after last spring's title run. Hurley reloaded. The Huskies started 7-0 before a late scare at Villanova — trailing by nine with four minutes left — exposed some early-season fragility. That's when freshman guard Solo Ball took over. Eight straight points, a game-winning layup with eleven seconds remaining. UConn won 74-73 and has not lost since.
Ball has become the season's defining story. Hesitant and turnover-prone in October, he's now the Big East's leading Player of the Year candidate, giving UConn something even their title team lacked: someone who thrives when the game is on the line.
"He's grown up before our eyes," Hurley said after the regular season finale. "When the moment gets big, he rises."
A 17-1 conference record is the result. It is the most dominant Big East regular season in years.
The Golden Eagles Circle
Marquette started the year unranked and nobody outside Milwaukee expected much. Shaka Smart, in his third season with the program, had been building toward something — and it became clear early on that this group was different.
Five players averaging double figures. The program's first to do that in over two decades. Defense that suffocates from the perimeter. The Golden Eagles climbed steadily into the top ten before a two-game stumble in late January — losses to Providence and Georgetown — threatened to unravel the whole thing.
Smart's response was to simplify. A smaller, quicker lineup, a stripped-down offense. Marquette won eight straight to close out the regular season, capped by an 81-64 demolition of then-No. 6 Creighton on the road. Senior guard Kam Jones averaged 22 points per game over that stretch and started drawing comparisons to the program's celebrated alumni.
"We went through a tough stretch and it made us better," Jones said. "We stopped trying to be perfect and started playing together."
What's at stake?
UConn wants a Big East title, a No. 1 seed, and the right to call themselves undisputed national frontrunners. Most of the country already believes it. A few voters still don't. Winning Saturday would settle the argument.
Marquette's situation is more pointed. Smart is 0-4 against UConn since his arrival, and the Golden Eagles haven't won the Big East Tournament since 2003. A win would erase both of those at once — and send the program into Selection Sunday on the kind of momentum that translates to NCAA Tournament runs.
Two years ago a Marquette team reached the Elite Eight before falling to eventual champion Kansas. Smart believes this group is better. Saturday will go a long way toward finding out.
"We've put ourselves in a position to compete for something real," he said this week. "Nobody's going to hand us anything — especially not them."

(CNN) Should Saturday's game be broadcast on ESPN or preserved in the Smithsonian?
That question has been hanging over the second-round NBA playoff series between LeBron James and Kevin Durant all week — the first postseason matchup in league history featuring two opposing players each with more than 27,000 career points still playing at an elite level.
James, 40, posted a black-and-white photo of the two men shaking hands at midcourt on Instagram ahead of the series. One word underneath: "Legacy." Durant replied within the hour. "Let's give them something to remember."
Between them, these two men account for eight championships, six scoring titles, four Olympic gold medals, and more All-NBA selections than any two players in the modern era. Both sit in the all-time top five in points scored. The argument over which of them is the most skilled offensive player the game has produced has been running for the better part of a decade and shows no sign of resolution.
Older, not slower
Dismissing either man would be a mistake.
James is in his 22nd season, completing a one-year deal with Golden State that raised plenty of eyebrows when it was announced last summer. He has averaged 24 points, nine assists, and seven rebounds — numbers that would be impressive for someone ten years younger — and silenced early skepticism about whether he could adapt to Steve Kerr's system. The short answer: he has.
The Warriors and Durant's Phoenix Suns crossed paths twice in the regular season. Phoenix won both times, including a February blowout in which Durant dropped 38 and held James to 19 on nine-of-twenty-two shooting. James brought it up unprompted on Thursday. "That one stung," he said. "But we're a different team now. I'm a different player. People are starting to see what we've built here."
Durant came into the weekend fresh off a five-game dismantling of a young Oklahoma City team, averaging 31 points while making it look easier than it was. Frank Vogel has spent five seasons building a Suns offense around what Durant does, and the results — three Western Conference Finals, one title in 2022 — speak for themselves.
"This is where you want to be," Durant said after closing out the Thunder. "I've been thinking about this matchup since the bracket came out."
What's at stake?
For James, a title with a third different franchise — following rings with Cleveland and the Lakers — would be something no player has ever done. The greatest-ever argument is one most observers consider settled in his favor already. Another championship wouldn't change many minds. It would just make the case harder to argue against.
For Durant, this is widely expected to be his last season regardless of outcome. A second championship, won head-to-head against the defining player of his era, would be the kind of ending a career like his deserves.
The two have met three times before in the playoffs. Durant's teams hold a 2-1 advantage. Every series has produced at least one sequence that still comes up in barbershop arguments years later.
"I've known for a long time this is what the end would look like," Durant said Thursday. "Competing at the highest level, against the best. That's all I've ever wanted."

(CNN) It was sold as an unbreakable private communications tool — premium encrypted messaging, marketed to anyone who needed their conversations completely off the grid. What it actually became was one of the most productive surveillance assets European law enforcement has ever run against organized crime.
Authorities in the Netherlands, France, and Belgium announced Monday that they had secretly infiltrated EncroChat, an encrypted network with tens of thousands of users across Europe, for nearly two years. The result: over 115 million intercepted messages, more than 1,500 arrests across the continent, upwards of 30 metric tons of narcotics seized, and more than 180 million euros in cash and assets confiscated.
The operation, codenamed "Operation Emma," was coordinated through Europol and Eurojust, with national police forces from more than a dozen countries involved. Europol Executive Director Catherine De Bolle, speaking in The Hague on Monday, called it a turning point. "For the first time, we were able to read the real-time communications of criminal organizations at scale," she said. "These were not low-level offenders. These were the planners, the financiers, the decision-makers."
EncroChat handsets changed hands on the black market for several thousand euros each. They were modified smartphones — camera disabled, microphone disabled, GPS stripped out — that could only communicate with other EncroChat devices. The platform's own promotional material promised, in so many words, total operational security. At its peak, it had an estimated 60,000 users worldwide. Authorities allege the overwhelming majority were criminals.
The breakthrough came when French cyber-investigators managed to implant malware on EncroChat's servers, which were physically located in France, allowing them to intercept messages before encryption kicked in. The covert phase ran for several months until EncroChat administrators detected the intrusion in June 2020 and shut everything down, sending users a blunt instruction: destroy your handsets immediately.
By that point, it was too late. The intercepted messages contained detailed discussions of drug shipments, weapons deals, contract killings, and money laundering arrangements. In several cases, law enforcement stepped in before planned attacks occurred — including, Dutch officials said, the disruption of at least three alleged contract murders in the Netherlands.
The operation has not been without controversy. Defense lawyers across multiple jurisdictions have challenged whether EncroChat evidence is admissible in court, arguing the mass interception of private messages violated defendants' rights. Cases in Germany and the United Kingdom are still moving through the courts, with judges reaching different conclusions about how the data was gathered and shared across borders.
More arrests are expected, Europol said Monday, as investigators continue working through the material collected. Related operations targeting networks that emerged in EncroChat's wake are already underway.
"Criminal networks will always look for new tools," De Bolle said. "But this operation has shown that no platform is beyond reach."

(CNN) Brentford manager Thomas Frank says he and members of his coaching staff have received threatening messages on social media after a controversial substitution during the weekend's defeat was blamed by some supporters for costing the club the match.
Frank confirmed Monday that he had reported the abuse to police and contacted the Premier League. The messages, which included threats of physical harm, followed widespread criticism of his decision to withdraw a key forward in the second half.
"It is deeply upsetting — not just for me but for my family and for staff members who have nothing to do with decisions made on the pitch," Frank said in a statement released by the club. "I have been in football long enough to know that fans are passionate. But threats of violence are far beyond any line."
The Premier League's response was unequivocal. "Threats of violence directed at any manager, player, official, or member of staff are completely unacceptable," a spokesperson said. "We fully support Thomas and his staff in reporting this to police."
The Professional Footballers' Association and the League Managers Association followed with a joint statement, tying Frank's experience to a broader pattern. Managers, players, and officials, they argued, are routinely subjected to abuse that would be treated as a serious criminal matter in any other profession. Social media companies, they said, needed to act — proactively, not reactively.
Metropolitan Police confirmed they had received a report. "Threatening behavior online is not consequence-free," a spokesperson said. "Those found to have committed offenses will be pursued."
Frank is expected to manage Thursday's fixture. Ahead of it, he spoke briefly to reporters — composed, but not hiding his frustration. "I love this game. I love managing in this league," he said. "But it has to be safe for everyone — not just the players on the pitch, but the people on the touchline, in the offices, and at home."
Several players posted messages of support, and Britain's Sports Minister used the incident to reiterate the government's position: the forthcoming Online Safety legislation would require platforms to act swiftly against criminal abuse, with legal consequences for those that don't.

Brussels, Belgium
Flemish nationalist parties won enough seats on Sunday to extend their majority in Belgium's regional parliament, though a stronger-than-expected performance from Flemish social democrats signaled that the road to a new coalition government in Brussels would be anything but smooth.
With nearly 99% of ballots counted, the combined nationalist bloc claimed 52.3% of the vote — their strongest result in a decade. A continuation of the existing center-right Flemish coalition appeared the most likely outcome, though smaller parties retained enough leverage to complicate negotiations significantly.
None of this is likely to produce any immediate push toward formal secession. The question of Belgium's constitutional future — the long-running tension between Dutch-speaking Flanders and French-speaking Wallonia — remains live, but voters on Sunday were more preoccupied with energy prices and the cost of living than with the country's survival as a single state.
Turnout came in at 68%, down from 74% in the previous regional election. Analysts suggested the lower figure may have modestly favored nationalist parties, whose supporters tend to show up in greater numbers when broader enthusiasm fades.
The centre-right New Flemish Alliance, or N-VA, held its position as the region's dominant political force, winning an estimated 29 seats in the 124-seat Flemish Parliament. Party leader Bart De Wever framed the result as a mandate for deeper autonomy and a renegotiation of fiscal arrangements between the regions.
"Flanders has spoken clearly," De Wever told supporters in Ghent on Sunday night. "We want control over our own future — our own budget, our own social policy. That conversation cannot be delayed any longer."
The far-right Vlaams Belang, which has spent several election cycles eating into N-VA's nationalist base, won around 26 seats — a modest decline from its previous peak. Leader Tom Van Grieken nonetheless insisted the result was a further rebuke of what he called Belgium's "dysfunctional federal model."
The evening's real story, though, may have been Vooruit. The Flemish social democrats picked up four seats to reach 21, instantly positioning themselves as a potentially pivotal player in coalition talks. Leader Melissa Depraeter was blunt about what she thought Sunday's vote actually meant.
"People are worried about their energy bills, their wages, their children's futures," she told reporters as results came in. "They did not vote tonight for an abstract debate about borders."
For federal Prime Minister Georges-Louis Bouchez, the results were mixed at best. N-VA's dominance deepens pressure on his already-strained coalition to accelerate stalled talks on a new state reform — a process that has collapsed repeatedly over the past several years.
Constitutional experts were quick to note, as they tend to after every Flemish election, that the formal partition of Belgium would require approval across multiple parliaments and a level of cross-community consensus that remains nowhere in sight.
For now, the question is more prosaic: who governs Flanders, and what they will have to give away to do it.

(CNN) The Paris 2024 Summer Olympics are finally here — and after years of anticipation and months of mounting anxiety about whether the French capital could pull it off, competitive events are already underway.
Football and rugby sevens kicked off Tuesday across multiple French cities, as is standard practice before the formal opening. But early enthusiasm has been dampened by a familiar mixture of security concerns, transport headaches, and questions that tend to follow any city that takes on the extraordinary burden of hosting the world's largest sporting event.
French authorities have deployed around 45,000 police officers and 10,000 military personnel across Paris — the most extensive domestic security operation in the country's history, officials say. Most of the attention has fallen on Friday's opening ceremony, which will take place not inside a stadium but along a six-kilometer stretch of the Seine River, with up to 300,000 ticket holders and 222 national delegations expected to participate in a floating parade that has never been attempted at this scale before.
"There are risks," Paris 2024 chief organizer Tony Estanguet conceded at a press conference Monday. "We are managing them seriously and with every resource available."
Days before the ceremony, French authorities arrested several individuals in connection with an alleged plot to disrupt the Games, triggering an emergency security review. The interior ministry confirmed the arrests but offered few details, citing the ongoing investigation.
Getting around Paris has been its own ordeal. The metro, already near capacity in peak tourist season, has buckled under the additional load. Long queues at Charles de Gaulle airport and widespread delays on regional rail lines drew complaints from delegations arriving over the weekend.
The athletes' village in Saint-Denis has also attracted unwanted attention. The absence of air conditioning — a deliberate environmental decision by organizers, who provided distributed cooling units as an alternative — prompted immediate pushback from several delegations. The United States, Britain, Canada, and others quietly installed their own systems at their own cost. American swimmer Caeleb Dressel, chasing more gold after his five medals in Tokyo, kept it light when asked. "It's warm," he said, smiling. "We've dealt with it."
Los Angeles looks ahead
Even as Paris opens, the Olympic world is already thinking about what comes next. The LA28 organizing committee is expected to present a progress report to the IOC later this week, with officials describing preparations as on track for the 2028 Games — the third time Los Angeles will host the Olympics, after 1932 and the celebrated 1984 edition that is still credited with rescuing the Olympic movement from near-collapse.
LA28 chairman Casey Wasserman was watching Paris closely. "Every Games teaches the next host something new," he told reporters at a pre-opening reception. "We are watching, learning, and preparing."
Brisbane, confirmed in 2021 as host of the 2032 Games, will follow. It will be Australia's third turn — after Melbourne in 1956 and Sydney in 2000 — and organizers there, too, will be taking notes this week.

(CNN) Los Angeles Dodgers outfielder Cody Bellinger says he is lucky to be unhurt after a highway accident outside Phoenix, Arizona on Saturday, just days before the Dodgers open spring training in Glendale.
Bellinger told reporters Sunday that he was driving to the team's facility when a vehicle traveling at high speed crossed into his lane on Interstate 10 and sideswiped his SUV, forcing it into the guardrail. The impact left his vehicle heavily damaged on the driver's side. Bellinger said he sat stunned for a moment before realizing he hadn't been seriously hurt.
"It happened so fast," he said. "One second everything is normal, and then you're just trying to figure out what happened. I'm really lucky."
The 2019 National League MVP said the experience had reset his thinking before a single spring training pitch had been thrown. "You get so caught up in the grind and the preparation that you forget to step back and appreciate that you're healthy and you're here," he said. "Yesterday reminded me of that pretty quickly."
Bellinger's agent confirmed he had been examined by Dodgers medical staff Sunday morning and cleared. He showed up to the complex that afternoon and took part in light workouts, according to a team spokesperson.
The Arizona Department of Public Safety confirmed the crash and said the other driver had been cited for an unsafe lane change. No other vehicles were involved, and no injuries were reported.
Bellinger, entering his third season in Los Angeles after signing a multi-year extension last winter, is expected to be ready when the Dodgers begin their Cactus League schedule next week. His first call after the accident, he said, was to his family.
"I just wanted them to know I was OK," he said. "Honestly, I'm just happy to be here talking to you guys."

(CNN) A seven-year federal investigation into corruption at the heart of international athletics ended Wednesday, with the US Department of Justice announcing it would return $165 million to World Athletics, the International Association of Athletics Federations, and several national federations identified as victims of a wide-ranging bribery scheme tied to doping cover-ups.
The DOJ concluded that officials across multiple organizations had accepted payments from athletes and coaches in exchange for burying positive drug test results — allowing competitors to perform at the Olympic level while concealing banned substance use from anti-doping authorities.
"This was a profound betrayal of clean athletes everywhere," said World Athletics President Sebastian Coe. "Money stolen from the integrity of our sport will now be returned to programs that protect it." He called it a significant day, but added that it was equally a reminder of how much damage had been done.
The investigation opened in 2016, spurred by whistleblower disclosures and a damning independent report commissioned by the World Anti-Doping Agency. By its conclusion, 38 individuals and four corporate entities across 15 countries had been charged. Former senior officials of the Russian Athletics Federation were among those named, alongside intermediaries operating across multiple jurisdictions and representatives of several sports management firms. Charges included wire fraud, money laundering, obstruction of justice, and conspiracy to defraud anti-doping bodies. Some defendants have already pleaded guilty. Others are still awaiting trial. The DOJ noted the investigation is continuing.
The recovered funds will go into a newly created Clean Sport Development Fund, jointly administered by World Athletics and an independent oversight board. The DOJ said the money would support anti-doping education, athlete welfare, and athletics infrastructure development in lower-income countries — places where governance has historically been thinnest.
The case drew natural comparisons to the 2015 FIFA scandal. In both instances, US prosecutors were able to assert jurisdiction because the financial transactions at the center of the schemes passed through American banks — a legal foothold that proved decisive.
CNN's Washington bureau contributed to this report.

Rome (CNN) Pope Francis will not travel to Lisbon for the closing ceremony of World Youth Day, the Vatican confirmed Monday — the latest in a series of adjustments to the 86-year-old pontiff's public schedule as ongoing knee and hip problems continue to limit his mobility.
The announcement came after weeks of reduced appearances. Rather than attending in person at the Parque Eduardo VII gathering, which is expected to draw hundreds of thousands of young Catholics, Francis will deliver his address and blessing via video link from the Apostolic Palace.
"The Holy Father deeply regrets that he is unable to be present in Lisbon for this historic occasion but looks forward to being with the faithful in spirit and through his message," a Vatican statement said.
The decision was made on medical advice, a senior official told CNN, and was not connected to any new or acute health event. Francis has been seen in a wheelchair at several recent public engagements — a visible sign of the difficulties he has managed since undergoing colon surgery in 2021.
None of that has slowed his pace at the Vatican itself. CNN's review of his public calendar shows more than fourteen formal engagements in the first three weeks of July alone, including audiences with multiple heads of state and a lengthy address to a symposium on environmental theology. He presided over his regular Wednesday general audience last week and met privately with a delegation of Ukrainian Catholic bishops, even as some within the Curia have quietly urged a more cautious schedule.
Earlier this month he drew extended applause from pilgrims in St. Peter's Square after saying that institutional "timidity" in the face of social injustice was "simply not acceptable" — language noticeably sharper than his usual register on the subject.
Last month, Francis dismissed retirement talk outright. "When the Lord wants me to stop, he will let me know," he told a group of Jesuit journalists in a wide-ranging interview — a pointed reference to his predecessor Benedict XVI, who stepped down in 2013.
Still, not making the trip to Lisbon is a notable absence for a pope who had spoken openly about wanting to be there. Long-haul foreign travel has grown increasingly rare. What was once a signature feature of his papacy — stadium masses on multiple continents, exhausting multi-country tours — has gradually given way to shorter domestic engagements and meetings that do not require him to leave Rome.
CNN has launched Vatican Dispatch, a new weekly newsletter covering the Holy See, the papacy, and the global Catholic Church. Sign up here.

(CNN) "There are days when it still doesn't feel real," Dwyane Wade said at a charity event in Miami last weekend, speaking about the friend whose absence he feels most in the moments basketball demands reflection.
One year since David Stern's death, the people whose careers he shaped most directly are still finding their footing. The NBA commissioner who spent three decades turning a domestically struggling league into a global institution died on January 1, 2020, after a sudden brain hemorrhage. He was 77. The anniversary has prompted an outpouring across the league — not the performative kind, but something quieter and more personal.
"He gave so many of us a stage," Wade said. "Not just to play, but to be who we are. To have opinions, to have a voice. That wasn't always the case, and he changed it."
LeBron James, who came of age in the league Stern built and clashed with him more than once, didn't soften the complicated history. "We didn't always see eye to eye, and I think he respected that, and I respected him for it," James said. "He was tough, he was honest, and he cared about this league more than anybody."
'He saw the whole board'
Current commissioner Adam Silver served as Stern's deputy for years before succeeding him in 2014. At a memorial event in New York last week, he said the past year had clarified rather than dimmed his sense of what Stern accomplished — and how much of what the league now takes for granted was built on decisions Stern made long before they seemed obvious.
"David had this ability to see around corners," Silver said. "He saw the global game before anyone else did. He saw the business of basketball becoming something that could rival any entertainment property in the world. And then he went and built it."
Silver admitted he still occasionally reaches for the phone to call Stern when a major decision lands on his desk. "I'll probably never fully stop doing that," he said.
Among players who knew him personally, one theme kept surfacing: Stern treated them as partners, even when the relationship was adversarial. Chris Paul, president of the National Basketball Players Association, put it plainly. "He pushed back. Hard, sometimes. But he also listened. That's rarer than people think in this business."
The NBA marked the anniversary with a moment of silence before New Year's Day games, commemorative jersey patches, and a tribute video aired during halftime of the nationally televised matchup.
For Silver, the day was less about ceremony than about a standard he's still trying to meet. "Every decision I make, I ask myself whether it moves the game forward," he said. "That's what he taught me. That's what he was."

(CNN) The Las Vegas Raiders may be about to do something no NFL team has managed in over a year: fill a stadium.
Nevada officials signaled Thursday they would allow Allegiant Stadium to operate at full capacity starting with the Raiders' preseason home opener against the San Francisco 49ers on August 14, pending final sign-off from the state health department. Raiders President Marc Badain didn't hide his enthusiasm.
"We built one of the most spectacular stadiums in professional sports to give our fans an experience unlike any other," he said in a statement. "To finally be able to welcome all of them through the doors is something we have been working toward since the day this building opened."
The announcement came alongside Nevada Governor Steve Sisolak's decision to lift most remaining capacity restrictions on large indoor venues, citing falling Covid-19 hospitalizations and a faster-than-expected vaccine rollout. About 39% of the state's adult population had been fully vaccinated as of Thursday, according to state health figures.
Not everyone is ready to declare victory. Dr. Fermin Leguen of the Southern Nevada Health District called the trend encouraging while urging caution about fully indoor venues. "We also ask that residents continue to exercise personal responsibility," he said at a Thursday briefing.
Masks will be encouraged but not required once Nevada's indoor mandate expires on June 1. The team said it would maintain enhanced cleaning protocols and boost ventilation throughout the building.
The full-crowd moment carries a particular weight for this franchise. Allegiant Stadium — a $1.9 billion domed facility the Raiders moved from Oakland specifically to inhabit — opened in July 2020, just as sports leagues were shutting fans out entirely. Las Vegas played its entire first season in Nevada in an empty building. No fans, no atmosphere, no payoff for the relocation that was supposed to give the franchise a fresh start.
The NFL is widely expected to allow all 32 teams to play before full crowds when the regular season opens in September, though local regulations could complicate things for some franchises.
Las Vegas Mayor Carolyn Goodman was unambiguous about what a full Allegiant Stadium means. "This city runs on hospitality, on spectacle, on bringing people together," she said. "Having a full house is exactly the signal Las Vegas needs to send to the world right now."
A few voices pushed back. Dr. Brian Labus of the UNLV School of Public Health pointed to the emerging Delta variant as a reason for humility. "We are in a better place than we were six months ago," he said. "That does not mean the pandemic is over."
By Thursday evening, 42,000 of the stadium's 65,000 tickets had already been sold. The remaining inventory, the team said, would go on sale before the end of the week.

A crowdfunding website that presents itself as a defender of open participation has found itself at the center of another debate over online fundraising and political activism.
A review of campaigns on the platform shows that a number of politically contentious figures and groups have used it to collect money for legal bills, travel costs, public events, and other projects. Some of those campaigns have attracted substantial support, bringing in tens or even hundreds of thousands of dollars.
Supporters of the platform argue that access to fundraising tools should not depend on whether a person's views are widely accepted. Many of the campaigns involve activists, commentators, or organizers who say they have been marginalized by larger institutions or removed from other online services.
Critics see the situation differently. Organizations that track extremist activity argue that money is often what allows controversial movements to expand, organize events, and maintain a public presence. In their view, platforms that provide fundraising opportunities cannot completely separate themselves from the causes they host.
Company representatives reject that criticism. They say their decisions are based primarily on legality and compliance with site policies, not on political ideology. If a campaign does not promote unlawful behavior or explicitly target people with hateful content, it is generally allowed to remain online.
The company's approach has not insulated it from outside pressure. Payment providers and financial partners have occasionally restricted transactions linked to certain campaigns, leaving some fundraising pages active but unable to receive donations.
The controversy highlights a larger disagreement that extends well beyond a single website. Technology companies increasingly face demands to decide where the line should be drawn between open access and social responsibility. Some believe platforms should remove controversial causes before they gain momentum. Others worry that giving private companies that power could create a different set of problems.
Meanwhile, the platform continues to expand beyond politics. Alongside the headline-grabbing campaigns are far more ordinary fundraising efforts: families covering medical expenses, local communities supporting disaster victims, and individuals seeking help during difficult times.
As debates over speech, accountability, and online platforms continue, services like this one are likely to remain under close public scrutiny. Whether they are viewed as neutral tools or active participants in political life may depend largely on who is looking.

(CNN) Daytime television is getting another high-profile addition.
NBCUniversal said Tuesday that actress and producer Viola Davis will be launching her own daytime talk show, which is scheduled to air on a group of network-affiliated stations next year.
In a statement announcing the project, Davis reflected on the experiences that have shaped her career and personal life.
"I've been fortunate to live through many different chapters," she said. "Those experiences have taught me about perseverance, purpose, and the value of connecting with other people. I'm excited to bring those conversations to a wider audience."
She said the show will focus on stories that reveal what motivates people and how they navigate life's challenges. Davis also noted that she is eager to hear from guests with a wide range of backgrounds and perspectives.
Davis first drew attention for her work in theater before becoming one of the most celebrated performers in film and television. Her portrayal in the 2016 movie "Fences" earned her an Academy Award, adding to a collection of honors that already included Emmy and Tony recognition.
Read More
The actress has frequently discussed the hardships she faced growing up, including periods of financial struggle. In recent years, she starred in "The Woman King," a historical drama that was well received by critics and moviegoers alike.
The new talk show will run for one hour and is expected to debut in the spring.

(CNN) The race for college football's national title entered its final stage Sunday as the selection committee revealed the four teams that will compete in the College Football Playoff.
Claiming the playoff spots were the Texas Longhorns, Oregon Ducks, Florida State Seminoles and Washington Huskies, each of whom put together standout seasons to earn a chance at the championship.
The semifinals will be played on New Year's Day. Top-seeded Texas is scheduled to meet Washington in the Sugar Bowl in New Orleans, while Oregon and Florida State will face each other in the Rose Bowl in Pasadena. The two winners will move on to the national championship game later in January in Houston.
Florida State arrives in the playoff unbeaten after navigating its schedule without a loss and securing a conference title along the way. The Seminoles consistently found ways to win, combining a disciplined defense with timely offensive performances.
Texas enters as the No. 1 seed after an impressive season that included a conference championship and several notable victories. Head coach Steve Sarkisian has guided the program back into the national spotlight, putting the Longhorns in position to compete for their first title in years.
Oregon earned the second seed behind a powerful offense that ranked among the nation's most productive. The Ducks finished the season strongly and strengthened their case with a commanding performance in their conference championship game.
Washington rounded out the playoff field thanks to a résumé built on quality wins and a dynamic passing attack. The Huskies spent much of the season near the top of the rankings and ultimately did enough to secure the committee's confidence.
The first team left out of the playoff was Penn State, which finished fifth after posting an 11-2 record. Despite missing the top four, the Nittany Lions are expected to be featured in one of the postseason's premier bowl games.
LSU followed closely behind at No. 6. The Tigers remained in the playoff conversation for much of the year, fueled by one of the most explosive offenses in college football.
Now that the bracket has been finalized, the focus shifts from rankings and debate to the games themselves. Four teams remain in contention, and two semifinal matchups will determine who gets the opportunity to compete for the national championship.

Justin Thomas reached another notable landmark in his team-match career on Saturday, but it wasn't something he seemed eager to dwell on.
Playing alongside Patrick Cantlay in the morning foursomes session at the Presidents Cup, Thomas helped secure a 2&1 win over Sungjae Im and Corey Conners. The Americans had trailed earlier in the match before gradually working their way back and taking control over the closing holes.
The result gave Thomas his 18th career victory in Presidents Cup competition and continued a productive week for the partnership. He and Cantlay had already earned a point together the previous day, and their combination once again proved difficult to beat.
Over the years, Thomas has built a reputation as a player who often raises his level in team events. His record in international competition reflects that consistency, and Saturday's win pushed him further up the list of successful American performers.
Still, the achievement was not at the forefront of his mind after the round. When reporters mentioned the milestone, Thomas quickly redirected attention to the larger picture.
"It's something I'll probably appreciate more later," he said. "Right now, the team result is what matters."
He also praised the opposition, noting that the matches had been closer than the scoreboard sometimes suggested. With plenty of golf still remaining, Thomas stressed that there was no room for overconfidence.
"We've put ourselves in a good position, but nothing's finished yet," he said. "Every point is important, and we have to keep showing up and doing our job."
For Thomas, the record books can wait. The immediate goal remains the same: helping his team finish the week on top.

(CNN) A woman's death following complications during pregnancy has reignited a contentious national debate over abortion policy, with critics arguing that restrictive laws may be influencing medical decisions in hospitals.
Public anger grew after details of the case emerged, prompting demonstrations in several cities. Protesters say the legal framework surrounding abortion has created an atmosphere in which doctors may hesitate to act quickly, even when a patient's condition becomes dangerous.
According to relatives and legal representatives, the woman was hospitalized during her second trimester after experiencing serious complications. Earlier examinations had identified major fetal abnormalities, raising concerns about the viability of the pregnancy.
Family members contend that doctors continued to monitor the situation instead of ending the pregnancy immediately. Over time, the woman's health reportedly deteriorated, and she developed a severe infection. Despite treatment efforts, she later died.
The tragedy has become a powerful symbol for abortion-rights campaigners. Many argue that the law's restrictions have had a chilling effect on medical professionals, leaving them uncertain about when intervention is legally protected.
Attorneys representing the family have called for a full inquiry into the circumstances surrounding the woman's care. They want investigators to examine not only the actions of the medical team but also whether the legal environment contributed to the outcome.
Hospital officials expressed sympathy to the family while defending the conduct of staff. They said treatment decisions were made in accordance with applicable regulations and medical guidelines. Health authorities have since opened a review into the case.
As news of the death spread online, a slogan meaning "Never Again" gained traction across social media platforms and appeared on banners at public demonstrations. The phrase quickly became associated with demands for legal reform.
Government officials have disputed claims that abortion laws were responsible. They argue that doctors retain the authority to intervene when a pregnant woman's life or health is threatened and say any mistakes should be assessed through the ongoing investigation.
Backers of the current legislation have echoed that view, warning against drawing political conclusions before all the facts are established. They maintain that the purpose of the law is to protect fetal life while still allowing necessary medical treatment.
The case has nevertheless intensified discussion among doctors, legal scholars and advocacy groups. Some experts believe uncertainty about legal consequences can influence clinical decisions, while others argue the existing rules already provide sufficient exceptions.
Observers have also pointed to similar cases abroad where the deaths of pregnant women became catalysts for broader debates over reproductive rights and healthcare policy.
For many demonstrators, the issue extends beyond a single case. They see it as evidence of deeper problems within the legal system governing reproductive healthcare and are calling on lawmakers to revisit the rules to prevent similar tragedies in the future.

Rory McIlroy finds himself in a familiar position once again: near the top of the leaderboard at a major championship and carrying plenty of momentum into the weekend.
The four-time major winner produced one of the strongest rounds of the day on Friday at the US Open, building a three-shot lead over the field. For much of the afternoon, it looked as though his advantage might be even larger. McIlroy picked up several birdies early and was moving steadily away from his competitors before a couple of mistakes late in the round slowed him down.
Even so, he walked off the course with plenty to be encouraged about. By the time he finished speaking with reporters, he had learned that other contenders had stumbled on the closing stretch, leaving him alone in first place.
"It's good news, obviously," McIlroy said. "But we're only halfway through the tournament. There are still a lot of holes left, and this course can change things pretty quickly."
A victory on Sunday would carry particular significance. Although McIlroy has remained one of golf's biggest names, another major title has eluded him for years despite several close calls.
He suggested that recent improvements have less to do with swing changes and more to do with what is happening between his ears. In recent weeks, he has devoted extra attention to maintaining concentration for long periods and avoiding the lapses that can prove costly in major championships.
"I've worked hard on staying locked in for the whole round," he explained. "When your mind starts drifting, that's usually when mistakes happen."
